We will focus on the range of choices available to filmmakers as they compose and combine images, incorporate sound, and use film as a medium to tell stories, express ideas and present arguments. The films we study range across the history of cinema (1890s-1990s) and include films produced not only in the US but also in Germany, France, the Soviet Union, Japan, Canada, and elsewhere. This course defines cinema broadly and therefore includes documentary, animated, and experimental films as well as feature-length, fictional movies. While our focus will often be on the close analysis of individual films (considering them at the level of individual shots, individual scenes, the use of specific techniques), we will undertake this close analysis in light of the contexts (social, artistic, economic) that shaped specific film practices. Course requirements include attendance at weekly film screenings, exams, short writing assignments and reading quizzes. Note: This course has an attendance policy. Spring 2009 Joy Young Introduction to Performance Studies This course introduces students to the dramaturgical vocabulary that has been deployed across a variety of disciplines to describe, define, and develop our understanding of "performance" as a primary cultural process. We will approach performance studies as both an acknowledged field of study and as an influential anti-discipline that draws its critical and disciplinary methods from social sciences, performing arts, post-structuralism, ethnology, philosophy and aesthetics. Among the topics discussed are the performing arts and popular entertainments, rituals, play and games as well as the performances of everyday life. Video screenings and guest lectures will provide an additional dimension for the course.

title: Medieval British Literature
description: This course will involve readings in medieval English literature, both in translation and in the original Middle English. Its readings will range from the Old English epic poem Beowulf, through such Middle English works as Sir Gawain and the Green Knight, numerous lyric poems, and selections from Chaucer's Canterbury Tales, and including as well works reflecting late medieval religious practices, Julian of Norwich's Revelations, the Cloud of Unknowing, and the Book of Margery Kempe. It will include three papers and a final exam, and there will be a creative option offered for two of the papers.

title: Worlds of Beowulf
description: The recent translation of Beowulf by Nobel laureate Seamus Heaney has reawakened interest in the Anglo-Saxons, yet stereotypes and misinformation persist in popular culture. This seminar will cover the intellectual and physical cultures of the Anglo-Saxons through the texts, archaeology, artifacts, language and art of the sixth to eleventh centuries in Britain to test our assumptions and develop new ways of seeing this ancient society in an interdisciplinary way. You will learn to read the physical culture alongside textual culture, creating a dialogue between them and using the different perspectives of each to test your perceptions of the period and its peoples. Is the term "Anglo-Saxon" useful at all, given the hybridity of the populations of the British Isles? How do we read early burials: do they reveal something about Anglo-Saxon beliefs or more about those of later scholars? How do the secular and spiritual worlds interact and influence each other? Do site excavations and architectural space reveal attitudes towards power, privacy, gender, access? Where were the women in this heroic world, and can we find their voices? We look at such issues as cultural interaction and opposition; the marginal vs. the normative; mythmaking and identity; gender constructions and representations; and the social, religious and political impacts of Christianity. We will read selections from Old English poems, riddles, sermons, histories, saints' lives and legal documents against the physical cultures of sites, sculpture, artifacts and manuscripts, culminating in a month on the most well-known period work, Beowulf, after we have spent the course preparing ourselves to understand its contexts. All texts will be read in translation, but there will also be some original language study so that students can avoid being misled by translations. (Language study will be required to pass, but not graded. This is not a language course.) Varied writing assignments throughout the term will develop a longer final paper at the end of the course.

title: Chaucer
description: Fall 2008 John Hirsh A reading and discussion of Chaucer's great (if unfinished) master-work, the Canterbury Tales, a work of first importance to anyone who reads (or writes) narrative fiction. Students will read the work in original Middle English in which Chaucer wrote it, and discuss it in small and in large groups, focusing upon the work's narrative strategies, intellectual constructions, and artistic accomplishment. They will also write, in a variety of ways, about those aspects which particularly take their interest. Spring 2009 Sarah McNamer In this course we will undertake a critical study of that brilliant masterpiece, The Canterbury Tales. By turns bawdy and lofty, racy and religious, hilarious and deeply moving, this collection of stories will challenge us to develop ways of reading adequate to its double status as relic of the distant past and as a text that anticipates many contemporary understandings of identity, meaning, and truth. We will read the Tales in the original Middle English, which is really quite easy; no prior exposure to the language is necessary. Requirements: 3 short papers (3 pages each), 1 longer paper (10 pages), frequent quizzes, and active participation in class discussion.

title: Arthurian Legends
description: In this course, we will explore the legends of King Arthur, the once and future king who has captured the imagination of authors and visionaries since the early Middle Ages. This interdisciplinary course offers an introduction to medieval history, tradition, and literature as well as an analysis of the meanings and uses of Arthurian myths from the sixth through the sixteenth centuries. One key question in our study of the Arthurian legends will be why these tales have held the interest of successive generations. Is it the enduring love story of Lancelot and Guinevere, with its conflict of loyalties? The heroic characteristics and tragic end of King Arthur's life? Or perhaps the appeal of the ideal Round Table society and its knights' enduring quest for the Holy Grail? These elements survive in different versions of the Arthurian legends, and they will form the core of our study this semester.

title: Shakespeare
description: Fall 2008 The course will look closely at five of Shakespeareâs best known plays, including King Lear and a play that will be in production in Washington during the semester. The course will focus largely (but not exclusively) on a single significant question: how might these plays be brought to life by actors on a stage in Shakespeareâs time and how might they continue to be brought to life by actors in our own time. In seeking an answer to that question the course will hope also to discover what the plays of Shakespeare, on the page and on the stage, might say to us at the beginning of the twenty-first century. Students will be required to attend a performance of a play by Shakespeare at a theatre in Washington. The course carries four credits in order to provide an opportunity for students to take part in several acting exercises during the semester. Spring 2009 The course will look closely at five of Shakespeareâs best known plays, including The Winterâs Tale and King Lear. It will focus largely (but not exclusively) on a single significant question: how might these plays be brought to life by actors on a stage in Shakespeareâs time and how might they continue to be brought to life by actors in our own time. Through the process of seeking an answer to that question the course will hope also to discover what the plays of Shakespeare, on the page and on the stage, might say to us now, some five hundred years after they were written. Students will be asked to take part in a number of acting exercises throughout the semester to enhance their understanding of how a script is brought to life on the stage (the course carries four credits in order to provide them an opportunity to do so) and also to attend a production of The Winterâs Tale at The Folger Theatre in Washington on either on either Tuesday, March 3 or Wednesday, March 4. (A lab fee for the course will cover the cost of the ticket).

title: Shakespeare's Problem Plays
description: Three Shakespeare plays have been called âproblemsâ for their generic indeterminacy: is âTroilus and Cressidaâ a comedy or a tragedy? Can we content ourselves that the ending of âMeasure for Measureâ is comic? Are we satisfied with the resolution of âAllâs Well that Ends Wellâ? But form is not the only problematic issue in Shakespearean drama, and the term has also been applied to âHamletâ and now, because of their fraught gender, religious, and racial politics, âThe Taming of the Shrew,â âThe Merchant of Venice,â and âOthello.â To look at the social work that Renaissance plays could perform in their own time, we will read one non-Shakespearean play that has been called a âradicalâ tragedy for its provocative, progressive address of cultural conflicts and anxieties. Then we will ask how Shakespeareâs social engagement measures up. What complicities does he require of us? Where do theory and historicism take us in negotiating our moral responses and aesthetic appreciation? Course members will be expected to attend a live production of a Shakespeare play (a lab fee is charged to cover the cost of the ticket).

title: Shakespeare Replayed
description: Fall 2008 As a cultural icon who often embodies âEnglishnessâ to outsiders, Shakespeare has been both an idol and critical target for contemporary authors. This course pairs canonical Shakespearean plays with provocative modern reworkings, using comparative analysis to examine changing notions of gender, class, race, empire, power, and history. There will be a significant theoretical component in the form of secondary readings and some visual analysis. By exploring how contemporary playwrights restage (or upstage) Shakespeareâs master narratives, we will open up larger issues of artistic appropriation and translation: can a transgressive viewpoint be adequately housed within a traditional literary structure? In post-colonial terms, can the key to the masterâs house be found within the masterâs architectonics? We will consider how well-known texts such as Hamlet, Othello, and The Tempest play out against upstarts like Suleyman al-Hassanâs The Al-Hamlet Summit, Tom Stoppardâs Rosencrantz and Guildenstern are Dead, Ann-Marie MacDonaldâs Goodnight Desdemona (Good Morning Juliet), and AimÃ© CÃ©saireâs A Tempest. Critical and creative writing assignments required.

title: Seminar: Milton
description: Fall 2008 By starting with the early literary productions in poetry and prose and concluding with /Samson Agonistes/, written in old age, when Milton, like his champion, was blind and disillusioned, we will give attention to the development of his literary powers. But we will spend most of our time on /Paradise Lost/. With all due reverence for Chaucer's /Canterbury Tales /and Shakespeare's /Hamlet/ and /King Lear/, Milton's epic is the greatest single work ever written in English. We will try to understand it mostly on our own but also with help from a few of the best contemporary essays. In addition to a longer paper due at the end of the semester, students will turn in shorter papers every two weeks. Since the seminar will be split into four parts, and the assignments staggered, one quarter of the class will be turning in an assignment every time we meet. Papers should concentrate on interpretive problems or questions raised by the work to be discussed in class that day. Please note also that seminar attendance and discussion are to be considered a formal part of this course's requirements.

title: Literature of the Atlantic Empire
description: Literature of the Atlantic Empire: From Colonies to Republic In this course, we will examine some British, Native American, Anglo-American, African-British, and American literature that grew out of the collision of cultures in the Americas in the late seventeenth and eighteenth centuries, particularly, though not exclusively, in the Chesapeake and the Caribbean. In these fictional and non-fictional narratives and poems, we will be interested in several related concerns: the ideas of civilization and savagery; the dynamics of slavery and freedom (historically, economically, and politically, and also as figures for psychological and spiritual processes); and the articulation of selfhood in a colonial context. We will pay attention to whether there are significant and growing differences between Old and New World identities, and we will chart some of the anxieties of dealing with a new American identity in the literature of the early Republic. We will study the following authors and works: Behn, Oroonoko; Defoe, Robinson Crusoe; the captivity narrative of Mary Rowlandson; narratives of indentured servitude by Moraley and Ashbridge; Sarah Knightâs Journal; Cookeâs Sot-Weed Factor; Byrdâs Secret History of the Dividing Line; Olaudah Equianoâs Interesting Narrative; Franklinâs Autobiography; Fosterâs The Coquette; Irvingâs âLegend of Sleepy Hollowâ; Brownâs Wieland; and Cooperâs The Prairie.

title: American Top Ten
description: This course will read in close detail some of the âclassicsâ â prose, poetry, essay -- of the American literary tradition (focused, with some exceptions, on the 19th century). We will consider these texts in their political and social histories; we will examine the aesthetic judgments made about them. This class will address some basic questions about canon-formation: How does one define âclassicâ? What makes a text great? What is the relation between a textâs assumed greatness and the culture in which it exists? Who gets to make these decisions that makes something âLiteratureâ? Once made can these judgments ever be reconsidered? More generally, why should these (or any) texts be considered especially âAmericanâ and why should Americans read them? Texts: Franklin, âThe Way to Wealth,â âNotes to those thinking to emigrate to Americaâ Crevecour; âWhat is an American?â (Letter III); Nathaniel Hawthorne, The Scarlet Letter; Edwards, âSinners in the Hands of an Angry Godâ; Edgar Allan Poe, âPit and the Pendulumâ; Frederick Douglass, My Life and Bondage, âRochester Independence Speech,â Booker T. Washington, âUp from Slavery,â Harriet Beecher Stowe, Uncle Tomâs Cabin; Abraham Lincoln, âGettysburg Address,â âEmancipation Proclamation,â Thomas Jefferson et al, âDeclaration of Independence,â Thoreau, âCivil Disobedienceâ; Mark Twain, Huckleberry Finn; Fitzgerald, The Great Gatsby, John Steinbeck, The Grapes of Wrath, [film]; MGM Wizard of Oz, [film] Toni Morrison, The Bluest Eye; assorted critical essays; Whitman / Dickinson / Frost poems

title: Nineteenth-Century British Womenâs Literature
description: While the women writers of the long nineteenth century were largely engaged with many of the same concerns as their male contemporaries (science, colonialism, national identity, industrialism), they often also took on questions specific to the conditions of womenâs writing, for example, the problems of creating a female public persona, the economic circumstances of womenâs lives, the relationship of sex to gendered identity, the benefits and disadvantages of marriage, the relationship of writing to child-bearing and child-rearing. This course examines several works of fiction, verse, and prose by women, reading them in light of both sets of nineteenth-century concerns. At the same time, we will be engaged in the question of how the process of literary canonization works and how womenâs lives and writings enter into that process. Authors will include Wollstonecraft, Austen, Shelley, Charlotte BrontÃ«, Rossetti, Barrett Browning, Eliot, and Sarah Grand, as well as readings from twentieth-century feminist critics of nineteenth-century literature.

title: Seminar: Staging Anti-Slavery
description: Fall 2008 What are the rewards and dangers of activism? What is the place of empathy in activist performances, both in print and in person? Can historical performances illuminate the challenges facing us as we try to work for justice? Through an investigation of the activist strategies used by nineteenth-century black and white female abolitionists, weâll address these questions. Weâll pay particular attention to those women who staged their resistance to slavery and racism from 1825 through 1865. How did these women perform their abolitionist activism, in print and onstage, and what can we learn from their practices? Weâll study anti-slavery essays, speeches, letters, poems, short stories, slave narratives, tell-all books, and autobiographical novels. Weâll trace the legacies of these women in our world, one which still sustains global human trafficking and fosters what many call the ânew abolitionism.â Our readings will include works by Lydia Maria Child, Angelina and Sarah GrimkÃ©, Frances Ellen Watkins Harper, and a host of lesser-known anti-slavery lecturers and essayists. Students in Performance Studies, American Studies, African American Studies, Womenâs Studies, Literary History, and Justice & Peace Studies may be especially interested in the material of this course.

title: Dickens and the Art of Fiction
description: A close reading of three major novels by Charles Dickens: Little Dorrit (1855 â 1857), A Tale of Two Cities (1859), and Great Expectations (1860 â 1861) in which we will consider the writer in the context of his era and the practice of fiction in the mid-nineteenth century. Discussion will dominate class time with students reporting on their analytical, inventive, and library research projects. There will not be any exams as such, but there will be short, factual quizzes on the dayâs reading which will begin each class session.

title: Wordsworth and Coleridge
description: This course will explore one of the greatest partnerships in English literary history. It was to produce the most accomplished work of either of the two poets, including âThe Ruined Cottageâ, âThe Pedlarâ, âThe Ancient Marinerâ, âChristabelâ and âKubla Khanâ, as well as a book that was to prove nothing less than revolutionary in its impact â Lyrical Ballads. The course will also explore some of the ways in which this remarkable collaboration shaped Wordsworthâs greatest poetry, including âThe Preludeâ and The Excursion. Students will be expected to adopt a scholarly approach, examining the letters, notebooks, journals and other manuscript materials of these writers, and overall the course will emphasize the importance of a sensitive, close reading of their work. Those who participate will be expected to deliver at least one class presentation relating to a literary work, the subject to be decided in discussion with Professor Wu. Throughout, the emphasis will be on close analysis of the text and class discussion in which all students will be expected to participate. Each week students will be expected to complete writing assignments which will contribute to their overall assessment, as will their performance in class.

title: Keats and Shelley
description: This course will examine the work of two major second-generation British Romantic poets, who were to produce some of the greatest poetry in the language, and to influence British and European culture throughout the remainder of the nineteenth century. We will explore Keatsâs sonnets, his romance narratives (âIsabellaâ, âLamiaâ, âThe Eve of St Agnesâ), Hyperion, the Odes, and his correspondence; Shelleyâs âAlastorâ, âAdonaisâ, Prometheus Unbound and prose writings. Students will be expected to adopt a scholarly approach, examining the manuscript materials of these writers, and overall the course will emphasize the importance of a sensitive, close reading of their work. Those who participate will be expected to deliver at least one class presentation relating to a literary work, the subject to be decided in discussion with Professor Wu. Throughout the emphasis will be on close analysis of the text and class discussion in which all students will be expected to participate. Each week students will be expected to complete writing assignments which will contribute to their overall assessment, as will their performance in class.

title: 19C Theatre and Performance
description: Fall 2008 19TH-CENTURY PERFORMANCE This course analyzes performance in relation to the political processes and cultural production of 19th-century America - an era of rapid urbanization and violent civil war. We begin with the emergence of two defining forms of the period: (1) melodrama, a dramatic mode preoccupied with the recognition of individual virtue in a deceptive world, and (2)black-face minstrelsy, the staging of a fictitious âbroad-casted blacknessâ by ethnically mingled people negotiating the terms of identity. Adopting a broader yet more nuanced understanding of âperformanceâ, we shall then turn to other cultural performances including religious revivals, âcelebrityâ trials, the âliving spectaclesâ of P. T. Barnumâs American Museum, and the mourning rituals occasioned by the mass destruction of the Civil War. The reading comprise an interdisciplinary mix of theatre, history, cultural studies, journalism, law, and performance theory.

title: 19th-Century U. S.  Literatures: Class, Poverty, and the American Dream
description: As several recent critics have noted, class remains an under-studied cultural category in nineteenth-century U. S. literature. This course will examine literary and cultural representations of social class, and will explore how class as literary construction is inseparable from discourses of gender, race, ethnicity, sexuality, nation, and empire. Overall in the course, we will explore the American Dream of unencumbered class mobility as a particular formation of what critic Lauren Berlant terms "national fantasy," a utopian national ideal at once deemed universally accessible and inscribed by a variety of social differences. We will examine the way 19th-C literary texts both reproduce, and expose the limits of and/or contradictions within, this governing ideology of âAmericannessâ: we will consider the ways in which these texts measure the distance between utopian ideal and historical ârealityâ and register what Richard Sennett and Johnathan Cobb term the âhidden injuries of classââthe invisible psychic and social costs of subscription to this national ideal. Course readings include literary texts by George Foster, Frank Webb, Frederick Douglass, Thomas Skidmore, Sarah Bagley and other New England millworkers who contributed to the Lowell Offering and the Voice of Industry, Nathaniel Hawthorne, Herman Melville, Horatio Alger, Stephen Crane, Edith Wharton, and Anzia Yezierska. We will also read selected theoretical, critical, and historical articles addressing various aspects of class formation and the construction of class subjectivities in the nineteenth-century U. S. We will end the course by considering two contemporary films, which foreground how (to quote the title of the influential series of articles (2005) in the New York Times) âclass mattersâ in contemporary U. S. culture, and which situate U. S. class relations and identities in a global context.

title: -   Wicked: Moralityâs Complex 19th C Literary Portraits
description: Fall 2008 This course examines, via literature and philosophical excerpts, the 19th centuryâs ârequirementsâ to be good in order to probe the complexity of the wicked. Is wicked the opposite of good? Is wicked synonymous with evil? Who determines/identifies another as wicked? How do different writers explain how someone âbecomesâ wicked? Does the 19th century believe that one might be born wicked and do they distinguish natural wickedness from learned ill behavior? With what other factions of human nature and society does this century align the wicked: gender, age, class, educational background, genetic background, etc? What causes the reader to sympathize with the wicked rather than spurning them? How do the wicked prompt our fear and anger? What happens to the wicked? Also to be considered is how discovering and pursuing the wicked shapes the narrative â are their discernable common characterizations, patterns in plot, or implied purposes in these novels? Or do the wicked provoke as many distinct styles, moods, and voices as stories focused on individuals who are weak or struggling unjustly? These quests and others will direct our reading of Henry Jamesâs The Portrait of a Lady, George Eliotâs Adam Bede, Thomas Hardyâs Tess of the DâUrbervilles, Charles Dickensâ Little Dorrit, and Fyodor Dostoevskyâs Crime and Punishment. Students will produce several short response papers, a major class presentation, and a final research paper.

title: American Detective Fiction
description: Fall 2008 As the twentieth century began, a new literary form emerged, a kind of urban poetry constructed out of images of gals, guts, guns, and gumshoes with a drinking problem and a fondness for chivalric myth. This course explores the evolution of American hard-boiled detective fiction and the larger mysteries lurking in, (to use Raymond Chandlerâs immortal phrase) âa world gone wrong.â Beginning with the âurban shockâ tales of Edgar Allan Poe and proceeding into the Golden Age British mystery tradition, this course goes on to discuss pulp fiction detective stories of the teens and twenties. Hard boiled detective fiction begins in earnest with the short stories of Dashiell Hammett and his classic novel, The Maltese Falcon. Raymond Chandler and Ross Macdonald--the other two members of The Holy Trinity of Crime Fiction--are represented by The Big Sleep and Black Money, respectively. Other hard-boiled writers whose work will be covered include: James M. Cain, Mickey Spillane, Robert B. Parker, Sara Paretsky, and Richard Stevenson. In addition, we will discuss highlights of the complementary tradition of film noir, including the classic films: âDouble Indemnity,â âThe Maltese Falcon,â âMildred Pierce,â âThe Big Heatâ and âA Touch of Evil.â

title: New York Stories
description: Spring 2009 New York City is, arguably the cultural capital of twentieth- (and, so far), twenty-first century America - the center of literature, music, art, architecture, and fashion as well as the focal point for political debates on the viability of cities, immigration, and the meaning of America itself. This course proposes to study autobiographical, fictional, non-fictional, poetic and cinematic accounts of twentieth-century life in New York City in order to understand the dreams and nightmares embodied by the city in the popular imagination. Beginning with the first decades of the twentieth century, we will investigate the decline of the "old guard" aristocratic society in the early 1900s and the nature of the immigrant experience in New York. The course will then focus on the Jazz Age when New York artists, writers, and architects shaped a new ethos of modernity that would define "The American Century." Attention will also be devoted to the various "counter-cultural" movements (e.g. the rise of the American Communist Part, the Beat Movement) that erupted in New York City during the early and middle decades of the last century. The course will conclude with contemporary dystopian and stubbonly utopian predictions about the future of New York City and urban life in America.

title: Appalachian Literature
description: Fall 2008 This course interrogates what it means to be "Appalachian," "hillbilly," or "from the mountains." In this course we will investigate the literature, the culture, and stereotypes of the Appalachian region. We will focus on the mountains as they figure physically and metaphorically in the language and lives of those who have hunted in, lived in, stayed put in, extracted from, left, returned to, or been haunted by the remoteness and the richness of the region. In addition to reading literature by native Appalachians we will study economic and cultural analyses of the region. We will celebrate the culture in an Appalachian feast in which we enjoy foods, music, dance, and storytelling of the region. Students will write three short papers, do a music presentation, and complete a hypertext project illustrating their understanding of the region in literary, historical, geographical, cultural, and economic contexts. Close reading, class discussion, BlackBoard postings, musical appreciation, and participation in group projects are crucial components of the course. Texts include: River of Earth, by James Still; Jack Wellerâs Yesterdayâs People; Storming Heaven by Denise Giardina; Oral History by Lee Smith; the Stories of Breece DâJ Pancake; Salvation on Sand Mountain by Dennis Covington; Excerpts from Women Writing in Appalachia edited by Ballard and Hudson, and assigned economic, historical and sociological readings by Richard Couto, Dwight Billings & Elizabeth Blee, Richard D. Blake, Mary Beth Pudup, Beth Bingham, etc.

title: American Expatriate Writers
description: Fall 2008 This course will explore the fiction and poetry of some of the principal American Expatriate writers, a tradition which has emerged as a rich vein in American Literature. In particular, we will investigate how their perception of America, coupled with their interaction with modern art and with each other, stimulated an experiment in realignment of traditional American values with corresponding innovative literary techniques. Short stories by Hemingway, Fitzgerald and Gertrude Stein; novels by Hemingway, Fitzgerald and Virginia Woolf; poetry by Ezra Pound, Hilda Doolittle, e.e. cummings and T. S. Eliot. (Virginia Woolf was not American, but a significant feminist contemporary.) Students in this four credit course will also research, create, and offer a power point presentationâeither individually or in a groupâon selected issues to further enrich our study of the period. Some of these presentations will be offered in an occasional extra class period of one hour.

title: Harlem and the Renaissance
description: In 1925, Alain Locke described the emergence of a generation of black artists and intellectuals who would represent the marginalized âfolkâ in the United States and who would serve as a bridge into the possibility of equitable American citizenship. This âNew Negroâ was characterized as a cultural vanguard responsible for the leadership not only of the African American community but also of oppressed people of color across the planet. In this course we will approach the Harlem Renaissance as a nexus of black cosmopolitanism beginning with an analysis of some of its central literary texts and crossing media to examine some of the visual and popular work of the creative period. While unpacking the prose and poetry of Langston Hughes, Nella Larsen, Claude McKay, and other integral figures, we will trace the conceptualization of Harlem itself as a virtual ârace capital,â and we will deal with several more contemporary representations of the aesthetic movement, its political ramifications, and its primary operative space. Considering recent critical disputes about the relative success or failure of this âHarlem Renaissance,â we will explore the limitations of this nomenclature. Through a series of readings, discussions, and essay assignments we will address the recurring themes of the movement with a particular focus on transnational mobility and transcultural exchange. We will investigate the location of black cultural production as it is simultaneously equated with and placed in opposition to conventional notions of Americannessâ-a context in which the New Negro is realized as both producer and product, commodifier and commodified body.

title: Asian American Literature
description: This course introduces students to Asian American fiction, autobiography, poetry, film, drama, the graphic novel, and other forms of audio-visual and print media. Beginning with an investigation into the seminal literary works by Frank Chin and Maxine Hong Kingston, we will discuss the broader social contexts in which Asian American literature had emerged in the 1970s as a direct response to racial stereotyping in U.S. popular culture. We will approach the issue of Asian American cultural authenticity in light of the âculture warsâ and the âgender wars,â and read works of social document fiction by Carlos Bulosan and Milton Murayama. Other readings may include literary texts by Lois-Ann Yamanaka, John Yau, Thien-Bao Thuc Phi, Jessica Hagedorn, Jhumpa Lahiri, Don Lee, Gish Jen, and Kaiji Kawaguchi. Requirements: two short papers (4-6 pages), a class presentation, take-home midterm and final exam.

title: Theorizing Afrofuturism
description: What happens when black bodies are projected and propelled across time, across space, and across the so-called digital divide? How are contemporary boundaries of margin and center problematized by the appropriation of advancing technologies? In this course we will discuss what is conventionally and conveniently called âscience fiction,â and we will explore the relative limitations of this nomenclature as black writers, artists, and intellectuals conceptualize a radical politics of representation through unconventional approaches to genre and form. Considering that agency and subjectivity are inevitably mediated through a shifting conjunction of race, class, gender, sexuality, and nationality, it is important that we take an accurate measure of the extent to which what we understand to be reality is informed by ruptures in and modifications of this media. During this term we will discuss a range of speculative text, including but not limited to novels by George Schuyler, Octavia Butler, Samuel Delany, and Nalo Hopkinson; films by Sun Ra and Melvin Van Peebles; and music by Prince, Norman Connors, and MF DOOM. We will think through the ways in which blackness is constructed and reconfigured as these cultural workers theorize new modes of critical resistance. We will investigate the ways in which alternative histories and presents are imagined through the production of various futures.

title: Twentieth-Century African Literature and the Poetics of Urban Space: Cities, Cultures, Continents
description: This course will offer a survey of 20th-Century African literature and culture though the lens of urbanization. Through our readings of a range of genres, including fiction, film, photography, poetry, and drama, we will consider how the city centralizes the conflicts of globalization, cultural nationalism, education, gender and sexuality in the texts. How and why do so many different forms focus on the city in various African contexts? How does the representation of metropolitan space engage ongoing questions in contemporary studies of African literature, including the relationships between the rural and the urban, modernity and âtradition,â history and memory, as well as nation and diaspora. Authors will include Chinua Achebe, Zoe Wicomb, Mariama Ba, Ama Ata Aidoo, and Ousmane Sembene, among others, including the 2008 Caine Prize Winner. Course requirements will include response and critical analysis papers, group presentations, a final project, and significant in-class participation, as well as several outside events and screenings.

title: African American Poetry
description: In this class, weâll bear witness to the âfurious floweringâ of African American poetry and poetic cultures at critical junctures over the course of the last century. Weâll delve into the theory and practice of the âNew Negroâ Movement (also known as the Harlem Renaissance) of the 1920s and 30s, the Black Arts movement of the 1960s, and the contemporary spoken word scene, devoting special attention to the journals, salons, small presses, clubs, and record labels that have and continue to nurture them. Weâll encounter a wide range of poets, from Paul Laurence Dunbar and Gwendolyn Bennett to Elizabeth Alexander, to Saul Williams, to DJ Spooky, in addition to extended study of the long and varied careers of Langston Hughes, Gwendolyn Brooks, and Amiri Baraka.

title: Jewish American Literature
description: This course explores the writing of contemporary Jewish American writers with special attention to both the contents and literary form of their work. In terms of content, we explore these authors' depiction of the Jewish-American community, its internal tensions (especially as regards gender), its interaction with the gentile world, and its relation with the state of Israel. As for form, the class scrutinizes the literary style of the writers in question in hopes of determining if something such as "Jewish-American literary idiom" really exists.

title: Seminar: Ulysses and Its Contexts
description: Fall 2008 This seminar will focus on what many consider to be one of the greatest novels of the 20th century, James Joyce's Ulysses. We will explore Ulysses in detail through close, careful reading of the novel and its past, contemporary, and current critical contexts. In addition to thinking about the relationship between Ulysses and Joyce's other works, we will read a number of historical influences on Joyce, including The Odyssey, Hamlet, selections from Danteâs Divine Comedy, the writings of Ignatius of Loyola, Giambattista Vico, Saint Augustine, and others, as well as some of Joyceâs contemporaries, including T.S. Eliot, Virginia Woolf, and Ezra Pound, who commented on the novel, both as it was being written and after its publication. Finally, we will read selections of critical commentaries from the extensive criticism that has been written about Ulysses since its publication. In addition to the reading for the course, the work of the seminar will include in class presentations and discussion, an ongoing reading journal in the form of a blog, writing annotations to an online version of Ulysses, and formal papers. Prerequisite: All students must have read James Joyce's Dubliners prior to the beginning of class. It is highly recommended that you also read Joyce's A Portrait of the Artist as a Young Man before the first day of class as well.

title: Orwell and the Politics of Style
description: The course examines the writing of George Orwell, among the most widely read and visible figures of twentieth-century literature. In our reading of works such as The Road to Wigan Pier, Homage to Catalonia, Burmese Days, Coming Up for Air, Animal Farm, Nineteen Eighty-Four, as well as his essays, we shall consider the interrelated subjects of Orwellâs representation of Englishness, his accounts of the role of the writer, and the convergent circumstances under which he was writing, such as the Spanish Civil War, the rise of fascism, the emergence of Stalinism, and the resistance to British colonial rule. In particular, we shall question and explore the relationship between his epistemological claims and the style of Orwellâs literary prose, his essays, and his reportage.

title: Faulkner
description: Fall 2008 This course will investigate one of the greatest American literary achievements of the modern era. We will begin with Go Down, Moses, a collection of interrelated short stories as an entree into the world of Faulkner. Subsequently, we will read and discuss, in chronological order, the major works of Faulknerâs most prolific years: The Sound and the Fury; Sanctuary; As I Lay Dying; Light in August; and Absalom, Absalom. We will discuss these works in terms of predominant themes, psychological and sociological, and Faulknerâs corresponding experimentation in a rich variety of literary techniques. We will also view and discuss The Reivers in movie form. Requirements: a mid-term examination, two 5 page papers and one 10-12 page final paper (or a journal).

title: Eliot's Waste Land
description: We will begin the semester by reading several of Eliot's earlier poems to investigate both his experimentation with poetic technique as well as his thematic concerns. The course will concentrate, however, on The Waste Land. Properly to understand Eliot's opus, however, requires reading its principal allusions: Scripture (Old and New), Dante, Virgil, St. Augustine, Tarot cards, fertility gods and rituals, Grail Knight romances, Shakespeare, Thomas Kyd, Baudelaire, and the Baghavad Gita--virtually a liberal arts curriculum in itself. The final re-reading of The Waste Land, however, with allusions understood and experienced, should represent an extraordinary experience of poetic technique and literature, both East and West.

title: Beckett, Borges, and Nabokov
description: Spring 2009 In his seminal 1967 article âThe Literature of Exhaustion,â John Barth identifies Samuel Beckett, Jorge Luis Borges, and Vladimir Nabokov as the standard bearers of the new literary tradition. For Barth, Beckett, Borges, and Nabokov are artists who are technically up to date and yet able to speak to the human condition as few others. This course will examine the literary works of these three authors and their respective roles in ushering in the literary tradition we now call postmodernism. We will explore the literary and cultural contexts of their writing, a selection of their major works, and the narrative and dramatic techniques of each. Readings will include Three Novels: Malloy, Malone Dies, and The Unnamable, Waiting for Godot, The Garden of Forking Paths, The Aleph, Lolita, Pale Fire, and Ada, or Ardor.

title: The Raw and the Cooked: Conflict in Contemporary American Poetry
description: The Raw and the Cooked: Conflict in Contemporary American Poetry In the early 1960s, two anthologies helped harden the world of poetry into a scene of conflict. Writers of well-wrought, formally traditional poetry like Robert Lowell, Richard Wilbur, Anthony Hecht, and the young Adrienne Rich squared off against such wild bards of spontaneity as Allen Ginsberg, LeRoi Jones, and Lawrence Ferlinghetti, and Diane Di Prima. The clash mirrored the country's emerging cultural and political divides, and helped shape the way poetry has been read and taught ever since. In this course we'll use "conflict" as a means of mapping the vast terrain of American poetry from 1950 to the present, exploring social struggles as well as aesthetic differences in a wide range of poets, including those currently writing for the page, the stage, and producing work in electronic media.

title: British Theater Since 1950
description: The production in 1956 of John Osborne's Look Back in Anger at the Royal Court Theatre in London is said to mark a turning point in the history of British theatre in the twentieth century. The plays that followed Look Back in Anger on the British stage were different in theme and usually in form from those that had been written and produced in the first half of the twentieth century, and they reflected not simply a new kind of theatre in Britain, but a new way of seeing the world outside it. The course will consider some memorable plays by important British playwrights of the last fifty to sixty years, giving attention to theme, form, and possibilities for performance. It will define British loosely, including not only (among others) the English playwrights John Osborne, Harold Pinter, Tom Stoppard, and Caryl Churchill, but the Irish playwrights Samuel Beckett, Brian Friel, and Conor McPherson, and the South African playwright Athol Fugard. The course hopes to come to some understanding of the distinct focus and idiom of the British theatre over the last 50 years and to discover how it might reflect the values and beliefs of the world in which the plays were written and performed. Students will be asked to take part in a number of acting exercises throughout the semester to enhance their appreciation of the theatrical power of these plays (the course carries four credits in order to provide them an opportunity to do so) and also to attend a production of Conor McPhersonâs The Seafarer at the Studio Theatre in Washington on either Tuesday, February 17 or Wednesday, February 18. (A lab fee for the course will cover the cost of the ticket).

title: Avant-Garde Film
description: From its inception, film has attracted generations of artists who viewed this technology as a medium of personal and political expression. Often working within frameworks that explicitly rejected character-driven storytelling and the industrial modes of production that made narrative film so dominant, avant-garde filmmakers produced works that provide multiple answers to the question "what is cinema?" This course examines experimental filmmaking from international and historical perspectives tracing links between avant-garde film and simultaneous movements in the other arts (constructivism, expressionism, dada, surrealism, pop art, the situationists, and postmodernism). While this course assumes no previous experience in film studies, students must be prepared to welcome challenges to their conventional experience of narrative filmmaking. Attendance at weekly evening screenings is mandatory. This course has an attendance policy.

title: The Hollywood Horror Film
description: Fall 2008 This course will explore the evolution of one of the longest-running and most resilient genres: horror. Tracing a history of the horror film from the 1930s to today, we will examine how the genre operates in the commercial and cultural realms of American society. We will also explore the notion of genre, paying close attention to formal conventions, stylistic expression, and historical conditions. The class will engage with a range of critical approaches, from psychoanalytic theory to ideological critique, in order to discover why American audiences seem to take so much pleasure in unpleasantness and fear. Attendance at weekly film screenings is required.

title: History and Memory
description: In this course we will explore the seemingly paradoxical proposition that history must be turned into fiction before it can be considered true. We will consider the ways in which modern and contemporary American writers have approached the problem of representing both personal and communal histories, especially disruptive histories, in various kinds of texts. We will consider these and similar questions: Why is it necessary to represent history, and why is that representation so problematic? What does it mean to say that history has to be authored? What is the relationship between historical perspective and narrative form? How useful is the concept of cultural memory in approaching literary texts? Texts will include William Faulknerâs Absalom, Absalom!, Tim OâBrienâs The Things They Carried, Art Spiegelmanâs Maus, and W.G. Sebaldâs The Emigrants. Requirements include a substantial research project as well as several shorter critical essays.

title: Politics and Theatre
description: This course will explore a variety of theatrical and performative phenomena of the 20th- and 21st-centuries. We will examine the transformative potential of theatre, based on its social dimensions, its ties to ritual, and the necessary bodily co-presence of performers and spectators, as well as the yearning for communal experience in a world seen as increasingly fragmented by the traumas of global conflict and the continual social, cultural, and political upheavals, displacements, and violence. We begin the course examining several theories about performance and politics (Brecht, Boal, Foucault, Ngugi wa Thiong'o among others) and then focus on issues of agency, space, event, and audience. Video screenings and guest lectures will provide an additional dimension for the course.

title: Radical Media
description: One commentator defines âradical mediaâ as those that âpose challenges to existing power structures, empower diverse communities and classes and enable communities of interest to speak to each other.â Throughout the semester we will explore aspects of the historical and contemporary use of various forms of mass media by a variety of radical leftist traditions. Course content will highlight both the theories and alternative practices of different radical traditions during the twentieth century as they mobilized media forms to attack racism, capitalism, patriarchy, imperialism and other social and economic ills. Todayâs indymedia centers, culture jamming, micro-power radio, and âelectronic civil disobedienceâ update and extend the ideas and labor of earlier media activists in the US and abroad. Our course begins to explore this rich history of media activism. Course requirements include attendance at weekly film screenings. This course has an attendance policy.

title: Queer Film
description: Fall 2008 This course provides an overview of contemporary queer cinema in the US and Canada. We will examine the emergence of queer cinema, exploring its relation to the American film industry as well as the social conditions of queer life in the late 20th century US and interrogating its aesthetic and political values and goals. We will also closely consider what âcountsâ as a queer film, including issues of authorship and audience, along with the question of what these films might or should represent in relation to past and present LGBTQ cultures and communities and to mainstream cinema. No prior experience in either queer studies or film studies is required. Course requirements include regular and active participation in class discussions, several short response papers and longer essays, and an independent research project and presentation. Attendance at weekly film screenings is mandatory.

title: Postcolonial Film
description: Postcolonial Film: Sammie and Rosie Go Global This course will think about the cinematic production of postcoloniality, both historically and geographically, produced in response and in relation to various global diasporas in the twentieth and twenty-first centuries. Films will imagine exchanges from England to the Caribbean, form France to Northern Africa, and across various other transnational crossings. in particular, we will think through the centrality of gender and sexuality in imagining postcolonial identity, particularly in queer cinema. Our work will include weekly extra-class film screenings, postcolonial and film theory and select literature, response papers, group presentations, a midterm paper and a significant final project. Films will include My Beautiful Laundrette, Mississippi Masala, Assemblages, East is East, Sammie and Rosie Get Laid, The Battle of Algiers, La Noire de . . ., and others.

title: Class Fictions in Contemporary US
description: Fall 2008 This interdisciplinary course examines U.S. literary and popular cultural representations of âlowâ classes/cultures (working class/poor/âunderclassâ) from the 1990s to the present. With the aid of recent theory and cultural criticism, we will focus on ways in which categories such as race, gender, and sexuality help to construct our contemporary notions of what âclassâ means in America. We will be studying a wide variety of texts, forms, and genresâfiction, autobiography, film, musicâand taking up a broad spectrum of issues, including theories of working class identity, social/cultural resistance, and the notion of ârepresentationâ itself. Course work will include 1 short paper (5-7 pp.); 1 class presentation; 1 midterm exam, and one final paper (12 pp.).

title: Literature and Commodity Culture
description: This course will examine the meaning and representation of âthingsâ in a diverse group of U. S. cultural texts from the late-nineteenth and into the early-twentieth century, a period widely identified as critical in the consolidation of mass consumerism and an era of U. S. external imperialism and economic expansion. We will also read selected works from the mid-nineteenth century, when an urban consumer culture was first being established and represented. Surveying a variety of cultural forms--including literary and film texts, advertising manuals, newspaper and magazine advertisements, and promotional and documentary materials about the display of âthingsâ in the 1893 Chicago Worldâs Fair--as well as a range of critical and theoretical essays on the subject, we will focus on the interrelations of gender, sexuality, race, ethnicity, class and nation in U. S. commodity culture. In particular, we will examine the diverse desires and identifications articulated through commodities in U. S. cultural texts, and the complex ways that individuals use commodities to construct (or deconstruct) individual and communal identities. Issues addressed will include: the "feminization" of consumption, and the complex relationship between feminism and consumerism, during this period; the ideological import of representations of acculturation or "Americanization" through consumption that were especially widespread in immigrant fiction during this era; the international, imperialist significance of tropes of American "overcivilization," "abundance" and "conspicuous consumption" in advertisements and fictional narratives; interrelations between depictions of commodity culture and the politics of "passing" in African American fiction; the saturation of commodity culture (especially advertising) with racial signifiers and imagery, and the racial fantasies activated in and by mass consumption; and the historically and theoretically complex relationship between cinema and commodity spectacle. Selected literary authors will likely include: Willa Cather, Kate Chopin, Stephen Crane, Maria Cummins, Theodore Dreiser, Pauline Hopkins, Henry James, Nella Larsen, Frank Norris, Edith Wharton, Anzia Yezierska, We will also watch at least one film: John Stahlâs Imitation of Life (1934).

title: Poems in the World: Personal Voice, Social Confrontations.
description: Fall 2008 How do poems uncover troubles in society? How do poems cause trouble for a poet, and for others? Though lyric poems are written and read in private, they can reflect and confront the social world in which the issues of identity, justice, and moral advocacy are at play (and war). We will read poems that face such problems, and that have brought social consequencesâon the poet, and on his/her community: poems of personal confession and poems of witness; poems by prisoners and victims; poems by repressors and rabble-rousers. Weâll also read essays on the poetâs social responsibility and, as many of our works come from other languages, essays on translation. Weâll write short analyses; give class presentations; pursue a research topic; and learn from the inside what makes these poems succeed.

title: Seminar: Poetry of Witness
description: In this seminar, we will read the works of poets who endured the impress of extremity in the twentieth century and the early twenty-first, those who suffered imprisonment, torture, forced exile, warfare, military occupation, house arrest. We will consider works in translation as well as poetry written in English, and will contextualize these works historically, while also considering the philosophical, ethical, and theoretical dimensions of the thought of witness. Poets will include Anna Akhmatova, Paul Celan, Miklos Radnoti, Robert Desnos, Nazim Hikmet and Mahmoud Darwish, as well as more newly translated poetsâSaadi Yussef, U Sam Oeur and Semezdin Mehmedinovic.

title: Critical Issues in Literary Studies
description: This course offers a broad overview of major twentieth- and twenty-first century critical approaches to literary and cultural studies. We will discuss the global impact of postcolonial and postmodern cultures and analyze literature or film by Wole Soyinka, Amitav Ghosh, Earl Lovelace, Ridley Scott and Christopher Nolan. Perhaps no critical movement in the last fifty years has influenced literary studies more profoundly than the so-called âlinguistic turn.â We will therefore address the key elements of New Criticism, Russian Formalism, Structuralism and Post-Structuralism, emphasizing the study of Semiotics and the theory and practice of Deconstruction. The class will also explore how these critical ideas and expressions have influenced three major schools of thought: Feminism, Marxism and Psychoanalysis. International films, critical documentaries, food and popular music, will supplement our class discussions and contribute to a larger sense of culture and identity in the modern world.

title: Intro to Creative Writing
description: Fall 2008 This course will introduce you to genres of creative writing by studying and practicing four modes: fiction, dialogue, poetry, and personal prose. We will explore and discuss great models of each of these forms; we'll hear craft lectures from the great writers in our Department and elsewhere who produce them; we will write critical prose on selected works; and then we'll try our hand at composing poems, stories, personal essays, and dialogue (for radio). Among the authors studied: D.H. Lawrence, Michael Ondaatje, and Anna Deavere Smith. You should already have experience in studying and writing in one of these genres, and be prepared for intensive research in how each form of writing happens, how it renders human experience, voice, plot, and vision. Be prepared both to experiment with literary forms and to share your work with others.

title: Short Fiction Workshop
description: Fall 2008 What makes a story sing? In this workshop, each student will develop his or her own original short stories while examining the elements that make any work of fiction truly brilliant. Questions of form, genre, style, and voice will be central to our investigation as we construct our own pieces and critique our classmates'. While the emphasis will be on the act of writing and the craft of fiction, we will also read selected short masterpieces of the genre with, as we move from draft to piece to masterpiece. Permission needed from instructor. Send letter explaining why you want to take this class and one page of your best creative writing to jnf5@georgetown.edu by April 1. You will be informed by April 7.

title: Advanced Expermental Fiction Workshop
description: BY APPLICATION ONLYâinterested students must 1) have successfully completed a college beginning-level creative writing class; and 2) submit a 1-page writing sample and 1-page cover letter . SUBMIT your application by email to jnf5@georgetown.edu by November 7; you will be notified about the status of your application by November 10. In this advanced writing workshop, we will read key texts of 20th and 21st-century experimental fiction. Using these writings as springboards, we will create our own weekly experiments. The class is student-led, and demands enormous commitment from its members. Expect to read sexually transgressive, politically charged, mixed media works. Expect to expand your notion of what constitutes a text. Expect to exceed your own expectations of your writing. Credits: 3 Prerequisites: One beginning-level college creative writing class; acceptance of application (see above) Deadline to Submit Application: November 7 Date by which you will receive a response: November 10

title: Introduction to Poetry Writing
description: In a workshop format, we will study a wide variety of published poems, and write and discuss our own poems, paying close attention to matters of form, image, figure, rhythm, voice, the "plot." Other activities we will pursue: a short critical paper on a recent book of poems; memorization of single poems; revision; a portfolio of your work; an anthology of poems by others; a class reading; visit by poets; attendance of local poetry readings; more revision.

title: Lannan Poetry Seminar
description: Spring 2009 Framed around the Reading Series, the Lannan Poetry Seminar encourages dynamic critical thinking and creativity with a view to examining literary forms and practices; the work of individual authors; the relationship between writing and other arts; and the place of writing in contemporary culture. Fellows study and discuss the work of the yearâs scheduled guests and then produce an independent final project (creative or critical) under the guidance of the instructor. There are also opportunities for students to share their own writing and, importantly, to participate in a service-learning componentâorganizing and facilitating educational outreach efforts in the D.C. Community. Enrollment in this seminar is limited to the Lannan Fellows.

title: Film-Making for Writers
description: Fall 2008 The course introduces students to the basic techniques of the screenplay. In the first half of the term students intensively study selected classic scripts. The second half explores models of screenwriting from outside the American canon. Regular writing assignments culminate in an in-depth outline of an original contemporary feature. The course is intended for students considering careers as scriptwriters but it should also be of value to students interested in creative writing, drama as a genre, and the cultural and critical study of cinema. Students considering careers in film should schedule themselves in ways that permit them to move onto the Interemediate and Adavanced courses before graduation. See English 336 and English 337.

title: Tutorial: Intermediate Scriptwriting
description: Students who demonstrate aptitude in Film-Making for Writers (English 335) may apply for permission to take Intermediate Scriptwriting. Working in tutorials, accepted students will write an original short script which they will then shoot during the second half of the term. Because time within the semester is limited, students are encouraged to discuss script ideas with the instructor before the beginning of the term.

title: Tutorial: Advanced Scriptwriting
description: Open only to students who have completed English 336, and only by permission of the instructor. In this tutorial-based course students write original, full-length scripts. A complete feature-length script is required for successful completion of the course. This course may be taken a second time, at the discretion of the instructor.

title: Creative Non-Fiction Writing Workshop
description: Spring 2009 An intensive writing workshop focused on discovering, researching and crafting first-person narrative, including memoir, personal essay, features, travel and nature writing. Readings and writing across the spectrum of this innovative âfourth genre.â Students should expect to write constantly and to contribute, as critical readers and writers, to the common cause of the workshop.

title: Persuasive Writing
description: Fall 2008 In this intensive writing course we will read, analyze, and write various samples of persuasive writing including public service announcements, promotions, and executive briefings. We will examine as well the power of narrative in the arts of persuasion by reading Jonathon Kozolâs Amazing Grace as a non-fiction narrative âpersuasionâ advocating for community involvement. In a similar vein, we read the novel Bastard Out of Carolina by Dorothy Allison as a piece of advocacy literature that alerts the public about child abuse. We will complement these texts with a close study of Rottenbergâs Elements of Argument as we explore explicit methods for making claims and warrants. Students doing community-based learning (CBL), should sign up for ENG 341-02 "CBL: Persuasive Writing.â Community-based learning, a course-based pedagogy, involves student work with disadvantaged and underserved individuals or groups. This work is structured to meet community-defined needs. Thus, students will participate in community service and create documents for that service group/non-profit and make its advocacy issues the focus for projects and topics in our persuasive writing units. Such projects might include informational videos, pamphlets, proposals or advocacy materials. This will entail approximately 25-30 hours of community-related work over the course of the semester. Evaluation will be made from an initial research paper; classroom interaction and BlackBoard postings; frequent short papers and materials from which students will assemble a final portfolio; a mid-term which will include a section on grammar, and one oral persuasion in relation to their chosen topic (or community service placement). Texts: Lunsford and Ruskiewiczâs Everythingâs an Argument; Rottenberg's Elements of Argument; Hackerâs A Pocket Style Manual; Kozolâs Amazing Grace; Allisonâs Bastard Out of Carolina and various handouts and materials gathered by the class on current issues.

title: Revolution & Respect: Writing for a Cause
description: Fall 2008 Using Black protest literature of the Civil Rights era as a model, this course will show students how to craft fiction for a purpose. Do you want to reform schools, expose genocide, stop abortion, end a war, promote or limit immigration? Whatever your political agenda, you can advance itâas writers always haveâthrough thoughtful and intentional storytelling. Readings will include authors such as James Baldwin, Lorraine Hansberry, and John A. Williams, and students will be asked to reflect, in writing, on how these artists identify and attack injustice in their work. The goal of the course, however, will be for each student to produce, through various exercises and extensive drafts, one solid piece of short fiction that advocates a position on a public issue.

title: Empathy and Activism
description: What is the relationship between the practice of empathy and political activism? In the 18th century, philosophers thought that sympathetic feeling was central to civic activity. Weâll test that notion by investigating the relationship between empathy and activism in the transatlantic world of nineteenth-century abolitionism. The diverse reading list will include philosophical treatises on sympathy, political theories about citizenship, and anti-slavery poems, short stories, speeches, letters, conference bulletins, and essays. With guidance, students will create their own research projects.

title: Marxism and Culture
description: Fall 2008 This course provides neither a comprehensive survey nor historical overview of the âtheoretical practiceâ of Marxist cultural production. Instead, it will offer a highly selective and idiosyncratic probe into some of the key theoretical issues and actions associated with the Marxist cultural tradition around the world. We will explore the realm of culture in its varied relationships with the political and revolutionary demands of socialism in several contexts, including both those of actually-existing socialist states and the capitalist West. We will read representative statements from Marx and Engels from the mid-nineteenth century including the âCommunist Manifesto,â Capital Vol. I and the Grundrisse, but will concentrate on more recent culture and criticism as it relates to postcolonial liberation movements, democracy, civil rights, gender and racial equality post World War II. Texts will include literature, theory, philosophy, political science, economics, film, critical media studies. One short paper, midterm exam, one long collaborative research paper.

title: Advanced Seminar: Testimonial Fictions
description: Testimonial Fictions: Atrocity, Memory and Sexuality in Post-Dictatorship US Latino Literature The course surveys mostly literary representations of US Latino diasporic communities negotiating the historical, political and cultural legacies of extreme state-sponsored violence on the part of dictatorial regimes in their homelands. Case studies include Dominican America after Trujillo, Haitian America after the Duvaliers, Cuban America "after" Castro, and Guatemalan/Salvadoran America after the 1980's-era juntas. Writers and texts include Julia Alvarez (In the Name of SalomÃ©), Junot DÃ­az (Oscar Wao), Edwidge Danticat (The Dew Breaker and Brother, I'm Dying), Reinaldo Arenas (Before Night Falls), Cristina GarcÃ­a (A Handbook to Luck) and Hector Tobar (The Tattooed Soldier). Course readings will also include significant scholarly work in Latin American and US Latino Studies, as well as in critical and political theory.

title: Cultural Constructions of Motherhood
description: This course will examine constructions of motherhood in the U.S. from the 1950s to the present. We intend to take an interdisciplinary approach to the material, including cultural and social theory; history; popular texts; fiction; poetry; film; television. Possible texts and authors include: Adrienne Rich; Sigmund Freud; Nancy Chodorow; Arlie Hochschild; Susan Douglas (The Mommy Myth); Angela Davis; Audre Lorde; Patricia Hill Collins; Tillie Olsen; Sarah Ruddick; Sharon Olds; Louise Erdrich; Dorothy Allison; Sandra Cisneros; John Updike; I Love Lucy; Roseanne; Julia; Imitation of Life; Far From Heaven; and Rosemaryâs Baby.

title: Introduction to Journalism
description: This course will teach students the basics of newspaper and magazine writing, beginning with how to assess the news value of an event or situation, how to develop story ideas and how to put together a story. Much attention will be paid to writing clearly and accurately and developing interviewing skills. The ethical issues facing journalists will be also be a focus. Guest speakers will includes reporters, critics and editors from Washington and Baltimore. There will be several short writing assignments and a final project. (Jon Decker is a Washington correspondent for Reuters Television, the USA Radio Network and LBC Radio [London Broadcast Corporation].)

title: Introduction to Journalism
description: Fall 2008, Steve Sternberg Spring 2009, Athelia Knight This course will teach students the basics of newspaper and magazine writing, beginning with how to assess the news value of an event or situation, how to develop story ideas and how to put together a story. Much attention will be paid to writing clearly and accurately and developing interviewing skills. The ethical issues facing journalists will be also be a focus. Guest speakers will includes reporters, critics and editors from Washington. There will be several writing assignments and a final project. (Steve Sternberg, an award-winning medical and investigative reporter now at USA TODAY, has also worked at two major metro dailies, the Miami Herald and the Atlanta-Journal Constitution.)

title: Introduction to Journalism
description: Fall 2008 Athelia Knight This course will teach students the basics of newspaper and magazine writing, beginning with how to assess the news value of an event or situation, how to develop story ideas and how to put together a story. Much attention will be paid to writing clearly and accurately and developing interviewing skills. The ethical issues facing journalists will be also be a focus. Guest speakers will includes reporters, critics and editors from Washington and Baltimore. There will be several short writing assignments and a final project. Spring 2009 Murray Schweitzer This course will teach students the basics of newspaper and magazine writing, beginning with how to assess the news value of an event or situation, how to develop story ideas and how to put together a story. Much attention will be paid to improving students' writing styles and developing interviewing techniques, as well as consideration of ethical issues facing journalists today. Several guest speakers from the Washington journalism community will visit. Writing assignments will include several short news assignments and one longer feature story. (Murray Schweitzer is the producer for Liz Crenshaw, NBC's local consumer reporter.)

title: Genres of Journalism
description: Fall 2008 Charles Lane Journalistic Fraud Fiction has recurrently masqueraded as fact in the American press. Why? How? What can be done? The goal: a nuanced sense of journalism as truth. This undergraduate seminar introduces students to the phenomenon of journalistic fraud and to various ideas about the origins and consequences of such misconduct. Its premise, elaborated through a series of case studies and intensive student discussion, is that journalistic fraud has been recurrent throughout the history of the American press, and that it has taken a variety of forms, ranging from the systematic to the spectacular. Fraud does not occur randomly, but under identifiable circumstances that are consistent across historical and technological eras. Journalistic fraud will be taken seriously as an ethical breach that threatens the legitimacy of a crucial democratic institution. But students will be challenged to test the assumptions that underlie this claim. In particular, the seminar will interrogate the bright lines asserted to separate unethical journalism from the acceptable kind. The goal is not to deconstruct journalism but to develop a more nuanced sense of its professed quest for objective truth. (This is not, however, a course in "media bias.") If all goes well, students acquire a complicated sense of what truth really is, and thus a deeper respect for it. Readings: Course materials will come from classic and contemporary print journalism, and from the surprisingly rich motion picture "literature" on this topic. Readings may include selections by Edgar Allan Poe, Joseph Mitchell (Old Mr. Flood), H. L. Mencken (Newspaper Days) and Tom Wolfe (The Right Stuff), as well as articles by Janet Cooke, Stephen Glass, Jayson Blair and Jack Kelley. We will also review the internal report by The Washington Post's ombudsman about the Janet Cooke case, The New York Times investigation of Jayson Blair, the CBS review of the 60 Minutes II report on President Bush's purported avoidance of regular military service, and the USA Today internal report on Jack Kelley. We will read relevant selections from Seth Mnookin's Hard News, Benjamin Bradlee's A Good Life, the monograph Lies! Lies! Lies!: Toward a Psychopathology of Human Deception, by Dr. Charles Ford, and Lying, by Sissela Bok. Films will include Under Fire (1984), Street Smart (1987) and Shattered Glass (2003). Guest speakers TBD. Writing assignments: Students will be asked to prepare brief (two-three page) papers each week, based on the reading assignment. There will be a substantial (20-25 page) assignment due at the end of the term. Topics may be based on students' investigations of actual news media reports they suspect of fraud. (Charles Lane, an editorial board member of the Washington Post, has previously served as editor of the New Republic; senior editor, Berlin bureau chief and Central America correspondent for Newsweek.) Spring 2009 John McKinnon Business Reporting This course teaches students the basics of reporting on business, finance and the private sector. Topics include corporate excesses and failures, such as Enron; the interactions between business, government and society; and emerging trends in the U.S. and world economies. The class will feature frequent guest speakers, including figures from the business world and government, as well as some of today's best reporters and editors. Materials will come primarily from newspapers and magazines but will include TV and film, as well as reports, public filings and academic texts. Skills to be emphasized include story conceptualization; source development and interview techniques; use of official records; and writing. Course requirements will include several short pieces and a final project. There are no formal prerequisites, but students will need energy, enthusiasm and insatiable curiosity about the real world. (John D. McKinnon is White House correspondent for The Wall Street Journal. He's previously covered the Treasury, tax policy, the federal budget and the economy for the Journal.)

title: Media Techniques
description: Fall 2008, Linda Kramer Jenning Spring 2009, Carol Guensburg This elective is for students who will be participating in an internship at a local media outlet (print, broadcast or Internet) during the summer or fall. It is the only way for students to receive credit for a media internship. This workshop-style course will give students an opportunity to advance their reporting and writing skills while sharing their internship experiences with other students engaged in similar journalistic pursuits. Written assignments will include a semester-long journal, two reported articles, and a final project that directly grows out of the internship experience. Students will have the opportunity to post their stories on a GU journalism web site. Required readings will focus on issues common to any journalistic endeavor such as ethics and fairness and the future of the profession in the new media era. Guest speakers will include prominent local journalists. Students must arrange the internship before enrolling in the course. (Prof. Kramer Jenning, lk78@georgetown.edu, is the Washington editor for Glamour and former deputy bureau chief for People. She also worked as a reporter for the Associated Press and as a television reporter and assignment editor).

title: Media & Social Justice: International Investigative Project
description: Fall 2008 A select group of undergraduate and graduate students will work collaboratively in a newsroom setting, in the spirit of the 2007-2008 Pearl Project investigation that sought answers regarding the 2002 kidnapping and murder of Wall Street Journal reporter Daniel Pearl in Karachi, Pakistan. Similarly, this yearâs course will seek answers regarding the death of another slain journalist, as yet not chosen, or a journalist whose life and work is in peril. Applicants to the class are asked to submit a 500-word essay suggesting either (1) a journalist who died in the line of duty whose murder, as well as unfinished journalistic endeavors, could become the classâs focus of investigation, or (2) a journalist in peril whose unfinished journalistic work could become the classâs focus of investigation. The class project will seek to answer a number of questions. In the case of a journalist who has been killed: Who killed the journalist? Why did they kill him or her? What are the underlying international and domestic politics surrounding the case? What unfinished work was left behind to be tackled? In the case of a journalist in peril: Why is the journalist being targeted? Who is targeting the journalist? What are the underlying politics surrounding the case? What is the work of the journalist still be to completed? The project will also seek to look at the wider relationship between the press and the government and political climate of the journalistâs nation state.
