description: Beginning with an overview of late nineteenth-century Palestine and the arrival there of the first waves of Zionist settlers, the course then follows the growing conflict between two peoples through the emergence of the state of Israel, four subsequent Arab-Israeli wars, the peace process, and the collapse of that process in the year 2000.

title: The coming of the Civil War, 1820-1861
description: âBy the eternal,â exclaimed Andrew Jackson, âthe Union must and shall be preserved!â For much of the nineteenth century, Americans North and South assumed that it would be. Yet in 1861 the United States broke in two and Americans fought each other in the nationâs bloodiest and most formative conflict. The purpose of this class is to ask why and how that happened. More precisely, the course examines how the political, social, and intellectual institutions of the United States contained growing sectional tensions between North and South amid vast demographic, economic, and geographic growth, only to be severed by sectional crisis in 1860 and 1861. This course pays particular attention to the place of slavery within the economies, societies, and competing definitions of the legacy of the American Revolution that divided the two sections. Topics to be addressed include the Missouri Crisis, the Nullification Crisis, white and black lives in the Old South, the abolitionist movement, expansionism, Bleeding Kansas, and secession.

title: American Diplomatic History I
description: This survey of the history of American foreign relations follows the development of diplomacy in its international and domestic contexts from the colonial era to the aftermath of World War I. Topics covered include: the problems of organizing a new nation, expansion in North America and beyond, the impact of racism, war, and revolution, the rise to world power, as well as consideration of the economic, political, and social imperatives behind foreign policy making. Fall.

title: American Diplomatic History II
description: This survey of the history of American foreign relations follows the development of diplomacy in its international and domestic contexts from the aftermath of World War I to the present. Topics covered include: the impact of racism, war, and revolution, the United States as a world power, as well as consideration of the economic, political, and social imperatives behind foreign policy making. Spring.

title: History of American Labor Since 1870
description: This course surveys the history of American labor from the nineteenth century to the present. It examines the history of work, unions, and labor relations and connects the story of workers and their struggles to the larger history of the United States from the era of industrialization to globalization.

title: Oil and world power
description: Oil has been central to power and wealth since the early 20th century, and the history of oil provides important insights into the nature and dynamics of power and influence in the international system. This course will examine the interaction of the history of oil and key events in international history from the early 20th century to the present. This course can count for either the Us or the Middle East region for History majors.

title: Depression and War in the US and Europe, 1918-1945
description: This course in social and cultural history will examine the internal causes and effects of the post World War I global depression in the US and Europe. Specifically, the course will cover America's Great Depression and participation in World War II, the rise and fall of fascism in Italy and Germany, the rise of the Popular Front in Western Europe, and the character of totalitarian regimes in Nazi Germany and Stalinist Russia. Offered in Qatar.

title: Women and the Maikng of Modern America
description: This course offers a survey and discussion of the history of women in the United States from 1865 to the present. Major themes include the class, race, and regional diversity of women; history of social movements like suffrage and womenâs liberation; history of everyday life; and sexuality and family. The course will be a mix of lecture and discussion, with an equal emphasis on understanding historical arguments and using primary sources. The class will require three papers and two exams.

title: The American Revolution
description: When British colonists in North America declared themselves independent from the British Crown in 1776, they affected the most successful revolution in modern history. To this day, historians continue to try to make sense of their actions. Two primary questions concern us in this course. First, what makes a man or woman a revolutionary? Why do people who lived their entire lives as loyal subjects of the British Crown suddenly decide to reject that political identity and turn their worlds upside down? Second, how ârevolutionaryâ was the American Revolution? Did the Revolution have radical consequences, or was it ultimately a conservative affair? This course is divided into four parts that move chronologically and thematically through life and events in America from about 1750 to 1800. To appreciate the American Revolution we need first to understand at the social, cultural, economic, and religious context of the British Empire in general, and the North American colonies in particular, at the middle of the eighteenth century. Next we will turn to the question of political mobilization, focusing on the period from the Stamp Act crisis in March 1765 through the declaration of American independence in July 1776. Then we look at how war offered the promise of new opportunities for freedom, but the creation of a new political order did not come easily. Finally, we will turn to the consequences of the Revolution, at home and abroad. By the end of the semester students are expected to have developed their own explanation for why men and women chose to support Revolution and just how ârevolutionaryâ the American Revolution proved to be.

title: United States Since 1945
description: This course will examine American history from World War II to the present. While it will deal with the rise and passing of the Cold War as a unifying force in American political life and with the emergence of the United States as the primary power in the developed world, the course will focus mainly upon the tremendous social changes that American society witnessed during this tumultuous period. It will examine the making of the Cold War "liberal consensus" in the 1950s, its subsequent unmaking in the 1960s and the attempts to reformulate a new consensus which have characterized our politics since 1968. In taking up this theme, the course will attempt to illuminate both the ways in which America's changing politics and culture shaped the lives of ordinary Americans and the ways in which ordinary Americans themselves have helped shape our politics and culture.

title: Historical Interpretation
description: This colloquium aims to introduce students to historical methodology and interpretation. Topics include the definition and use of different types of sources, the question of objectivity, the definition of proper subjects for historical investigation, and the evolution of different ideas of causality. We will do this through a study of the history of western historiography: we will read the works of several historians from the ancient world to the present day. The class consists entirely of discussion focused on these texts. There are no exams; there will be several writing assignments.

title: Independent Study in History
description: Special reading tutorials or independent research projects may be proposed by students; ordinarily, only students with a 3.5 average may request such tutorials. The student who wishes to propose such a tutorial of project must gain the approval and consent of a Faculty mentor within the Department. The tutorial has to be on a topic not usually covered in regularly offered courses, and the student must demonstrate his/her need for the course. The Faculty mentor will supervise each project which must result in a substantial historical essay. Approval by the departmental Director of Undergraduate Studies is required.

title: Independent Study in History
description: Special reading tutorials or independent research projects may be proposed by students; ordinarily, only students with a 3.5 average may request such tutorials. The student who wishes to propose such a tutorial of project must gain the approval and consent of a Faculty mentor within the Department. The tutorial has to be on a topic not usually covered in regularly offered courses, and the student must demonstrate his/her need for the course. The Faculty mentor will supervise each project which must result in a substantial historical essay. Approval by the departmental Director of Undergraduate Studies is required.

title: International Perspectives and Global History
description: Processes of historical change have become increasingly global during recent centuries. This colloquium explores diverse approaches to historical globalization: political, diplomatic, economic, ecological, cultural. In addition, it examines the relations between globalizing processes and history as it is experienced, discussed, and debated in nations and communities. It asks why historical understandings have focused on national developments, while the forces of change have operated on ever larger scales. This course is targeted primarily at SFS juniors and will be taught on a rotation by different faculty members.

title: A History of Australian Frontiers
description: Australian historians have recently engaged in vigorous debates about Australia's nineteenth-century frontier history, the so-called "history wars." The central point of contention concerns the level of violence in these frontiers and disputes over historical sources. This class will examine the history of Australia's frontiers in light of these debates. We will examine the processes and features of colonization in Australia and its impact upon indigenous Australia, comparing it to U.S. frontier myth and history. We will also engage in an examination of the processes of writing national histories and their implications for Australian national identity and contemporary political debate.

title: Witches and Witchcraft, 1400-1750
description: What is a witch? This seminar first investigates the phenomenon of witchcraft in Europe before exploring the transmission of witch beliefs across the Atlantic to America, focusing especially on the outbreak at Salem. It also explores the variety of witch beliefs held by Africans and Native Americans in the Atlantic world and the impact of colonization and cultural contact on the expression and meaning of those beliefs. History majors can apply this course to whichever region best reflects their work in the seminar.

title: Comparative history of the U.S. and South Africa
description: Most casual observers would not necessarily identify the United States and South Africa as similar or comparable societies. But their early histories largely parallel each other: both countries were settled by European immigrants who had contentious relationships with the indigenous population and who relied heavily on imported slave labor; both societies developed elaborate racial hierarchies and fought internal wars in part over the nature of those hierarchies; and both instituted elaborate systems of segregation in the wake of those wars. But in the 1940s, as the United States began to move away from its segregationist policies, South Africa hardened its policies into apartheid. Students in this course will explore how ideas and policies about race and rights have developed in the United States and South Africa since the seventeenth century. We will consider how comparative history aids in understanding race and racism in our own society, and we will seek to answer some key questions inherent in all comparative history: Are these two societies in fact comparable? Or are they too fundamentally different to be compared? What does a comparison of them reveal, and what does it obscure? This course is a seminar. There will be no lecturing, and students will be expected to pick up the broad outlines of U.S. and South African history on their own. Students are expected to do all the readings promptly and carefully and to actively participate in the class discussions. Assignments will include response papers to assigned readings, a historiographical essay and a final research paper.

title: Environment people and state in Africa
description: This seminar examines how interactions between people, environment, and state contributed to social, economic, political, and ecological changes experienced in different parts of Africa beginning in the late nineteenth century and ending in the late twentieth century. It focuses on four major themes: indigenous perceptions and use of environments, colonial and postcolonial state policies on Africaâs environments and motivations behind them, local impacts of global conservation discourses, and African adaptations, as well as responses to the consequences of the state interventions on the environment. Going through these themes, we will also highlight the significance of social differencesâgender, class, and raceâin shaping contests between African subjects and state officials for control over the environments. Three short papers and a final paper. Required Readings: Helge, Kjekshus. 1996. Ecology Control and Economic Development in East African History. London: James Currey. James, Fairhead et al, Misreading African Landscape: Society and Ecology in the Forest Savanna Mosaic. Cambridge University Press, 1996. James, Scott. 1998. Seeing Like A State: How Certain Schemes to improve the Human Conditions Have Failed. New Haven: Yale University Press. Terence, Ranger. 1999. Voices from the Rocks: Nature, Culture, and History from the Matopos Hills of Zimbabwe. Bloomington: Indiana University Press Christopher, O. Conte. 2004. Highland Sanctuary: Environmental History in Tanzaniaâs Usambara Mountains. Athens: Ohio University Press. Wangari, Maathai. Unbowed.

title: The slk road
description: More than a network of caravan routes, the term "Silk Road" stands for a wide range of religious, artistic, musical, technological, culinary and biological as well as economic exchanges across Eurasia. As traditionally conceived, the high-points of the silk road were from the 1st c. BCE to 2nd c. CE, from 7th - 9th c. CE, and in the 13th â 14th c., and involved linkages between China, India, Persia and the Mediterranean via Central Asia. While this course will cover these classic periods of trans-Eurasian connectivity, it will also consider questions of cross-cultural exchange and "East-West" contact more generally, and for some purposes bring the discussion up to the present. Moreover, in format this course will experiment with some neo-Silk Road connectivity of its own: it will be taught simultaneously by Prof. Yao Dali at Fudan University in Shanghai, and GU students will have opportunities to interact with and get to know Chinese students in a virtual silk road caravansarai through blogs, joint projects, skype-type conversations, and video-conferenced plenary sessions between the two classes. There will be a midterm and a final, as well as short papers and a final project. As befits the subject-matter, besides a mixture of primary and secondary readings, we will examine arts, music and other materials in audio-visual and other sorts of media. No prerequisites, though some knowledge of Chinese or Asian history is highly recommended. And if you know the Chinese language, you will have a great time.

title: China from Liberation to the Olympics
description: The Peopleâs Republic of China (PRC) was established in 1949 after a century of foreign humiliation, subjugation, and internal turmoil. Today, the PRC is poised to emerge as one of the most dominant and vibrant players in the 21st century world. The intervening six decades of Chinese history is a story of epic proportions. In 1949, Mao Zedong, leader of the Chinese Communist Party (CCP), put the world on notice that âthe Chinese people, comprising one-quarter of humanity, have now stood up.â That same year, he reminded the Chinese people that the end of the Chinese civil war was âonly the first step in a long march of ten thousand liâ¦The Chinese revolution is great, but the road after the revolution will be longer, the work greater and more arduous.â This purpose of this seminar is to trace that journey. As such, this course will survey the major domestic and international trends, policies, events, and individuals that played a role in shaping the PRCâs path to the present. The format of the course will be discussion. Each week, the class will begin with a general review of the historical context of the period being examined, followed by a student-led discussion of the weekâs assigned readings.

title: China and the United States
description: âPeace is the foundation for development while development is fundamental for peace. For years, the Chinese government and people have made unremitting efforts to create a peaceful international environment.â¦.China did not seek hegemony in the past, nor does it now, and will not do so in the future when it gets stronger. China's development will never pose a threat to anyone.â âChina's Peaceful Development Road,â Information Office of China's State Council, December 23, 2005. ââ¦.Chinaâs rise presents a comprehensive challenge, in part military, but principally economic and intellectualâ¦.China is becoming an increasingly able competitor on the global playing field America did so much to build. China wants to play ball with America. The question is: Will America perform well in a game and on a field it long dominated?â David M. Lampton, âThe âChina challengeâ: Is America headed for second place?â (March 13, 2005), Boston.com (Boston Globe) In the 21st century, perhaps no bilateral relationship is more important than that between the Peopleâs Republic of China and the United States. How this relationship between the globeâs sole superpower and a rapidly emerging contender evolves will not only affect their state-to-state interactions but also will affect the daily lives of their citizens, the nations of the Asia-Pacific region, and, inevitably, will have global repercussions. To understand and fully appreciate the current status of US-PRC relations, and in order to envision the possible course this relationship might follow, an examination of their past interactions is necessary. To provide such a foundation, this colloquium will survey major trends, policies, and events that played a role in shaping the Sino-American path to the present and will continue to influence the path to the future. The format of the course will be discussion. Each week, the class will begin with a general review of the historical context of the period being examined, followed by a student-led discussion of the weekâs assigned readings.

title: Monks, Nuns, Hermits
description: This seminar will attempt to explain why certain members of the Christian church have chosen to reject the traditional path of marriage and family in favor of a counter-cultural lifestyle of celibacy and (usually) poverty. It will deal with the origins of Christian monasticism in the third century and trace its development down to the advent of the friars in the thirteenth. What did the men and women who withdrew into the eastern deserts hope to accomplish by withdrawing from society? How was the monastic impulse transformed by its encounter with new political and social circumstances in western Europe? What accounts for the tremendous variety of monastic orders, and how do hermits fit into the monastic movement? To explore these questions, we will read some modern historians, but we will rely heavily on primary sources, especially saintsâ lives and monastic rules, in an effort to understand how monasticism influenced, and was influenced by, the major historical currents of this crucial millennium in European history.

title: The Present and the Past
description: Historein, to relate, histoire, story â these two roots of âhistoryâ will serve as the touchstones of our inquiry into the relationship of the present and the past. This class grows out of my childhood experiences in Hartford, CT, where I learned about life from Irish politicians, basketball lifers, and an assortment of Runyanesque characters. I lived among tale tellers, whose versions of history evaded textbooks everywhere. I watched my native Hartford destroyed by political ineptitude, economic greed, and social disorder, moving to New York just in time to see the City driven to the brink by similar forces. As a citizen, I want to learn how politics, social life, culture, and economics interact with ideas to create the world around us. As an historian, I want to learn how societies manipulate their past to structure the future, a goal apparent in the title of my most recent book, From Tribes to Nation: The Making of France, 500-1799. Special warning: if you are uncomfortable with ambiguity, if you want answers instead of questions, Iâm the wrong professor for you.

title: Topics in European History (see description)
description: This number will be used for seminars devoted to specific subjects in the area of European history. Students may take more than one of the courses offered under this number. Each course will receive its own sub-title. The following courses under this number are planned for the 2008-09 academic year. Fall 2008: Global environmental history since 1945, taught by Professor Peter Engelke: This course examines the environmental history of the world since 1945. As a course in global history, it is designed along thematic rather than geographic lines. Weekly sessions are thus organized by either an environmental topic (such as water resources) or by topics that have proven significant for the natural environment (such as warfare). Additionally, as a course in world history, we will discuss historical events and trends from all parts of the globe, with an emphasis on the interaction between the rich and poor parts of the world, as well as on those parts of the world, rich or poor or simply uninhabited, that have provided the natural capital for the global economy. Specific historical topics include: the treatment of nature under socialism and capitalism; the emergence of the postwar environmental movement; the impact of warfare, including the Cold War, on the natural environment. This course also addresses the origins of those issues that dominate environmental discourse in the twenty-first century: climate change; fossil fuels; water pollution and scarcity; global deforestation; wildlife and conservation; agriculture and food safety; and the question of âsustainability.â Readings consist of a mixture of primary and secondary source material. Students are asked to write either three short papers or one term paper. Grading is based on written work and class participation. History majors may apply this course to other regions, depending on their written work. Spring 2009: Military occupations from Ireland to Iraq, taught by Professor Corey Campion: Since Coalition forces toppled the government of Saddam Hussein and assumed control of Iraq in April 2003, military occupation has remained a subject of intense debate. Questions regarding the goals and conduct of occupation troops and the duration of the Coalitionâs presence in Iraq continue to fill the airwaves and inspire the pens of academics and pundits alike. This colloquium contributes historical perspective to the current debate. It explores the experience of eleven military occupations from the French Revolution to the present and considers the perspectives of both the occupiers and the occupied. Through a combination of primary texts, secondary literature, photos, and films students will explore the history and historiography of each of the selected cases. While each discussion session will focus on a specific occupation, students are encouraged to think comparatively across the syllabus. History majors may count this course toward the European, United States, Asian or Middle Eastern region, depending on written work.

title: Christians and the Holocaust
description: This course addresses the reactions of European Christians to the Nazi persecution and killing of Jews. The course starts with an overview of the history of antisemitism throughout the centuries, and then moves to the core of the course: an exploration of the specific attitudes and actions of both Catholics and Protestants in the inter-war and World War II period. In the section on Catholics and the Catholic Church, two to three weeks is spent examining the issue of the papacy and the Jews, concentrating especially on the controversy over Pius XII. Students will read some of the most important literature by the detractors and by the supporters of Pius XII so as to understand the chief arguments and the evidence offered by the two sides. Following that, the course considers the role of the Catholic clergy, the religious orders, and the laity, with particular attention to the efforts of a distinguished Georgetown professor, the late Dr. Jan Karski, sometimes known as "the man who tried to stop the Holocaust." The section on Protestants will survey the actions of the various denominations, certain key pastors, and rescue operations on the part of brave Protestant families or individuals. The course will conclude with a short survey of post-WW II efforts to clarify the historical record and improve Jewish-Christian relations.

title: Collaboration and Resistance in Europe 1933-1956
description: Using a mixture of film and written sources (memoirs, autobiographical accounts, collections of documents), we will examine the question of collaboration and resistance in World War II. The classes will be a mixture of films, lecture, and discussion.

title: European Fascism
description: This course will look at the origins, practice, and nature of Fascism as it developed in Europe between the world wars. To that end, readings by Fascist theorists such as Mussolini and Hitler, as well as by historians who analyze and describe the phenomenon will be used. Films from and about the era will also be part of the material for the course. In addition to discussing historical Fascism, the course will also take a look at what, if anything, connects the meaning of Fascism as it existed in the past and how that term is used today.

title: Art science and technology in the Renaissance
description: This course examines the integrated world of art, science, and technology from a comparative perspective during the broad historical period known as the Renaissance, from roughly 1300 to 1650. It focuses on art, architecture, and other forms of construction as expressed in images (of all sorts, including maps), buildings, and machines. Rather than viewing these forms of expression as originating in mutually exclusive cultures, this course aims to dissolve customary distinctions between art, science, and technology. The significance of that melding will be used to understand broader historical issues such as exploration and globalization, expressions of power, social distinctions, the impact of print culture, religious disputes, and even cultural changes what âseeingâ meant.

title: Topics in Latin American History (see description)
description: This number will be used for seminars devoted to specific subjects in the area of Latin American history. Students may take more than one of the courses offered under this number. Each course will be announced in the course schedule and receive its own sub-title. In Fall 2008 the following course is offered: Healing, Gender, and Power in Latin America (Professor Veronica Vallejo): This colloquium examines health and healing in Latin America as a means to study power relations within the colonial context and under modern state structures. We will explore gender, the relationships between men and women, as well as hierarchies of class and race as they changed from the conquest era to the modern period. Students will use monographs, articles and primary texts in order to explore such topics as how Indigenous, European, and African peoples created a contested medical culture in Latin America, the âDark Side of the Enlightenmentâ and its repression of popular healers, slave healers during revolution, the Eugenics movement and national debates about prostitution and public health. This class is organized chronologically and geographically spans Mexico, Peru, Guatemala, Haiti, and Brazil. Class will meet once a week to discuss shared readings. Students will be responsible for three papers and a map quiz.

title: Pirates, Soldiers, and Diplomats: Islam and the West, 1500-1914
description: The course examines violence and diplomacy between the Islamic "gunpowder" empires (Ottoman, Safavid, and Mughal) and some of their adversaries (Spanish and Austrian Habsburgs, and Russian empires). It studies competing ideologies (jihad and crusade) and practices, raids (e.g. Tatar and Cossack), piracy (the Barbary corsairs and the Knights of St. John), imperial campaigns, frontier warfare, as well as wars of nationalism and imperialism. Studying the diplomatic relations between Islam and the West, special attention will be given to questions such as Islamic and Western intelligence, permanent embassies in Istanbul, and the conversion from unilateral to reciprocal diplomacy. Spring.

title: Topics in Middle East History: Comparative nationalism in the Middle East and North Africa
description: This number will be used for seminars devoted to specific subjects in the area of Middle East history. Students may take more than one of the courses offered under this number. Each course will be announced in the course schedule and receive its own sub-title. In Spring 2009 the following course is offered: Comparative nationalism in the Middle East and North Africa (Professor Jonathan Wyrtzen): What is a nation? Can one be built? How did the ones that exist come to be? Why do some "work" and others not? These questions are of particular relevance for understanding the recent history of the Middle East and North Africa and are at the core of many of the most pressing current policy dilemmas. In light of these questions, this course will examine the rise of nationalism in the "Mashriq" (or Arab East) and "Maghrib" (or Arab West) in light of current theories about nationalism. In the first half of the course, the major typologies and debates in general discussions about nationalism (ethnic vs. civic, state-centric vs. popular, modernist vs. primordialist) will be introduced, as well as specific themes regarding the development of nationalism in the Middle East and North Africa including the influence of transnational (pan-Islamic and pan-Arab) ideologies, ethnicity, gender, and religion. In the second half, we will evaluate case studies from the Mashriq (Syria/Lebanon, Jordan, and Iraq) and the Maghrib (Algeria, Morocco, and Tunisia) in light of the analytical frameworks introduced earlier.

title: Islam in the West
description: The course will examine the formation and growth of the Muslim communities in North America and Europe. It will explore the history and dynamics of community development in a western context, the creation of an Islamic minority perspective, the challenges the community and the host countries face, and the variety of efforts to meet these challenges.

title: Revolutionary Thought in Islam
description: This course examines the history of revolutionary activity in Islam. It will focus on the interaction between the paradigm of the Prophet's example during the formative period of Islam, the Muslim debates on issues of peace, jihad, revolutionary change, government accountability and human responsibility for social, political and economic decay. It will study how these paradigms functioned in a variety of revolutionary movements in Islamic history including the Abbasids, Ikhwan al-Safa, the Fulani Jihad in Sub Saharan Africa, the Mahdi revolution in the Sudan, the Muslim Brotherhood of Egypt, Hamas, Jihad Islami and Tahrir in Palestine and Hisbullah in Lebanon.

title: Empires and the Greater Middle East, 1500-2005
description: The course seeks to understand how imperial grand strategies of the European Great Powers regarding the Greater Middle East âdefined as the Arab Middle East, Asia Minor, Iran, the Caucasus and Central Asiaâ as well as the strategies and policies of regional agencies influenced the fate of the region. Of the regional Islamic empires special attention will be given to Ottoman Turkey and Safavid and Qajar Persia. With regard to the European powers the course focuses on the British, Russian, Soviet and American strategies and experiences in the Middle East.

title: USSR and Russia after WWII
description: An exploration of the last 60+ years of the Soviet Union and Russia, in both internal and international dimensions. Topics include: late Stalinism, the Thaw, competition (sports, space, military), GULag, the USSR and the Third World, an in-depth focus on Leningrad (housing, daily life), 1968 in the SU and the world, economic crisis, perestroika, the 1991 revolution and politics in the new millenium. Sources will include newspapers, first-hand accounts, and movies. Emphasis on student research, with one major paper.

title: Novels of empire
description: A close reading of Rudyard Kipling's Kim, Orhan Pamuk's White Castle, Mikhail Lermontov's A Hero of Our Time, and Gillo Pontecorvo's film, The Battle of Algiers, in the context of their respective empires - British, Ottoman, Russian, and French. Some attention to theories of empire, but emphasis on historical setting. History majors may apply this course to various world regions.

title: Topics in Russian History: The Caucasus
description: This number will be used for seminars devoted to specific subjects in the area of Russian history. Students may take more than one of the courses offered under this number. Each course will be announced in the course schedule and receive its own sub-title. In Fall 2008 the following course is offered: The Caucasus (Professor Mikail Mamedov): This course will focus on the history of the Caucasus: Armenia, Georgia, Azerbaijan, and the north Caucasus area. The course will elucidate Russia's advance into this area, the mutual influences and interactions between colonizers and colonized. It will also discuss resistance to Russian rule as well as the empire's impact on the area. The course will examine emerging collective identities of the peoples of the Caucasus and the sources of their nationalism. The course will invite students to reflect on the historical roots of the modern conflicts in the region. We shall confront such questions as to what extent the consequences of the Russian and Soviet heritage contributed to the continuous turmoil in the Caucasus? Or to what extent the national elites (intelligentsia, intellectuals) of the peoples of the Caucasus were responsible for demonizing those of the others (their neighbors), thus contributing to the escalation of violence and war? What are the key issues of post-Soviet politics in the area? Why is the Caucasus still important for Russia and the West? Is Chechnya a keystone of Russian security? How did history contribute to this notion? Is it legitimate to compare historical events from the nineteenth century to the modern time? Students may, if they choose, incorporate comparisons to similar historical phenomena in the history of other countries and regions (especially the Balkans). One of the course goals will be to develop critical historical thinking, reasoning, and analysis of important historical events, and their relations to the present.

title: Judaic-Christian encounters
description: This course will examine the nexus of textual, religious-cultural and social interactions between the makers of and adherents to Judaism and Christianity from time of the rise of Judaic messianism and apocalypticism, through the development of both Christianity and rabbinic Judiasm, into the sixteenth and seventeenth centuries of the Christian Era. The students will read a variety of primary sources (scriptural, theological-polemical, legal, and autobiographical), as well as secondary studies â mainly articles and book chapters produced over the last half century by a plethora of excellent scholars. The work of the course will consist of class discussions, a few short papers, and a term paper with oral presentations of them to the class.

title: Military History of NATO
description: This colloquium will trace the development of the Atlantic Alliance of 1949 into a military organization under United States leadership. The impact of particular events from the Korean War in 1950 through the Berlin and Cuban crises of 1948-1962 to the INF agreement of 1987 and the subsequent implosion of the Soviet Union will be examined. Special attention will be paid to the differing views on nuclear weaponry between the United States and its European allies. The course will conclude with an evaluation of NATO's status in 2008 and of its potential roles in the immediate future.

title: Topics in US History (see descriptions)
description: This number will be used for seminars devoted to specific subjects in the area of American history. Students may take more than one of the courses offered under this number. Each course will be announced in the course schedule and receive its own sub-title. In Spring 2009 the following two courses are offered (they will receive different section numbers): Nature's Nation: Americans and the environment in the twentieth century (taught by Professor Kevin Powers), and Prophecy in American history (taught by Professor Matthew Bowman). Nature's Nation: Americans & The Environment in the 20th century: This course will examine the evolving reciprocal relationship between Americans and nature during the twentieth century. We will proceed chronologically, beginning with the conservation, preservation, and urban reform movements at the turn of the last century and concluding with contemporary debates concerning climate change. Among the topics to be discussed are urbanization and suburbanization; industrialization and de-industrialization; roads and automobiles; the manipulation of rivers for the purposes of flood control, hydroelectricity, and irrigation; energy; the industrialization of food and agriculture; the impact of military mobilization on the American environment; the emergence of the environmental movement and the science of ecology; and the contemporary political backlash against environmental regulations. Prophecy in American History: Though it is among the most dramatic of religious phenomena, prophecy bridges the secular and the sacred. The prophet challenges an existing social order with the word of God; he or she calls a people to repentance for sin individual and collective, and frequently envisions a new utopian society based upon religious principle. This course will investigate the careers and thought of several American prophets, from Anne Hutchinson to Malcolm X to Tim LaHaye. Some drew upon visionary experience; some, upon moral passion inspired by religion. All, however, offered a criticism of American life and a sometimes radical alternative that they believed coincided with Godâs will. This course will will encourage students to think about the social and cultural tensions which prompted the rise of prophets, the ways in which their religious language was tied to developments in American religious, political, and social history, and to evaluate the successes and failures of the solutions they offered.

title: What Is an American? Cultural Identity in the United States
description: This course will explore the evolving question of what constitutes American identity from the time of the American Revolution to the present. The focus will be the history of community, group experiences, major American autobiographies, and the continuing shifts and changes in how Americans defined and redefined their understanding of the nation. The American Revolution, slavery, white-Indian relations, Civil War, industrialization, gender and race, Civil Rights and the "cultural wars" of the late twentieth century will be examined in depth.

title: American cultural history
description: This course will introduce students to major topics in American cultural history from the late nineteenth to the twentieth century. We will examine both elite and popular cultural forms: the writings of well-known intellectuals and the leisure pursuits of the American masses. Major themes will include the relationship between high and popular culture and the role of culture in shaping America's relationship to the world. Throughout the course we will pay attention to the ways in which different groups have influenced American culture and how ideas and experiences have differed across lines of class, race, gender and ethnicity. In addition to primary and secondary historical readings, course material will incorporate literature, films and visual material. Students will learn how to analyze cultural sources and how to think critically about culture and its relationship to specific historical contexts.

title: Religion in early America
description: This course explores the religious beliefs, practices, and world views of Americans between initial European-Native American contact and the Civil War. Through the personal experiences of a confessed West Indian witch, an evangelical businessman, a Roman Catholic saint, a Muslim slave, and many others, we will uncover what religion has meant to Americans. Particular attention will be paid to the experience of race and gender, to the centrality of material and print culture to the practice of faith, to how religious pluralism has shaped society, and to the enduring question of why religion has flourished in America in ways that it has not in Europe.

title: American Foreign Policy in the Age of Jefferson
description: This colloquium will center on the problems of American diplomacy from the American Revolution to the Monroe Doctrine. The primary theme will be the efforts of the new republic to free itself from the political control of France and the economic control of Britain. The impact of the Wars of the French Revolution and Napoleon on American foreign policy will be the major issue from 1793 to 1814. The successful disentanglement from Europe after 1814 will be contrasted with the entanglements of the Confederation.

title: War and environment in the modern world
description: This course investigates the role of modern warfare in environmental change, as well as the natural environmentâs role in shaping military conflict. Readings and discussions focus on the following questions: How has demand for resources emerged as a cause and consequence of military conflict? What has been the role of the natural landscape in shaping military strategy? How have wartime considerations led to changes in environmental policy and management? How has war affected peopleâs conceptions of the environment? How have ideas about nature factored into wartime propaganda and the representation of enemies as the âOther.â The class examines these issues from an international perspective, analyzing the environmental dimensions of military conflicts in Africa, the Americas, Asia, and the Middle East from the nineteenth century to the present.

title: Senior Honors Seminar in History
description: HIST 408-409 form a two-semester study of History as an intellectual discipline. Enrollment is by invitation of the Department. Fall: Readings and discussions with departmental faculty on the various methods, concepts, and philosophies of history, and the development of a research prospectus with a faculty mentor. Spring: Research seminar under the guidance of the mentor and Seminar Director. (Enrollment only by permission of the Director of Undergraduate Studies). Students must commit themselves for the full two semesters.

title: Senior Honors Seminar in History
description: HIST 408-409 form a two-semester study of History as an intellectual discipline. Enrollment is by invitation of the Department. Fall: Readings and discussions with departmental faculty on the various methods, concepts, and philosophies of history, and the development of a research prospectus with a faculty mentor. Spring: Research seminar under the guidance of the mentor and Seminar Director. (Enrollment only by permission of the Director of Undergraduate Studies). Students must commit themselves for the full two semesters.

title: Indian Ocean in the age of empire
description: This is an introductory course on the history of Indian Ocean during the age of British Empire. The British gave a new meaning to the historical unity of the Ocean, at one level by territorializing its waters, and at another, by integrating it into a global thalassocracy. In order to examine the global dimensions of the Imperial Oceanic history, the course will employ a comparative framework and will historiographically engage with other Oceans and Seas. While the focus is on Britainâs Indian empire, the course will also touch upon the histories of other countries which constituted the Indian Ocean world. In doing so the course will examine the movements and migrations of peoples, ideas, objects, cultures, diseases, businesses, trade exchanges, and technologies across the Ocean.

title: The ends of war: Japan after 1945
description: On August 15, 1945, Japan's emperor called on the Japanese to "endure the undendurable" and surrender to the Allied Powers. Hostilities officially ceased and the "postwar" began. For some, however, the emergence from war would take years: families had to find each other and cities had to be rebuilt. Soldiers and refugees from the former empire were still trickling home in the late 1950s; A-bomb radiation sickness emerged over a period of years; the US retained control of Okinawa until 1972. Using films, literature, media reports, and a range of historical studies, this class will explore the ways in which the war lived on as direct experience even as it became the subject of memory and history. We will consider when and how the war receded in cities and in the countryside; for soldiers and for widows; in Okinawa and on the mainland; etc., and what these distinctions have meant for the national experience of the postwar. There are no prerequisites for the class. Background information and readings will be provided for students who are new to the study of Japanese history. Requirements: participation in class discussion; oral presentations; analytical essays; and a research paper. There will be no exams.

title: Chinese environmental history
description: The human toll, political challenges, and global impact created by environmental degradation in contemporary China have attracted widespread attention. But because most commentators link Chinaâs environmental problems to the countryâs post-1978 economic growth, they tend to overlook the historical roots of many of these ecological problems. To gain a fuller understanding of the current environmental dilemmas facing China and the world, this seminar will investigate key topics in the environmental history of China over the last three thousand years.

title: The Byzantine empire
description: This course will present the Byzantine or East Roman empire from 602-1204 AD, giving equal attention to internal political and religious history, in an effort to understand how this state overcame numerous crises to last from Antiquity to the dawn of the modern age. It will give much attention to the pivotal role of Byzantium between the Islamic world, the kingdoms of Western Europe, Bulgaria, Russia, the Turks and the Crusades. The course will also consider how Byzantium preserved and spread classical culture and Christianity. Lectures will be rare; most sessions will focus on class discussion of primary texts and will require constant and active participation.

title: Food in European History
description: During the Renaissance, the foods and cuisines of Europe bore a strong family resemblance to their counterparts in late Roman antiquity and the Muslim cultures of the medieval world. Cereals dominated the diet and the prevailing culinary style favored complex, spicy sauces. Wine was the universal beverage of choice, distilled spirits were rare, and coffee, tea, and other caffeinated drinks were unknown. Medical advice about diet and health drew on the teachings of Hippocratic medicine (which originated in ancient Greece) as expanded and amended by Roman and Arab physicians. Shortly after 1500, however, this picture began to change, laying the foundations of the dietary and culinary regimes with which we are familiar today. This course will examine the transformation of the habits of cooking, eating, and drinking in Europe between 1500-1900 with an emphasis on France and England. Topics will include: the reception of new foods from the Americas; the growing availability and popularity of sugar and colonial beverages (coffee, tea, and chocolate); the uneven impact of economic growth on the diets of rich and poor Europeans; changing ideas about diet, health, and disease; changing manners and patterns of social behavior associated with food and drink; the rise of the restaurant, and the emergence of modern culinary styles, including national ones.

title: 20th-c Mexico: revolution, reform, migration
description: HIST 450: 20th-century Mexico: Revolution, Reform, and Migration The history of twentieth-century Mexico has been shaped by three major developments: the Revolution of 1910 and its legacy; the subsequent political and social reforms of the revolutionary state; and mass migration to the United States. By exploring these three themes â Revolution, Reform, and Migration â this course will familiarize students with the major historical developments in Mexico during the past century, while introducing a more transnational approach to the study of Mexican history. Beginning with the Revolution of 1910, the course will cover the revolutionary state from 1920-1934; the age of Cardenismo; the PRI and the Mexican âmiracleâ of the 1940s; the crisis of 1968; the âlost decadeâ of the 1980s; the collapse of populism and the rise of the technocrats in the 1990s; and the decline of the PRI and the rise of the PAN over the past decade. As it proceeds chronologically, the course will pay particular attention to the crucial role that migration has played in Mexican politics and society. Throughout the twentieth century, migration to the U.S. has served as a reliable âsafety valveâ that has relieved the state of certain demographic and political pressures; yet, it has also introduced potentially destabilizing social changes. Therefore, readings on Mexican political and social history will be paired with shorter chapters and articles on migration during that time period. Topics will include the refugees and exiles of the Revolution, agrarian reform and the steep rise in migration during the 1920s, the Depression-era repatriations, the bracero programs, migration after NAFTA, and migration in the age of terrorism, narcotrafficking and national security. Class sessions will primarily be comprised of student-led discussions and presentations of the readings; students will be required to write two analytical essays (5-8 pgs.) based on the materials presented in class, as well as a longer research paper (10-15 pgs.) based on primary and secondary sources.

title: Gender and sexuality in Latin America
description: By focusing on the ways in which ideas about gender and sexuality have informed regional societal debates regarding such issues as nation-building; political parties and ideologies; work, slavery, and immigration; economic development; the role of the military in public life; and health, welfare and social assistance, this interdisciplinary course will help students appreciate the ways in which cultural assumptions have underpinned and significantly influenced national discussions about politics, governance, and public policy in Latin America from the late colonial era to the present. Readings, discussions and assignments will focus on Argentina, Brazil, and Mexico.

title: Traveling: Middle East and European encounters
description: In the period from roughly 1750 to 1940, travel between the Middle East and Europe was a primary site of cultural encounter. People travelled for reasons of business, both official and private, health, curiosity, pilgrimage, or simply pleasure, and they observed, fantasized about, reported on, and otherwise interacted with others whom they regarded as different. This course studies two-way travel literature, European writing on travel to the Middle East and Middle Eastern writing on travel to Europe, with an eye to placing this series of encounters in historical context, a context of a shifting balance of power, the development of imperial ambitions and agendas, and the emergence of new kinds of nationalist aspirations. We read a number of travel narratives as journeys that both reflected and shaped relations between the two regions at a personal, perceptual, and cultural level.

title: Islamic law women and gender
description: The course approaches the topic of Islamic law, women, and gender as a study of a multilayered history. It is part the history of doctrinal development, the ways in which Islamic jurists, working with received texts and sophisticated methodologies, formulated rules about women, men, and their relationships. It is part the history of legal institutions and practices, how these rules were understood, implemented, and even modified by a range of legal actors, from individual judges to centralized state powers. It is also part the history of lay members of Muslim communities whose choices of doctrines to follow and legal avenues to pursue allowed the law to develop in rhythm with social needs, just as their legal inquiries and court appearances also served, at times, as contestation of legal discourse on women and gender issues. We look at the historical evolution of basic legal doctrines, the range of legal practices in the Ottoman and modern (19th and 20th centuries) periods, and end with an examination some of the heated legal issue of today.

title: Lincoln
description: In 2009, the United States will commemorate the 200th birthday of Abraham Lincoln, who remains the most written-about figure in United States history. In this class, we will explore why that is, and ask what if anything remains left to say. In the first half of the semester, we will read several recent, important books in order to establish the existing state of the historiographical conversation surrounding Lincoln: what questions have captured historiansâ attention, what have the most recent answers to those questions been, and how and why have both questions and answers changed over time? Each student will also give particular thought to identifying questions that have remained unexplored or have not been answered to his or her own satisfaction, in order to prepare for the second half of the semester in which we turn to primary sources and original research. Each student will pose a manageable but significant research question about some aspect of Lincoln or his era, and then delve into the Lincoln Papers and other primary sources to draft, give a presentation on, and revise a research paper. This class is heavy in both reading and writing, and assumes significant prior knowledge of nineteenth century United States history; in return, it will provide students with the opportunity to engage in the work of a historian.

title: Inventing the Illegal Alien: The immigration crisis in the Progressive Era U.S.
description: This course offers a chance to do original research on the fascinating time period in immigration history from 1880 to 1929. It was during these years that the legal restrictions that ended mass immigration to the United States until the 1950s were put into place. Using both primary and secondary sources, the course will trace the rise of immigration and restrictions placed on it, changing notions of citizenship, and the differentiation by raceâor scientific racismâamong immigrants of Asian, Mexican, and eastern and southern European background. Topics explored will include the relationship between race and citizenship status; the history of the idea of the âillegal alienâ; and the gendered aspects of the immigration experience. Each student will have the opportunity to do original research using both published and archival primary sources. A field trip to the National Archives is part of the course. The first half of the course will focus on common readings to establish themes and background; the second half will be built around developing individual research interests.

title: America in Vietnam
description: This colloquium examines the war in southeast Asia as a problem of American foreign policy, exploring the reasons for US involvement, escalation, fighting of a limited engagement, intervention into Laos and Cambodia as well as the decision to enter into negotiations and sign the Paris Accords in 1973. To understand the pressures on policy makers the course also explores the domestic context within which decisions were made, the ramifications of alliances and the involvement of wider adversary relationships. The course also looks at the Vietnamese dynamic from 1945 to 1975.

title: Life in the early Republic
description: Thomas Jefferson and Andrew Jackson have long symbolized the development of American politics, economics, and culture during the Early Republic, the period between the end of the American Revolution and the advent of the Civil War. Jefferson and Jackson continue to cast a long shadow but historians over the last few decades have turned their attention to the experience of the broad range of Americans â black and white, female and male, immigrant and native born, urban and rural, enslaved and free â who lived through this tumultuous period. Across the landscape of the young United States, economic panics, religious revivals, election days, lyceum lectures, chain gangs, and sewing frolics provided just a few of the many places where men and women negotiated what it meant to be an American. This course asks students to think about this crucial period in American history from a bottom-up as well as a top-down perspective. This course will examine life in the Early Republic from both a local and national perspective. We will begin the semester by visiting Decatur House, one of the few surviving houses in DC from the early nineteenth century, to get a feel for what life looked, felt, and smelled like. There we will meet a cast of characters who represent a cross-section of Americans. Each student will âadoptâ one of these individuals and develop an understanding of life in the Early Republic through the eyes of a War of 1812 military hero, his disgraced widow, a freedom-seeking slave, a Gold rush pioneer, or even the Russian ambassador to the United States. Each week we will look at a different facet of life during this period â the market, labor, slavery, religion, reform, immigration, and so on. We will think about how each of these factors shaped the lives and experiences of different groups of Americans, and the role larger factors such as race, gender, and class played in this process. At least one class will be devoted to learning how to do primary source research on this period. At the end of the semester, we will return once again to the Decatur House to discuss what we have learned about life in the Early Republic.

title: America in the 1930s
description: This course explores one of the most turbulent periods in the history of the United States, the years between the economic panic of 1929 and the beginning of war in 1941. This was a period when American society, culture, politics and economics underwent dramatic transformations that have continued to reverberate and shape political discourses into the 21st century. The Great Depression prompted Americans to question basic assumptions: how society should be structured, the relationship between government and the citizenry, between labor and capitol, and between culture and the people. Vehement debates in the political arena were accompanied by an explosion in cultural production, from literature and art to music and film. We will examine the range of transformations and responses that characterized the 1930s, considering upheavals in American culture, society, politics, and economics. Major themes will include political change, the idea of âAmericanâ culture, modernism, the rise of mass culture, and variations in experience across lines of gender, race, ethnicity and class. Major topics will include the causes of the Depression, the New Deal and the rise of the welfare state, federal cultural projects, the labor movement, and the relationship between culture and politics. During the first part of the course, we will engage the historical literature and develop a strong understanding of the major historical currents of the period. The second part of the semester will be focused on research: students will formulate a research question and work with primary documents available online and in local archives to pursue a topic of their own interest.

title: Washington DC: History and politics
description: In this class we will explore the city where Duke Ellington was born, and where Frederick Douglass died. We will look first at Washington DC, as a city of slaves, and then as a city of freedmen and women and home to the Freedmanâs Bureau. Then we will look at how the city and its African American population developed over the 20th and into the 21st Century. We will explore issues or race, class, sex, the riots of 1919 and 1968, education, gentrification and political activity. We will study the migration of Blacks from the American South, Africa, the Caribbean and Latin America, (some who came to work and others to attend Howard University and other schools) all who brought something special to the make up of Washington, DC. The end results with be a research paper on a topic of your choosing.

title: History Core Colloquium
description: This course is required for all incoming doctoral students in the department. It will (1) provide an introduction to historical method and ways of thinking and (2) demonstrate what is distinctive about the subspecialties of history and what is common to the discipline as a whole. (Fall)

title: Introduction to Global and International History
description: This course is a selective introduction to some of the key issues and main themes in global and international history. Course Goals & Description This course is a selective introduction to some of the key issues and main themes in global and international history. Texts & Readings Jared Diamond, GUNS, GERMS, AND STEEL. Kenneth Pomerantz, THE GREAT DIVERGENCE. David Abernethy, DYNAMICS OF GLOBAL DOMINANCE. Herman M. Schwartz, STATES VS. MARKETS, 2d ed. Paul Kennedy, THE RISE AND FALL OF THE GREAT POWERS. Richard Overy, WHY THE ALLIES WON. Melvyn P. Leffler and David S. Painter, eds., ORIGINS OF THE COLD WAR, 2d ed. Eric Hobsbawm, THE AGE OF EXTREMES. John McNeill, SOMETHING NEW UNDER THE SUN. Fiona Venn, THE OIL CRISIS. Geoffrey Barraclough, AN INTRODUCTION TO CONTEMPORARY HISTORY. (This book is out of print, but inexpensive used copies are widely available.) Assignments & Expectations of Students Course requirements include regular attendance and participation in discussion; an oral briefing; and four or five essays. What Students Should Know This course is intended for history graduate students. It will be open to graduate students from other fields and to advanced (second semester seniors) undergraduates by permission of the instructor and on a space available basis.

title: Introduction to Comparative History
description: Explores key historical developments across the world since about 1500 through comparative perspectives. Emphases include the rise and fall of empires, revolutions, industrialization, state making, and nationalism.

title: Asia and Europe, 1500-1800
description: TBA

title: The Transatlantic Community: Europe & U.S.
description: The subject of this course is the history of the transatlantic community referring to the creation of a network of interdependent relations between Europeans and American from the First World War to German Reunification. This course aims at understanding the relationship by looking to its past, to its formation and evolution, to the problems and issues that have marked it, and to long-term trends. The principal questions we will investigate are: how and why the Atlantic was transcended and a partnership or bloc formed? The relationship will be addressed from both American and European perspectives. In particular the course will examine: political-military-security ties; economic and financial relations; cultural exchange including Americanization; the role of the US in the making of the European Union; and the quest among Europeans for independence within and outside the bloc. The design of this course is less a systematic narrative of US/European relations than it is an examination of some of the critical encounters that reveal the dynamics, evolution and the nature of the relationship. There will be two assigned papers. The first, or short paper (5 pages) on a topic assigned by the instructor. The second, or long paper (15 to 20 pages) will be written on a subject selected by the student in consultation with the instructor. For those interested in history, the most likely approach will be to write a historiographical essay; for those more inclined to a contemporary perspective, the paper will probably be on âhistory as prologueâ or how history illuminates the present. Students will also be asked to lead one of the class discussions.

title: Americanization of Europe
description: This colloquium will be a historical inquiry into both the development of an American presence in Europe and the response of Europeans to Americanization during the 20th century. The emphasis is on consumer society and mass culture rather than politics. We shall address questions like: What is the explanation for the seemingly irresistible power of Americanization? What was/is the appeal of Hollywood movies, jazz, McDonaldâs and Disney? How has Americanization contributed to anti-Americanism?âa topic of some relevance in Europe and elsewhere today. How has Americanization affected identities like gender, race, class, and ethnicity? We shall also consider the proposition that Americanization is an illusion. Have Europeans successfully resisted or domesticated what America exported? And how does Americanization relate to modernization? To Globalization? Are either modernization or Globalization superior analytical categories for this process? Most important, what does this transformation mean? Is it something to regret or to applaud? For example should we be concerned about global homogenization via the âAmerican wayâ and the loss of diversity? We shall begin examining these issues historically as a way of understanding their origins, momentum, trajectory, and meaning. Then we shall study the contemporary situation reading about issues like cultural imperialism and anti-Americanism. Some of the authors we shall read will be Josef Joffe, Victoria de Grazia, and Thomas Friedman. Class sessions will be devoted almost entirely to discussion. Students will conduct one session and write two papers, one short and one long (15-20 pages).

title: German and European History
description: History 541 is the foundational history course for graduate students in the interdisciplinary program of the Center for German and European Studies and its affiliated doctoral programs. Focusing on selected historical and historiographical issues of 20th century Europe, the course is designed to convey the major outlines of European history; to cultivate an expertise in handling categories of historical analysis; and to instill an appreciation for the interdisciplinary dimensions of historical scholarship, especially in relation to the other facets of the Centerâs curriculumâcultural theory, economics, international affairs, and comparative politicsâas well as to other social sciences. Key problem clustersâsuch as modernity, environmental history, science policy, economics, national and transnational identity, gender, culture, globalization, and energyâare some of the themes covered in fall 2008. Major frameworks of historical analysisâsuch as grand narrative, comparative history, biography, crisis, periodization, and othersâare critically examined. The readings are designed to raise critical questions about the foundations of historical understanding, interpretation, and writing, especially in relation to other social sciences.

title: Latin American Origins and Transformations
description: This course examines the origins of the nation-state in Latin America and how elites and subalterns participated in the shaping of modern states in the region. It provides a multi-disciplinary perspective on the problem, through readings by historians, political scientists, and anthropologists. Usually taught in conjunction with LASP 501, for Master's-level students of the Latin American Studies program.

title: Arab Intellectuals in the Modern World
description: Selected topics in Arabic intellectual history during the 19th and 20th centuries. The course will look at debates about Arab nationalism, socialism and Islamism as well as a variety of issues such as democracy, social justice, gender, heresy, pluralism and globalization.

title: History of the Arab-Israeli Conflict
description: The history of the Arab-Israeli conflict from the Palestinians' first encounter with Zionism through the creation of Israel to the "peace process" of the 1990s.

title: Post-Colonial North Africa
description: The course offers advanced readings on the topic of French colonialism in North Africa (Maghrib) in order to acquaint students with the main theoretical frameworks of (post/)colonial studies. It will highlight recent trends in historical scholarship on the Maghrib by addressing such issues as the impact of colonialism on the formation of cultural/national identities; the various modes of interaction between "colonizers" and "colonized;" representation, resistance, and subjectivity; the concepts of "subalternity" and "overlapping histories;" the processes of decolonization; and the construction of (post/)colonial "memories."

title: U.S. Historiography
description: This is a survey of how leading scholars of those lands that became and remain part of the United States have interpreted some of the major periods, issues, and ideas in that history. Every Georgetown historian in the U.S./North American field will teach at least one week in their area of special expertise.

title: Nationalism
description: This colloquium examines selected issues in the history of 19th- and 20th-century European nationalism, combining theoretical readings with case studies. The approach is broadly comparative, and will discuss classic problems such as: what is the nature of the connection between modernization and nationalism? What is the relationship between civic and ethnic conceptions of nationhood? Did East European nationalism develop in a manner that contrasts clearly with West European models, or are these cultural stereotypes completely misleading? How does nationalism evolve from intellectual movement to mass movement, and what impact does this shift have on its ideological content? Although the geographical focus of most assigned readings is on modern Europe, students are encouraged to write their term papers on any region and time period they are interested in, as long as the theme is connected to nationhood and nationalism. The course can thus be counted towards any regional field, depending on the paper topic and subject to the approval of the student's advisor.

title: Ottomans and Russians: Comparisons
description: The aim of the course is to compare and contrast two multi-ethnic land empires that influenced the life of millions in Europe and Asia, by examining their sources and historical literature. Despite the many similarities between these empires and the centrality of mutual interactions and conflicts of Russians and Ottomans over the centuries, the two historiographies have followed divergent paths. By constantly juxtaposing the two literatures and trying to pinpoint common questions regarding economic, administrative, military, and legitimizing systems used in the two empires, we try to understand how they mobilized their resources, expanded and maintained control, and built legitimacy during two distinct periods: in the era of âorganic economyâ and during the industrial age. We also look at crucial historical moments of contact â mutual images in the 15th-18th centuries, modernizing reforms, military conflicts, European diplomacy, and finally, 20th-century revolutions. Knowledge of one relevant language other than English is required.

title: China's Challenge to the U.S.
description: This class examines Chinese-American relations from the late 19th to the early 21st century. It looks at issues of security, politics and economic exchange as well as cultural interaction, and contrasting values and institutions. Students will be asked to examine current developments in historical perspective, coming to appreciate the motives and myths that guide policies. The class will also explore the prognosis for the relationship given the complex legacies of the past that impede mutual understanding.

title: Imperialism & Colonialism in Asia
description: This course will provide a general introduction to the expansion and disintegration of colonial empires in Asia in the 19th and 20th centuries, specifically the British, French, Dutch, Japanese, and American colonial empires in the Pacific. Its purpose is three-fold: first, to introduce students to the multiple trajectories of empire-building and, after the conclusion of WWII, decolonization; second, to examine how the experience of colonialism/imperialism was lived, understood, and written about; and third, to acquaint students with the major works and historiographical issues within the field of empire studies. Readings will be drawn from scholarship in several disciplines, from anti-colonial writings, and from literary genres such as travel writing and novels.

title: Recasting the Raj: History and Historians of Colonial India
description: This is an introductory graduate course on the history of South Asia. Retelling and writing the âpastâ in all human societies is as much a creative exercise as it is a political one. This holds true for South Asia more so because not only the British colonists in the nineteenth century but movements with all sorts of political shades right down to the present day have sought to interpret the subcontinentâs past to suit their own ends. The historians of South Asia have also been as complicit in writing a prejudiced history, which instead of dispelling the stereotyped perceptions have reinforced them. The central aim of this course is to enable the students to engage critically with the historiography of region. The chronological frame of the course spans 1757 to 1947. It will acquaint students with the history of colonialism in South Asia and its historians. It will begin with exploring the British conquest of India, colonial policies and ideologies, followed by an examination of Indian reactions to colonial rule, history of popular rebellions, nationalist politics, and finally the regionâs liberation from colonial rule and its partitioning into India and Pakistan.

title: Collective Identities in 20th C Europe
description: This colloquium will focus on how collective identities like nation, class, gender, race, religion, and ethnicity have defined and motivated Europeans and how these identities have evolved over the century. For example we shall examine: how collective identities functioned as causes or explanations of events, e.g. nationalism and the First World War, or in creating political movements, e.g. social class and socialism and communism. Similarly we shall look at how collective identities competed with or complemented one another, e.g. nation vs. class, or gender vs. class. We conclude with questions about the alleged decline or transformation of traditional loyalties like nation, class, and religion and the rise of commitment to other sub national and transnational identities. âHow different were the identities of Europeans in 1914 from today?â is the grand question we will address. Students will be asked to write one short paper (5 pages) due at mid semester and one longer (12 to 15 pages) paper.

title: Conflict and Reform in the Andes
description: TBA

title: Global Power and Local Culture in Early Latin America
description: TBA

title: Islamic Law, Women, and Gender
description: Islamic Law, Women, and Gender

title: Political History of North Africa
description: The course introduces the students to readings on the political, social, and cultural profiles of modern North Africa (Maghrib) from the establishment of the Ottoman Regency of Algiers in the early sixteenth century until national independence and the post-colonial period. It also examines the current state of North African historiography, and presents an overview of the principal schools of thought on the history of the Maghrib. Topics of study include: the impact of Ottoman European colonialism on North African societies, states, and economies; the social, economic, cultural, and political dimensions of colonial rule and administration; the rise of Maghribi anti-colonial and nationalist voices; the different paths taken by Morocco, Algeria, Tunisia, and Libya towards independence; and the various issues confronting the post-independence state.

title: Nineteenth-Century Imperial Russia
description: Advanced overview of the "long nineteenth century," juxtaposing diverse traditions of interpretation: Imperial Russian historiography, Soviet and American "schools," new trends post-1991.

title: Readings in Atlantic History
description: This course explores the history of the Atlantic world in the early modern period. History 702 offers both a rough chronological survey of some main topics in the field and a sampling of the approaches and methodologies employed by scholars.

title: Comparative Colonialism
description: History 703 will explore European colonialism in Africa (McKittrick's specialization) and the Americas (Games's specialization) with likely detours to Australasia and the Middle East. All students will write a research paper.

title: Environmental History
description: An introduction to the field of environmental history, focusing on the last three centuries.
