The Vietnam War was a major feature of US Cold War strategy, during which time Thailand was â and still is - a staunch US ally. Once ruled by a royal Confucian dynasty, Vietnam now adheres to a one-party political system. Thailand is a constitutional monarchy that has struggled to consolidate its democracy. Buddhism in Vietnam is primarily Mahayana, a legacy of the countryâs long relationship with China, while Thailand favors Theravada Buddhism, which has stronger ties to India. Much Southeast Asian history and many current issues can be explored through a comparative study of these two countries. This course will use historical and contemporary analysis and literature, as well as films and art to explore some of these differences. No prior knowledge of Asia or Southeast Asia is required.

title: Revolutions in Comparative Perspective
description: This course examines the causes, processes, and consequences of revolutions both theoretically and empirically. The first great revolution dates back to 1789 in France, though it was not until the twentieth century when most major revolutionary upheavals occurred in countries as diverse as Mexico, Russia, China, Cuba, Vietnam, Iran, and elsewhere. This course seeks to understand the underlying causes of revolutions, why they form and unfold in particular historical directions, and why they have the consequences they do.

title: Chinese Foreign Policy
description: This course analyzes the evolution foreign policy of the Peoples Republic of China since 1949. It deals with China's objectives, instruments of policy, changing alignments, and growing role in international affairs. Course goals include gaining a thorough understanding of the variables affecting Chinese foreign policy and familiarity with major competing perspectives and issues in analyzing Chinese foreign policy.

title: Somalia to Afghanistan: A New World
description: This seminar examines the evolving use of military power, in conjunction with diplomacy, law enforcement and humanitarian assistance, to resolve complex civil-military challenges to U.S. security. In an era of growing globalization, with the United States facing such transnational threats as terrorism, ethnic violence and the proliferation of mass destruction technologies, the distinction between domestic and international crises is increasingly blurred. This situation raises significant questions about the appropriate use of Armed Forces and Defense resources in responding to these new threats to U.S. security. Degree candidates will have an opportunity to explore the policy dilemmas facing today's national leaders as they make decisions concerning terrorism and homeland defense.

title: Theory and Practice of International Negotiation
description: This course offers a multidisciplinary perspective on international negotiation. It examines the role of negotiation in international affairs, the nature of the negotiating process, and the differences among states in terms of their negotiating style. The goal is to let each student form and test some judgments about how the relationships among the dynamics of the negotiating process, national negotiating styles, and the international system affect the outcome of negotiations.

title: Regional Economies of Asia
description: Coming Soon

title: International Business Ethics
description: This course studies the application of ethics to contemporary issues of international business operating in different economic, political and cultural settings. Drawing on established ethical theory, the course uses normative criteria to evaluate âbest choiceâ options for real-world decisions. Consideration of global economic justice and corporate social responsibility are examined, along with mandatory and voluntary methods of influencing business behavior. Students will examine these issues from the perspective of corporate employees and managers, home and host government officials, and civil society activists.

title: U.S.-Southeast Asian Relations in the Post 9/11 World
description: This seminar course will examine the relationship between the United States and Southeast Asia in the aftermath of the September 11, 2001 terrorist bombings. The United States is paying more attention to this region due to the links between several militant Islamic groups in Southeast Asia and the Al Qaeda network of Osama bin Laden. The course will cover U.S. relations on the political, economic, and socio-cultural levels with the eleven countries of Southeast Asia: Brunei, Burma (Myanmar), Cambodia, East Timor, Indonesia, Laos, Malaysia, the Philippines, Singapore, Thailand, and Vietnam. The United States is especially concerned about Indonesia, the largest Muslim nation in the world.

title: Islam and Politics
description: The course will discuss Islamic notions of politics, government, sovereignty and legitimacy and their varying interpretations throughout history. It will also discuss the more recent emergence of Islam as a political force and the reasons behind it. It will address how Islam has been used both as a factor of legitimating power by governments and as tool to challenge the legitimacy of existing systems. It will use case studies to illustrate these points.

title: African Political Economy
description: Coming Soon

title: Images of Eve, Mary, And Fatima
description: Eve, Mary, and Fatima are the central women of Judaism, Christianity, and Islam. This course explores their definitions, roles, and presentations in visual culture. Feminist scholars argue that the fundamental purpose for the imaging of women is to teach both theological doctrine and appropriate societal roles for women. Following that mode of analysis, images of women in the modes of Eve, Mary, and Fatima will be witnessed through the arts from painting and sculpture to film and television. Lectures and in-class discussions will focus on modules devoted to an in-depth examination of the scriptural texts on the role of the visual modality, a chronological overview of the role of women in each tradition, and an analysis of the core relationship between the image of woman and the religious understanding of woman. Special questions arising from the images and theologies of Eve, Mary, and Fatima will be considered.

title: Egypt 1952-Present: Pol/Society
description: This seminar is an introduction to the politics, culture, society and economy of Egypt covering roughly the period from 1952 to the present. As such, the course takes up an interesting and tumultuous period in Egyptâs recent history; an era of tremendous political, social and economic transformation. In addition to conveying a basic understanding of Egyptian politics under Gamal Abdel Nasser (1952-1970), Anwar Al-Sadat (1970-1981) and Hosni Mubarak (1981-present), the seminar will approach Egyptâs recent history from an interdisciplinary perspective. Along with political history and analysis, we will examine Egyptian literature, film, and music in order to understand Egyptian society more broadly. An attempt will be made to introduce students to some of the major questions, debates, and recurring themes in the study of contemporary Egyptian politics and society.

title: Makers of Modern Asia
description: This course will explore the lives and legacies of the leaders who built East Asiaâs modern nation states. There will be a lot of reading, almost all of it single authored biographies, but also some DVDs and other presentations on the subjects. The course will begin by examining studies of leadership in international relations and also the political culture of leadership styles in different Asian societies. . The course will then look at the political lives of Yoshida Shigeru of Japan, Lee Kuan Yew of Singapore, Deng Xiaoping of China, Ho Chi Minh of Vietnam, Kim Il Sung of North Korea and Kim Dae Jung of South Korea. The latter part of the semester will compare how Asiaâs current generation of leadership stacks up against these historical figures. Some prior course work in East Asian politics or history is strongly recommended for students taking this class. --------------------------------------------------------------------------------

title: Regional Combatant Commands and 21st Century Foreign Policy Execution
description: Coming Soon

title: The Military Instrument of Power: Past, Present, Future
description: Coming Soon

title: Political Legitimacy & Identity in Southeast/East Asia
description: The purpose of this interdisciplinary course is to attempt to explain the complex forces that affect the ability of individuals and institutions in Southeast and East Asia to guide the internal operations of states and interaction with other states in accordance with internal or international norms and expectations. It will analyze the present drawing upon history in the region, and attempt to delineate forces that will affect the future. The course will examine some relevant theoretical literature on legitimacy, varying historical patterns of classical and modern legitimacy in the area, nationalism and legitimacy, questions of identity based on ethnicity/ language/religion related to legitimacy, and then explore how states/individuals have used these concepts for their acceptance and governance. The course will be open to juniors and seniors (sophomores and graduate students with the approval of the professor), and will meet once weekly for approximately two hours. The course will be limited to twenty-five students, and, depending on the number of students, be conducted in the manner of a seminar. Students will be expected to write a term paper on one country or topic approved by the instructor, and make a class presentation on the progress toward that paper. There will be a take-home final examination.

title: The United States and Latin America in the Modern Era
description: Through readings, essay writing, and case studies in the field of Inter-American Diplomacy, this seminar will explore the origins, foundations, and direction of foreign policy goals of the United States and its Latin American neighbors in the Modern Era. In two phases, four major periods - first, the Imperial Era, World War II, the Cold War, and Post Cold War will be covered in assigned readings with short bibliographical essays. In the second phase, case studies on "Polarization in Venezuela: The Battle Between Chavez and the Opposition", "Panama Canal Treaty Negotiations (1977) and "The Central American Arias Peace Plan" (1987) will be studies. In this second phase of the seminar, students will be asked to apply principles guiding the foreign policy interests of the United States and Latin American Republics. Seminar participants will take a position of a diplomat and prepare a memorandum supporting that person's views resolving the case in ONE of the three studies.

title: Islam and Global Terrorism
description: The course will attempt to put September 11 and the current war against global terrorism in perspective. It will survey the rise of militant Islamic groups from North Africa to Southeast Asia, their ideologies, strategies, tactics and track records. Special attention will be given to Osama Bin Laden and the origins and development of al-Qaeda, America's relationship with Islam and Muslim extremist groups and its prosecution of the war against global terrorism, internationally and domestically.

title: China and India as Emerging Global Technological Powers
description: The Emergence of China and India as Technological and Economic Powers looks at the history, and recent performance of these two giant countries. They account for 38% of the world's population. If measured in terms of purchasing power parity, they already are the second and fourth largest economies in the world, although they are still poor developing countries. There are many similarities and differences in their development strategies. Both turned inward and went left in the middle of last century; and adopted Soviet style five year industrialization plans. Both have been opening up to more interaction with the rest of the world, albeit at a different pace and to a different extent. China is becoming the worldâs manufacturing hub; India is becoming its information technology enabled service exporter. China is a one party state, India is a tumultuous democracy. Furthermore they are growing at more than three times the world average, and have critical mass in terms of highly trained people, scientists and engineers, and R&D expenditures. Where are they coming from? Where are they going? What can we learn about development strategy from their experience? How sustainable are their strategies? The course looks on the impact they are already having on other developing countries, as well as on developed countries. It also explores what further impact they may have under different scenarios.

title: Language and Nationalism in Europe
description: Since the emergence of nationalism, language has played a substantial role in nation building processes, sometimes at the cost of minority languages. Languages have also helped to advance the political demands of peripheral nationalist movements. In this seminar, we are going to study the interplay of language and national identity. We will explore the language ideologies (coming from both the rationalist and the romantic political tradition), the role of languages as identity carriers and political tools, as well as language planning policies in some European countries. We will also devote some time to study the language challenge within the European Union itself, a political entity with 27 member states, and 23 official languages

title: China, India, Southeast Asia: New Dynamics
description: Historically, Southeast Asia has been a cultural and economic crossroads for China and India. However, Western colonization, independence struggles and Cold War alliances severed these traditional connections and, in some cases, created tensions and conflict between Southeast Asian nations and these traditional powers. In the past decade, the region has increasingly returned to this crossroads role, albeit on twenty-first century terms. As relations with China and India become closer and more complicated, Southeast Asia faces a number of balancing acts - between these rising Asian powers and traditional Western security and trade partners, and between China and India itself â as it struggles to maintain new-found autonomy. This course will look directly at political, economic and security dynamics in Southeast Asia, China and India. However, the underlying thrust is comparative, and equal attention will be given to the increasing interaction among these three entities. Finally, the course will explore the implications of these new dynamics for US policy in Southeast Asia as well as Northeast and South Asia.

title: Taiwan in the 21st Century
description: The rise of China, US power and influence in Asia and the Pacific, and Taiwan assertiveness have made Taiwan a focal point of US and international policy concern as the main international hot spot that could lead to great power military conflict involving the United States. This course explains the background, status, and outlook of the China-Taiwan-US triangular relationship. It gives special focus to political, economic, social, and intellectual trends on Taiwan, how they affect Taiwan's standing in the triangular relationship, and what this means for both China and the United States. Class procedures are a combination of lectures, student reports, and class discussion.

title: Balkan Instability: A Case Study in International Peacebuilding
description: Coming Soon

title: Military History of the Modern Middle East
description: The course examines the history of warfare in the Middle East over the past two hundred years, from Napoleon's invasion of Egypt to America's incursion in Iraq. The Battle of Navarino, the Crimean War, Gallipoli, Operation Torch, the civil wars in Yemen and Lebanon, and the Arab-Israeli Warsâare among the dozen decisive engagements investigated. Sources for the course include primary documents, official military histories, and the memoirs of combatants. The course will stress â and students will be asked to identity â the major themes in the military history of the Middle East and the characteristics that distinguish it from that of other regions. Situated at the junction between Africa and Asia, containing strategically-vital waterways and invaluable resources, the Middle East has historically served as an ambit for competing empires. Since 1798, however, the date usually assigned to the advent of the modern age, the Middle East has repeatedly been a battlefield in the conflict between the technologically dynamic West and the region's traditional, pre-industrial states. Whether in direct clashes between Western and Middle Eastern armies, or conducted through local proxies, this confrontation has an immense impact on the political development of the area as well as on the economy and security of the world. The course aims at instilling an in-depth knowledge of that history and an appreciation of its ramifications, past and present. Students will examine the geopolitical circumstances surrounding each campaign, the factors that precipitated the fighting, and its regional and global consequences. Effort will be made to place the war or confrontation within the overall context of Middle Eastern strife and Great Power relations. Students will be required to submit a twenty-page research paper on one battle or war, analyzing its background, conduct, and aftermath.

title: Intro to Arab Society
description: This course introduces students to the study of contemporary Arab society, in both broad conceptual terms as well as details of daily life. After gaining a general background to the history, geography, and demography of the Arab world, students will study cultural trends, political movements, religiosity, family, gender, labor, and reproduction, among other topics. The course will also focus critically on the variety of methods used to study the modern Arab world.

title: Islamic Philosophy and Theology
description: This course is intended to be an introduction to the major issues, figures, and texts of Islamic philosophy and theology. Philosophy and theology represent two main intellectual perspectives in the Islamic tradition and have produced a vast literature. We shall first attempt to answer the question of what Islamic philosophy and theology is and how they figure in the larger context of Islamic religion. In dealing with philosophy, we shall analyze its historical rise and development, the translation movement from the 8th to the 10th century, and its interaction with Greek and Hellenistic traditions of philosophy. While dealing with such towering figures of Islamic philosophy as Kindi, Farabi, Ibn Sina, Ghazali, Ibn Rushd, Ibn Hazm, Ibn Tufayl, Ibn Bajjah, Suhrawardi, the school of Ibn al-Arabi, Nasir al-Din Tusi, and Mulla Sadra, we will also discuss the central issues and concepts of Islamic philosophy, including existence (wujud) and essence (mahiyyah), Godâs existence and knowledge of the world, knowledge (âilm) and its foundations, cosmology, causality (âilliyyah) and its role in sciences of nature, and political thought.

title: Korea: Contempoary Pol/Cul/Society
description: This course will analyze the socio-cultural, economic, and political forces that have influenced contemporary Korean society. The interpenetration of economic and social changes over almost two generations have affected Koreaâs internal transformation and international relations. The growth of civil society has been important in all aspects of contemporary Korea, and Korean cultural heritages continue to have contemporary impacts. Although concentrating on conditions in South Korea, the course will consider relations with North Korea and its internal dynamics, as will South Koreaâs role in Asia and with the United States. A broad range of readings will be assigned.

title: Religion and Modern Politics of Iran
description: In 1979, a broad-based revolution led by the Ayatullah Rouhullah Khomeini toppled the regime of Muhammad Reza Shah Pahlavi thus ending a 2,500-year tradition of monarchy in Iran. The forces that contributed to this momentous event adhered to different political ideologies and belonged to different segments of the Iranian society. They also had widely divergent visions for a post-revolutionary Iran. However, in the end, Islamic forces prevailed and established an Islamic republic, marking the beginning of a fundamental alteration of Iranâs social, political and cultural make-up in the hopes of creating a utopian Islamic society. They also took it upon themselves to fill the role of champions of the rights of Muslims and all other âopppressedâ peoples, at a great cost to Iran. The remnants of this mindset continue to influence Iranâs external behavior and the character of its relations with other major powers. In the nearly three decades since the revolution, the Islamic republic has had to abandon many of its earlier objectives because of internal opposition and external events, notably the eight year war with Iraq in the eighties and pressures exerted by the great powers. Iranian nationalism and culture have proven stronger than the regime had anticipated after the revolution, while the disappointment with the performance of the Islamic regime has undermined the regimeâs ideology and to some extent the position of Islam. In a more positive vein, it has given rise to a new Islamic reformist discourse with a potential for reconciling Iraniansâ desire for modernity with their attachment to their religious and cultural values. Yet, Iran is not the absolute failure and the cultural wasteland at times portrayed by the international press and media. Rather, it is a country full of contradictions and conflicting intellectual activity of all sorts takes place. This course will focus on the following themes: (1) the factors that contributed to the Islamic Revolution in 1979, including the role of Islam; (2) Iranâs evolution in the post-revolutionary era and the factors that influenced it; and (3) an assessment of the likely trends for Iranâs future direction and factors that are likely to affect various alternative scenarios.

title: American Diplomacy, Religion, and the Politics of International Religious Freedom
description: Note: This class will be held at the Berkley Center, 3307 M Street, NW, Suite 200 Religion is assuming an increasingly critical role in international affairs. Although the secularization theory holds that faith will diminish with the advance of modernity, the 21st century is literally brimming with religious ideas, actors and communitiesâfor good and for ill. Religion influences societies and politics from China to Saudi Arabia to Cuba, and transnational trends from terrorism to the spread of democracy. Given the religious nature of the American people, it is perhaps surprising that U.S. diplomacy has had considerable difficulty engaging this new world of faith. At the same time, the United States is the only country in the world with an official policy of advancing international religious freedom (IRF). Could that policy provide a vehicle for U.S. diplomacy to engage religion in the world, or is advancing religious freedom too disruptive? Using IRF policy as the starting point, this course will explore the contradictions and continuities in U.S. foreign policy in a world of faith. It will examine the origins and performance of IRF policy and its prospects for development. Along the way it will explore the attitudes about religion and religious freedom which have helped to mold diplomatic actions. It will ask whether U.S. strategies have reduced religious persecution, or advanced religious freedom, and what impact they have had on terrorism and the spread of democracy in the greater Middle East.

title: Transatlantic Comparison of Migration Debates and Policies
description: This course examines the history as well as contemporary debates on migration and migration policies in Germany and the United States. The readings will reflect not only competing points of view but also highlight the transatlantic differences. The fact that even though the same categories are used in current arguments, they have vastly different analytical meaning and political implications on either side of the Atlantic, will serve as point of departure for class discussions. The course will explore the conceptual issues that lie behind contemporary debates as well as the policy implications of these debates.

title: Islamic Art: Historical Attitudes, Cultural Perceptions
description: Islamic art is a term filled with complexities and multiplicities. Through a series of illustrated lectures and class discussions, this course will review the varied definitions of âIslamic art,â and examine the historical questions of the relationship between Islam and art, especially throughout its history of geographic and cultural expansions. The course will be divided into 3 modules beginning with an examination of traditional texts, then move to an in-depth analysis of the contribution of Oleg Grabar to the study of Islamic art, and finally a consideration of the newer approaches through international exhibitions and publications. An eye will be raised to two critical factors: the cultural significance of religious art in the shaping of western perceptions of Islam, and the question of criteria for categorizing art as either religious, historical, or geographic.

title: Coporate Finance
description: This is an intermediate level corporate finance course, with emphasis on international subject areas topics and with a focus on applying previously learned corporate finance theory in a practical, real-life way. In the first class, we will briefly review financial statements, analysis and reporting. The second Class will cover IRR and NPV, and expose the students to pro-formas and financial modeling. The third and fourth classes will cover the Capital Markets, with an emphasis on real-world fundraising. During the next four classes, we will dive into the technical aspects of international finance, including currency, international risk, and swaps. We will pull the lens back in the following three classes to review how corporate finance is affected by macroeconomics in the international arena. In the last two classes we will look at finance from the entrepreneurâs perspective and explore project finance, banking and borrowing. Our goal is for students to leave the class with perspective, and to understand the practical application of finance for international managers and for entrepreneurs. Case studies will be used to see theory put into practice.

title: Religion and Politics in Turkey
description: This course looks at the interplays between religion and politics from the late Ottoman to the modern Republican period in Turkey. While there has never been a clear-cut separation of religion from politics in the Islamic tradition, the way the two have interacted and influenced each other has changed over the centuries and in different social contexts. As the last seat of the classical caliphate and the first secular Muslim country, Turkey presents a rich laboratory for investigating this dynamic process. The course will start with a background discussion of late Ottoman history and the major intellectual and institutional changes that led reforms in the 19th century. Whether the new Turkish republic was a continuation of Ottoman reforms, known as Tanzimat, or a clear break with the Ottoman tradition will be discussed. The role of traditional scholars, religious figures and Sufis will be discussed within the context of the rise of a new class of intellectuals, activists and political leaders under the influence of such new ideas as the nation-state, nationalism, rationalism, secularism, the French revolution, constitutionalism, and modern science and technology. The modern Republican period will be discussed in four main periods: from the founding of the Republic to the multi-party system (1923-1950), Democrat Party and decades of military coup (1950-1982), the Ozal period and its aftermath (1983-2002), the Justice and Development Party period (2002-present). In this period, we will be looking at the transformation of religious and political ideas, their interactions, conflicts and confrontations within the context of the secular Turkish state. We will discuss the ways in which old traditional religious groups, communities and sufi orders have adopted to the new circumstances of modernity.

title: Problems in Multilateral Diplomacy
description: A series of case studies illustrative of the most serious problems in modern multilateral diplomacy. Cases will include intervention in intrastate conflict; UN Security Council reorganization; arms control; trade and environmental policy; and human rights.

title: International Financial Markets
description: This course provides an introduction to modern finance theory and its application to international financial markets. The course examines the foreign exchange market and related markets in derivatives such as foreign exchange futures, currency and interest rate swaps, and currency options, the eurocurrency market, international bond markets, and cross-border flow of equity investment. The course, which is open both to undergraduates and masters students, emphasizes computer and quantitative skills that are essential to understanding the operations of modern financial markets.

title: Immigration Research Seminar
description: This seminar course will offer you the opportunity to: 1) complete a deep analysis of some topic related to the intersection of science and policy appropriate to STIA; 2) write a thesis; and 3) integrate your work over your undergraduate experience. You will be working independently and will be expected to meet every deadline and present as scheduled during class time.

title: History of Peacmaking in the Middle East
description: This course will deal with the history of the Arab-Israeli conflict and the efforts to resolve it. It will explore why each side tends to see the world the way it does, and why mythologies have taken hold of all sides and made reality hard to grasp. We will examine narratives of the Israelis, the Palestinians, and the Arabs more generally. Mindsets must be understood in any negotiation, and we will look at what shaped each sideâs approach to the conflict historically as well as its approach to conflict resolution over the periods of the most intensive diplomacy. We will analyze how close the efforts in the year 2000 came to ending the conflict between Israelis and Palestinians and Israelis and Syrian, and will discuss what lessons must be learned from the past in order to shape a different future.

title: Muslim-Christian Relations in World History
description: This course examines the nature of Muslim-Christian relations in the different contexts and eras of world history. Particular case studies ranging from medieval debates and the Crusades to modern experiences in Bosnia and in globalization will be discussed. The course concentrates on historical and societal dimensions rather than theology.

title: Jewish Civilization Senior Colloquium
description: Individual directed research with a faculty member working in an area relevent to student's research interests in Jewish Civilization. The semester's work will culminate in a spring colloquium where students will present condensed oral versions of their 30-40 page senior thesis. PJC certificate/minor students only.

title: African Development
description: The course will survey the development of the Sub-Saharan African economies since independence. The focus will be on economic development, but aims to place the subject in its broader political, social and cultural context. The approach will be historical: starting with a brief review of the colonial inheritance, then covering the largely optimistic period to the early 1970s, moving on to the problems and stagnation of the 1980s, followed by the slow recovery of the 1990s which has accelerated in the first half of this decade. The survey will conclude by looking at where Africa is today and the prospects going forward. The course will explore key issues and themes that marked each period and try to piece together an overall story concerning where African economies have been and where they are going.

title: Modern France, French Jews, and the State of Isreal
description: France has played a pivotal and paradoxical role in the modern history of European Jews. The French Revolution emancipated French Jews; the Napoleonic wars began the process of Jewish emancipation throughout Europe. By the end of the 19th century, French Jews played a disproportionate role in French economics, politics, culture and the State. Yet France was also a locus classicus in the rise of anti-Semitism, which emerged as a powerful political force during the Dreyfus Affair. Vichyâs persecution of Jews during World War II was the culmination of home grown French anti-Semitism, not Nazi pressure. Recent years have witnessed the rise of a new anti-Semitism in France. French foreign policy has also has demonstrated ambivalence towards the Jewish Question and Israel. French diplomacy played a distinctly anti-Jewish role during the Damascus Affair of 1840. After World War II, France was successively a strong ally and an acerbic critic of Israel. This course will explore two major themes: the relationship of Jews to French society and French foreign policy towards the Jewish Question and Israel in order to disentangle the complex love-hate relationship between France and the Jews.

title: America's National Security Tool Box
description: The primary objective of this seminar is to investigate the tools available to todayâs foreign-policy practitioner. Students will evaluate the strengths and limitations of individual policy instruments in the areas of traditional diplomacy, economic measures, military force, and public diplomacy by looking at them in the context of recent cases. Role-playing scenarios will challenge students to design U.S. policy responses to foreign affairs crises, thereby testing the application of their policy âtoolboxâ in a complex, dynamic, inter-agency environment.

title: European Studies Colloquium
description: This interdisciplinary reading and research colloquium is intended to serve as the capstone course for senior candidates for the Certificate in European Studies. Particular topics of inquiry will vary according to the interests of both students and instructors; course requirements will typically include a substantial research project(s).

title: Muslim Response to Modernity: In History and Today
description: This course will examine the process through which the Muslim World encountered Modernity and the range of its responses to its challenges to the traditional of fabric of Muslim societies. It will examine the implications for Muslims of the fact that encounter with modernity was often the result of military conquest. It will trace various discourses in the Muslims world regarding relative roles of tradition and modernity , which continue today and efforts of some Muslim intellectuals to develop a culturally relevant definition of modernity for Muslims. It will also draw some comparison between Muslim responses to modernity and those of other non-European and even some late modernizing European countries.

title: Foreign Aid and Development
description: This course is designed to complement courses that explore the history and theory of economic development by focusing critically on the role of foreign aid as an instrument of economic development. The course will examine the purposes of foreign aid, the history and evolving organization of US foreign aid, and the public, private and multilateral institutions that make up the constellation of foreign aid actors. The course will give considerable attention to the current debates about foreign aid effectiveness as well as the changing landscape of development donors, including private foundations, corporations and committed individuals.

title: Topics on Contemporary Turkey
description: This course proposes to examine in depth some of the major debates and issues faced by the citizens of the Turkish Republic at the present time. In doing so this course will briefly examine the origins of the modern Turkish State with a focus on how the founding realities and myths have aided or hindered contemporary Turkish society. This course will give particular emphasis to the interplay of domestic and international agendas in the larger framework of the current Turkish debates on such topics as accession negotiations to join the European Union, the PKK, civil society and the rights of women and ethnic minorities.

title: I Lost it at a Turkish Movie
description: Contemporary Turkey is a nation of 72 million people located at the crossroads of the modern world. Largely Muslim but offically secular, Turkey and Turkish culture has a rich and dynamic history. It also has one of the most creative cinemas. Turkish films, during the past decade have consistantly taken top prizes in Europeean, Asian and american film festivals. This course examines both Turkey and Turkish cinema. I examines both the art and what we can come to understand of Turkey and its culture through the constructed lens of film.

title: Burma/Myanmar as Nexus: Regional Dilemmas
description: This course will examine the questions of regional strategic concerns and foreign policy responses toward a multi-ethnic state, riven by internal ethnic, political, and economic tensions, while pivotal in China, India, and ASEAN relations. International and internal conceptions of legitimacy and problems of human rights, often highly polarized, will be considered within the context of contemporary Burmese history and its relations within the China, South Asia, and Southeast Asia regions. United States policies will be analyzed. Too often ignored, Burma/Myanmar is an important case study illustrative of the effects of colonial policy, ethnic diversity, periods of incipient pluralism, political repression, economic autarchy, and militarism. Although unique, its dilemmas represent case studies more broadly applicable. Guest speakers of varying views will be invited.

title: Religion and Global Development
description: This graduate seminar will explore both theoretical and practical aspects of links among religious and development institutions in approaching policies and programs aimed at social and economic development. It will begin with practical exploration of current debates and partnerships for a list of priority issues, notably health, education, HIV/AIDS, conflict resolution, and environment. This will reflect both exploration of "state of the art" thinking and experience and work with specific case studies. The institutional picture of how institutions operate, approach issues, and interact will be explored. The seminar will also explore the evolution of thinking within both faith and development institutions and in the major global ecumenical and interfaith institutions about poverty and equity and their links with changes brought by globalization. The seminar will involve both active discussion and team projects and an indepth individual research paper.

title: Maritime Southeast Asia- From Colonies to Democracies
description: This course is focused on the recent history, current politics, and future challenges of the five states that make up what could be referred to as Maritime Southeast Asia (Philippines, Indonesia, Malaysia, Brunei, Singapore). Although due to political groupings, people tend to view the 10 nations of ASEAN as being one region, this is a recent phenomenon, and in many cases, the peninsular and island nations of this area have little in common with their neighbors on the mainland of Asia. Starting with an initial focus on the underlying cultures in this diverse region, along with the colonial heritage of these nations, the course will then explore in greater detail the domestic politics, international relations, and ethnic, economics and environmental issues facing these nations. The final sessions will be devoted to looking at the likely future issues that will concern these nations, from dealing with the rise of China and climate change, to terrorism and regional identity.

title: European Growth in the Knowledge Economy
description: The course is focused on the Lisbon strategy aimed at enhancing the growth prospects of the European Union. After illustrating the economic and policy backgrounds of this policy agenda, the course analyses whether and how the different countries and regions of the EU are achieving the Lisbon goals. In doing so, particular attention will be devoted to knowledge (R&D, patenting and innovation) and higher education and the need of raising both their level and growth-enhancing potential in terms of per capita GDP and productivity. The expected outcome is that of providing the students with a comprehensive and updated picture of the challenges still faced by the EU to become a knowledge-based economy. By consulting policy reports and statistical sources, they should be able to assess, in a comparative perspective, the economic and knowledge performance of different countries and regions.

title: Explorers, Warriors, and Statesmen I
description: This colloquium explores watershed events in world history through the prism of actions taken by historic world figures. It examines the historical context in which determined individuals moved to center stage and changed the course of history. It studies what these individuals did, how they did it and the world transforming impact of their actions. It aims to stimulate reflection on the character and legacies of individuals who stood in the center of watersheds and fundamentally changed the course of history. The historical events and world figures chosen for study are among those decisively involved in the shift, over the last millennium, of the world's main axis of commerce and communication from East to West. The first semester covers roughly 900 years from the Viking voyages to North America to the failed French effort to build the Panama Canal. Historic figures studied include: Leif Eiriksson, Mehmet the Conquerer, Christopher Columbus, John Cabot, Vasco da Gama, Vasco Nunez de Balboa, Ferdinand Magellan, Queen Elizabeth I, James Cook, Thomas Jefferson, Merriwether Lewis and Ferdinand de Lesseps. The modus operandi of the course involves presentations by the professor and the students and discussion based on readings of historical treatises and biographies and the viewing of relevant audio-visual material. Several short reflection papers and a final mini-biography are required. This is a two semester course. The semesters can be taken separately or seriatim.

title: Religious Sources of American Foreign Policy
description: U.S. foreign policy has always been influenced by domestic religious traditions and religiously informed purposes. Since the early days of the Republic faith-based groups have lobbied for or against particular policies, and American exceptionalism has been energized by a religious sense of mission. During the late 20th century the âfourth great awakeningâ intensified the involvement of some religious groups in foreign affairs, especially in advocacy for human rights and religious freedom. More broadly, evangelical Protestants, Catholics, Jewish supporters of Israel and, particularly after 9/11, American Muslims, have had a significant impact on our foreign policy. Abroad, religious actors and communities have become major players on the international stage, with enormous implications for American national interests. This course will examine the impact of religion on U.S. foreign policy and its salience in the contemporary international order. It will begin with an historical survey of religion in U.S. foreign affairs. It will then examine contemporary religious movements and their influence on foreign policy, and conclude by exploring the effects of religion on the major international issues of the day, including terrorism, war, economic development and the spread of democracy.

title: Economic Devt in SE Asia
description: The goal of the course is to create and enhance a capacity for understanding contemporary development issues and debates in Southeast Asia. Long-run and current economic issues in Cambodia, Indonesia, Lao PDR, Malaysia, the Philippines, Thailand, and Vietnam will be examined; Brunei, East Timor, Myanmar, and Singapore will receive secondary attention. Lectures will cover the economic, political, and institutional context, draw on theoretic essentials in the areas of economic growth, international trade, and human capital, as well as confront national statistics and firm-level data. Students will acquire comprehensive knowledge of the economic experience in Southeast Asia, and develop an analytical framework to understand the determinants of competitiveness and growth in the region. Current debates on growth, distribution, and globalization will be addressed by utilizing the tools of economics and interpreting data in order to understand and make judgments about alternative development paths in Southeast Asia. To do this well requires a degree of formal economic analysis as well as carefulscrutiny of the empirical evidence. These are the kinds of exercises the course aims to make possible for students.

title: Assessing U.S.-Japan Relations
description: This course draws on a number of faculty members and guest lecturers to cover commercial, political, and socio-economic aspects of this important bilateral relationship. Business executives from the Japan Commerce Association in Washington participate in some class sessions, including several case studies on corporate alliances. Topics include evolving commercial strategies; cooperative business alliance practices, domestic processes and pressures on trade and investment issues; comparative business-government relations; national economic policies and prospects; U.S.-Japanese relations in a regional and multilateral context; and the framework for social and cultural understanding.

title: International Business and Governments
description: The course focuses on the attempt by developing countries to integrate into the world economy, taking into consideration both economic and non-market factors that influence firm behavior in the developing world. In particular, we will look at specific economic, legal and political factors that form the basis for institutional differences between developing and developed nations and play a critical role in shaping the business environment in these economies.

title: WTO Dispute Settlement
description: The multilateral trading system is widely argued to be more ârules-basedâ than ever before. Dispute settlement under the World Trade Organization (WTO), in particular, is increasingly being called upon to adjudicate rights and obligations in international commerce. These decisions bear directly on business opportunities, both nationally and internationally. Indeed, not only do these decisions influence specific industries and trade-related measures, but the breadth and depth of âglobalizationâ more generally. This course is about WTO dispute settlement. The course begins by examining theories of political economy, setting the stage for an in-depth look at international trade law and WTO dispute settlement. Readingsâof which there are manyâare drawn from these disciplines.

title: Topics in IBD: International  Business and Governments
description: This course focuses on the attempt by developing countries to integrate into the world economy, taking into consideration both economic and non-market factors that influence firm behavior in the developing world. In particular, we look at specific economic, legal and political factors that form the basis for institutional differences between developing and developed nations and play a critical role in shaping the business environment in these economies.

title: Topics: IBD, Business, Government and the Global Economy
description: This course offers an introduction to the political economy of international business. In todayâs global economy, firms are as much in need of non-market strategies for dealing with governments and non-governmental organizations as they are in need of market strategies for competing with other firms. The aim of this course is to examine how these strategies interact in a marketplace that is increasingly shaped by the âvisible handâ of governments and international institutions. In particular, the course surveys issues from the prevalence of so-called âborder effectsâ (i.e., the way political boundaries impede the flow of commerce) to obligations under the World Trade Organization and the growing number of regional trade agreements (i.e., the North American Free Trade Agreement), and from labor and environmental standards to the strength of intellectual property rights.

title: The War of Ideas Seminar: Implications of Policy and Public Diplomacy in the Long War
description: The National Security Strategy and National Defense Strategy assess that the United States is engaged in what has been termed "The Long War"âwhat is foreseen as a generational struggle against international terrorism. A key component of this struggle is countering the influence of ideological extremism that inspires terrorism. This course will provide students an opportunity to think through the competing doctrines espoused by Western thinkers and radical Islamists. Students will read key documents written by Ali al-Khomeini and Sayid Qutb, al-Qaâida ideologues such as Usama Bin Laden, Ayman al-Zawahiri, in addition to democratic thinkers that have inspired US policy thus far to include Natan Sharanski, President Bush, and Condoleezza Rice. Seminar participants will come to appreciate the intersection of policy and public diplomacy through a series of policy exercises, and will also complete a final paper or project.

title: Europe and Contemporary Middle East
description: In this course, we will discuss how Europe is involved in the contemporary Middle East. The European dimension of the course will be studied through its historical evolution (WWI to present day), explaining todayâs diplomatic and political choices about the Palestinian issue, Lebanon and the Gulf. Particular attention will be paid to Euro-Gulf cooperation, the "Euro-Arab" dialogue, and the evolution of European policy towards the Middle East.

title: Globalization: Challenges for Developed Countries
description: This course develops the theoretical and practical tools needed to participate in the design of public policy, conduct business-government relations, and formulate some aspects of corporate strategy in the industrial economies of Europe, Asia and North America. The course focuses on policies to deal with the dilemmas of globalization, and to strengthen the competitiveness of firms, workers, and nations; strategic trade theory and contemporary trade policy; forward pricing, fair pricing, and dumping; outward investment and the A Great Sucking Sound; inward investment and the Who-Is-Us? Debate; critical technology development; foreign acquisitions and national security; and policies to enhance the returns to workers and slow the growing disparity in income. While concentrating on contemporary problems of public policy, business-government relations, and corporate strategy in the United States, the course contrasts dilemmas in North America with the corresponding experience in Europe, Japan, and to a certain extent China. (This course replaces Political Economy of Competitiveness, and cannot be taken by any student who has enrolled in that prior course.)

title: Orientalism & Terrorism
description: This course examines the connections between the classical and neo-classical discourse of Orientalism (study of the âorientâ) and the contemporary discourse of terrorism as they relate to the Middle East. The course will be divided into three basic, though overlapping, sections: the first deals with the discourse of orientalism, the second with the discourse of terrorism, and the third section deals with empirical challenges to these discourses and to the very premise of the course (i.e., its assumptions, and discourse(s) that produce it). The empirical dimension of the course deals with (a) post-colonial development, (b) the advent of political Islam, and (c) US foreign policy in the Middle East. This course is open to graduate students only. The pre-requisites include a solid knowledge of 20th century Middle Eastern history and politics, including items (a), (b), and (c) above.

title: International Political Risk Analysis
description: This course has three major purposes: first, to introduce the student to the political assessment and management needs of private firms; second, to allow the student to become familiar with a range of conceptual approaches and methods which are used by corporate political analysts to assess political factors and their impact on the firm; and third, to expose the student to the full range of political risk management techniques.

title: European Roots of Turkish Nationalism
description: Focusing on the ethnic cleansing of the Ottoman Muslims in Europe from the eighteenth to early twentieth centuries, this course examines the nationalization of religious identities and the rise of Turkish nationalism in the Ottoman Empire. Special attention will be devoted to the expulsion of the Ottoman Muslims into Turkey, and their gradual amalgamation into the Turkish nation. Students will develop a framework within which to evaluate Turkish nationalism as well as some of the most significant recent and current conflicts in former Ottoman lands: conflict in the Middle East; the Armenian catastrophe; Kurdish nationalism; the Balkan Wars; as well as recent conflicts in Kosovo, Bosnia and Chechnya.

title: Qualitative Research Methods
description: Coming Soon

title: Economy of Energy and Oil
description: This course studies the links between the economy and politics of the major oil producers/exporters with a special focus on the main exporters of the Arab-Persian Gulf: Iraq, Iran, Saudi Arabia, Kuwait, UAE, Qatar. Each of these states is attempting to diversify away for straight production of crude oil and expand in value adding energy-based products like petrochemicals. This transition will be reviewed in some detail, especially the internal and external political consequences of this evolution.

title: Terrorism and Counterrrorism
description: Terrorism has gone from a persistent yet marginal security concern to one of the most important problems of our day. As the September 11, 2001 attacks made clear, terrorists can and will kill thousands to pursue their ends. In addition to threatening the United States, terrorists menace governments worldwide: indeed, there are few if any countries that do not suffer from some form of terrorism. This course examines a wide range of questions in order to provide students with a deeper understanding of the threat of terrorism today. Among the questions it examines are: What is terrorism? How has the threat of terrorism changed over time? What motivates different types of terrorist groups? When does terrorism succeed and fail? How can terrorism best be fought? This course explores the nature of terrorism, the motivations of terrorists, and the tactics that terrorists use. It surveys state-sponsored terrorist groups, as well as several leading past and current radical groups. The final weeks of the course will assess different methods of countering terrorism, ranging from law enforcement to covert action.

title: Project Finance: The Persian Gulf
description: This course would be based on a series of simulations. It would seek to teach the students how projects are actually negotiated in the Gulf, but also worldwide. The course will discuss the various financing techniques involved. However, it will also greatly emphasize how the governments, local investors, foreign investors, technology partners and banks have to interact in order to achieve the establishment of a new project. In fact real project finance is eminently political. It brings out competition between various governments to place their own technologies in the Gulf. It shows how savvy investors can play governments, banks and companies to get the best deal. The simulations will be run along basic scenarios, such as the establishment of a highly profitable, but obscure petrochemical in the Gulf using US technology, local investors, Chinese buyers, and bureaucratic government officials. The class would divided between the various actor and would negotiate the deal around a feasibility study which they would have to write. In my experience, these simulations are extremely well received by the students. I have run them in the US and in Europe with good success. Students discover that they do not have to be finance experts to actually negotiate project finance deals. Thus the simulations benefit political scientists, business majors, journalists and engineers alike.

title: Economic Development in the Global World
description: This course surveys the major theories, concepts, analytical tools and related issues associated with the study of economic development in poor countries with an emphasis on the links with the global economy, particularly through international trade and investment. Related empirical evidence is also examined through the use of examples and case studies. The course starts with the basics: the meaning and measurement of development, the relationship between economic growth and development, and a general introduction to regression analysis, aimed at giving students access to the vast empirical literature that now uses these methods. The course then turns to the major subject areas, including: the sources of economic growth and why growth still eludes so many countries; the linkages between poverty, inequality and economic growth and development; the challenges of macroeconomic stabilization in a globalizing world economy; the evolving role of the state and the management of global public goods; international trade, the WTO and developing countries; foreign direct investment in developing countries; portfolio capital flows, international financial crises and the role of the IMF; the importance of remittances; and the role and effectiveness of foreign aid and debt relief in developing countries. The format of the classes is a mix of lectures and seminar-style discussions. Students are encouraged and expected to actively participate.

title: U.S. Policy Toward European Enlargement
description: Taught by a U.S. Foreign Service Officer, this course will examine U.S. efforts to integrate former Warsaw Pact and other nations on the European periphery into Europe's mainstream. NATOâs enlargement to 26 members and the European Unionâs to 27 have advanced stability, democracy and free-market reform across Central and Eastern Europe. However, the creation of a Europe âwhole, free, and at peaceâ has since entered a new and less certain phase. The Balkans remain fragile, while the integration of Georgia, Ukraine and other countries on Europe's new periphery pose even greater challenges. Russia's reaction is a major concern. Meanwhile, the EU seems gripped by enlargement fatigue as it contemplates the daunting prospect of Turkish accession. This course will challenge students to think through the next phase of NATO and EU enlargement and to formulate policy recommendations for specific countries and sub-regions. Taught from a practitioner's perspective, the focus will be on real-world issues that confront U.S. policy-makers. Close attention will be given to the foreign policy-making process, and to the differing perspectives of the key players. Students will draft briefing memoranda, talking points, and other standards tools of the diplomatic trade, and participate in simulation exercises.

title: Political Change in Asia
description: In contrast to other regions, Asia has the broadest political spectrum in the world. It has established democracies, totalitarian systems on both the right and left ends of the spectrum, and a great deal of variation between these two extremes. Many analysts consider that the 21st century will be âThe Asian Century,â based on economic growth rates, but politics may be a wild card. How do Asiaâs history, culture and economic levels affect its political systems? Which countries are moving toward greater freedom, and which away from it? Is there a genuine âclash of civilizations,â or can the West â the United States in particular â influence the Asian political path? This course will begin by building a fundamental framework and then focus on country case studies: China, Taiwan, South Korea, Vietnam, Thailand, Indonesia and Burma. No prior knowledge of Asia is required.

title: Financing Development in Africa
description: At the end of the 1990s, African economies began to recover after decades of stagnation. Growth has not only spread to more countries but has accelerated since the turn of the century, exceeding 6% in 2007. This is the highest it has been in twenty five years. What has been driving this recovery and growth? What will be required to sustain it? Among other factors, Africa will have to increase the rate of accumulation of capital and improve productivity of existing assets. This will require stepped up financing for development, from both foreign and domestic sources. This course will examine various ways in which Africa could mobilize required financing. Normally people immediately focus on external financing (foreign aid) which will clearly be needed. However, external financing can only be a bridge to self financing in the long run. Therefore, it is important that Africans step up domestic resource mobilization through both public and private sector savings and investment, reduce capital flight and facilitate remittances from the Diaspora. With regard to official foreign aid assistance (ODA), the G8 and other donors have given special attention to the financing needs of sub Saharan Africa in the last decade or so. This began to reverse the declining trend in ODA that characterized the decade of the 1990s. This century has seen G8 promise to double aid to Africa and to provide significant debt relief. These promises have generally not been completely met except for debt relief and humanitarian aid. New emerging donors, especially China and India, have begun to play an important role as providers of aid to the continent since 2000. This has provoked debate about international norms that should guide aid and the extent to which any new lending by these donors could undermine the hard won gains from debt relief of the past decade. How should Africa seek and manage financing from these new sources? Among the new actors in development financing are foundations (i.e. Gates, Clinton, Ford, etc) as well as many international NGOs. Most are active mostly in education, health including epidemics such as HIV/AIDS, Malaria, and TB. Many innovative ways such as UNITAD, IFF for immunization, Red, etc have been put in place and have potential for expansion. Nonetheless, the amounts from these sources will be relatively small and fragmented, requiring coordination and harmonization. However, at the end of the day, private sector investment will drive sustainable growth and wealth creation in Africa. Fist, African countries have to create environments in which domestic capital stays and is invested at home, earning attractive and secure returns. Second, FDI flows to Africa, although low compared to that going to other regions, has been increasing amounting to $50 billion in 2007 as compared to Â¬Â¬Â¬Â¬ $9 billion in 2000. There is therefore scope for further growth provided the investment climate is favorable. Portfolio flows and equity investments are also on the rise and provide support to the development of capital markets and small businesses. The course will explore all the above sources of financing for development in Africa. It will use a seminar format: discussion based on assigned readings, some and occasional outside speakers. Generally, short summaries will be given at the end of each class. Each student will be required to make a short presentation on a selected topic. Students will be graded on class participation (25%) class presentation (20%), one research paper (20%) and a final exam (25%).

title: Transatlantic Relations
description: Transatlantic Relations used to be seen as a partnership based on bargains between the United States and Europe. During the Cold War both sides were bound together by a security partnership, which allowed for consultation and joint decision-making. At the same time, the United States and Europe pursued their own political projects. The US was building a global system of alliances, markets, special relationships and multilateral regimes. The European Project was the unification and integration of Europe. This course will focus on the European Project as a driving force of transatlantic relations. Transatlantic relations after the Cold War were increasingly determined by the European Union's ambition to be an actor on the international scene. From a strategic point of view, transatlantic relations can be seen as a triangle between the United States, France and Germany. The impact of these states on the shaping of transatlantic relations is decisive. This course will concentrate on their mutual relationships.

title: Historical Memory and Transitional Justice: An Introduction
description: Political leaders are not expected to do social science and find historical truth. They invent the past, commemorate it, highlight some historical facts, forget others, and occasionally ask for forgiveness for past deeds. In this course we will investigate historical memory, or the way nations invent and cope with their history. After a brief interlude in moral philosophy, which will help us understand the moral functions of forgiveness, revenge and reconciliation, we will briefly analyze transitional justice and overview the options and trade-offs that new democracies face when confronting the legacies of the past. We will focus on European countries.

title: Europe and Russia in the Post Cold War Era
description: The end of the cold war had raised the expectation that the east-west division in Europe could finally be overcome. Yet relations between Europe and Russia remain difficult. Mis-perceptions prevail on both sides. At the same time the economic and strategic importance Russia has for Europe (and vice versa) is obvious. Russia matters. This course will in its first part try to give a realistic picture of RussiaÂ´s economy, society and polity under the Presidents Gorbachev, Yeltsin, and Putin. It will then turn to RussiaÂ´s policy towards Europe and finally to EuropeÂ´s policy towards Russia. The course will be taught as a seminar with an emphasis on student involvement. Students will be asked to conceive of a Russia policy which could represent a fresh start in relations between the "West" and Russia.

title: International Relations in Europe
description: The revolutions in Eastern Europe, the unification of Germany and the disintegration of the Soviet Union have radically changed the structure of Europe's international relations. This seminar focuses on understanding what distinguishes today's European order from previous orders, how Europe has reached its current position, and what is relevant about the past for understanding Europe's future. The course explores the role of the balance of power in the European state system, the origins and consolidation of the Cold War stalemate, the nature of alliances, the attempts to enhance security through detente, and the debate over why the system collapsed. It also examines the central role of Germany, the content and significance of French exceptionalism, the debate over U.S. hegemony, and the significance of European integration for European interstate relations. Finally, the course surveys the efforts to create a new order based upon common commitment to democratic governance and multilateral institutions, the reconstruction of norms of sovereignty and nonintervention, and the contending theories about the nature of the emerging European state system.

title: Diplomacy and Statecraft
description: Diplomacy and Statecraft is about the successful conduct of Foreign Policy. It is through this lens that the course will look at postwar European politics. Cases of successful foreign policy will be analyzed, among them European Integration, German Ostpolitik and Unification, and The Transatlantic Partnership. What are the essentials of Diplomacy and Statecraft? What roles do personalities, decision-making processes, influence and power play? To what extent do concepts and ideas matter? The course will try to answer these questions from a practitionerâs point of view, with special emphasis on Post War German Foreign Policy.

title: Business and Investment Negotiations in Developing and Restructuring Economies
description: This course will develop the theoretical and practical tools need to participate in the design of public policy and/or corporate strategy in developing countries (as well as East Europe and the former Soviet Union). Through intensive use of case materials and policy analysis in class, the course will cover business-government negotiations in natural resources, manufacturing and agribusiness; effective tax policy, appropriate technology, attracting and harnessing foreign investment; and managing political risk. It will include an overview of development policy; of trade, investment, and the environment; and of labor issues in North-South relations.

title: Organizations and Society in Comparative Perspective
description: Organizations have become major players in contemporary societies. Whereas until quite recently, social life could be conducted with almost no help from formal organizations, it is impossible today to avoid them. We depend on organizations to fulfill any conceivable task, even to make them work efficiently for which we need to set up new organizations. We live in an organizational society, and the social sciences have developed several approaches to understand how organizations work, why they succeed or fail, whether they can change or not, as well as our relations to them as members or clients. In this introductory course we will review and discuss the major sociological approaches to the organizational world.

title: Economic Development
description: This Economic Development course is designed specifically for all SFS graduate students with an interest or specialization in development studies. The course surveys the major development issues and general theories or explanations used to understand a countryâs level of development, with extensive applications and the use of examples and case studies drawn from countries throughout the developing world. The course starts with the basics: the meaning and measurement of development, its relationship to economic growth, and a general introduction to regression analysis, aimed at giving students access to the vast empirical literature that now uses these methods. The course then turns to the major subject areas, including: economic growth, its determinants (policies, human capital, institutions, resources, etc.), âflavors of the month,â and why growth still eludes so many countries; the linkages between poverty, inequality and economic growth and development; the challenges of macroeconomic stabilization; theories of structural change, structural adjustment programs and the political economy of reform; the evolving role of the state and public sector management in developing countries; foreign capital flows, financial crises, and the role of the IMF; trade, development and the WTO and; and the role and effectiveness of foreign aid and debt relief in developing countries. The format of the classes will be a mix of mostly seminar-style discussions and some lectures. Students are encouraged to actively participate.

title: Arab and Middle East International Politics
description: Did the attacks of September 11, 2001 mark a watershed in the international relations of the Middle East, and if so how does the new order differ from the old one? Has the role of the United States in the region fundamentally changed, and if so is it in the direction of expanded hegemony? Or is the US, by accident or design, moving into a state of war with the world of Islam? Or is America actually becoming less influential in the region? Our seminar will also examine a set of enduring issues in Middle East international relations. Is mainstream international relations theory adequate for helping us understand Middle Eastern conflicts? To what extent is the state still the primary unit of analysis? How do shifting identities and conceptions of legitimacy affect regional stability? Can we still speak of an âArab regional systemâ? What is the impact of economic globalization and new information technologies? What are the linkages, if any, between major conflict areas like Palestine/Israel, Iraq, Afghanistan, and transnational âIslamist terrorismâ? As we deal with these issues, students will also be encouraged to assess âthe state of the artâ of the scholarly literature in the field.

title: International Migration and Development
description: This seminar is intended for students interested in preparing a research paper on a topic related to international migration. The course will focus on trends in international migration, including future economic, social, demographic, political, and other factors that will influence population movements; the elements of a normative and legal framework on which coherent migration policies could be based; the relationship between international migration and such issues as economic development, economic growth and competitiveness, poverty alleviation, trade, national and international security, social support systems, human rights, transnational organized crime, and public health; and institutional arrangements that will enhance international cooperation to promote safe and orderly migration.

title: Great Famines, Humanitarian Assistance, and War
description: The course will explore famines: their various definitions, theories of their causes and consequences, how those affected by them cope with them, the stages through which famines pass, and means by which they may be predicted, measured, and assessed. Famines will be analyzed from three interrelated perspectives: as economic events, in their political context, and finally as public health and nutritional crisis. Finally various humanitarian responses to famine and conflict will be examined, the strengths and weaknesses of each, and how they may be pursued in insecure settings. Students will choose individual famines as case studies, write a research paper, and make class presentations on their work.

title: America's National Security Tool Box
description: The primary objective of this seminar is to investigate the tools available to todayâs foreign-policy practitioner. Students will evaluate the strengths and limitations of individual policy instruments in the areas of traditional diplomacy, economic measures, military force, and public diplomacy by looking at them in the context of recent cases. Role-playing scenarios will challenge students to design U.S. policy responses to foreign affairs crises, thereby testing the application of their policy âtoolboxâ in a complex, dynamic, inter-agency environment. Note: Permission needed to attend this course; space is very limited.

title: International Regulatory Politics
description: From climate change to financial accounting standards, regulation has emerged as a leading global public policy issue. While political economists have long examined the politics of trade, only recently has attention shifted to similar questions dealing with international regulation. Because national regulations are often delicate political compromises about sensitive issues such as what food should be eaten or who can work in a factory, finding international agreements is fraught with contestation. This class will develop a set of theoretical lens to examine cooperation and conflict surrounding global market rules. It then applies these theoretic lens to a series of sector issues such as financial services, information technology, and the environment. Students will be required to pick an issue area and write a research paper that contributes to this burgeoning field.

title: Crime, Law, and Politics in Modern Europe
description: This class is designed to introduce graduate students to historical research on crime, criminal justice and the law in modern Europe since about 1800. Drawing on a recent boom in research in this field, the course will pay roughly equal attention to the social history of crime, the legal history of criminal justice, the cultural history of penal practices, the intellectual history of the âpenal sciences,â and the changing political contexts and meanings of crime and criminal justice; it will also introduce students to different theoretical approaches to the subject. The classâs geographic focus will be on Britain, France, Italy and Germany. The topics covered will include: the broader history of deviance and social control; crime and social unrest; crime and gender; criminal justice and the media; the spectrum of penal sanctions; prison reform and legal reform; penal policy and national politics; the discovery of âcriminal classesâ and âborn criminalsâ; the rise of âscientificâ criminology; criminal justice and psychiatry; criminal justice and the welfare state; criminal justice in democracies vs. dictatorships; the place of crime and criminals in the Nazi state; the comparative study of European and American penal policy since 1945.

title: Theory and Policy in Asia
description: This course serves as the gateway requirement for students pursuing the Masters Certificate in Asian Studies at Georgetown University (the course is also open to other graduate students; undergraduates may enroll only with permission of the instructor and should have previous coursework on Asia). The focus is on the dynamics of contemporary international relations in Asia. The course begins with three sessions to set the theoretical, historical and U.S. policy contexts. Subsequent sessions then test these theories, historical lessons and policy debates against specific case studies or âhot spotsâ in the region. These case studies include: the transformation of Japan; the rise of China; the nuclear stand-off on the Korean peninsula; Southeast Asia; India-Pakistan; the emerging institutional architecture of Asia; and transnational security threats.

title: Refugee Law and Policy
description: This course will examine international and domestic refugee law and policy. After looking at the nature of forced migration, the course focuses on the origins of modern refugee law, the institutional framework of refugee protection, and the history of U.S. refugee policy.
