Alcida Rita Ramos, Indigenism: Ethnic Politics in Brazil (Madison, University of Wisconsin Press, 1998). Donna Lee Van Cott, The Friendly Liquidation of the Past: The Politics of Diversity in Latin America (Pittsburgh, University of Pittsburgh Press, 2000). Deborah J. Yashar, Contesting Citizenship in Latin America: The Rise of Indigenous Movements and the Postliberal Challenge (Cambridge, Cambridge University Press, 2005). Kay B. Warren, Indigenous Movements and Their Critics: Pan-Maya Activism in Guatemala (Princeton, Princeton University Press, 1998). Guillermo Bonfil Batalla, Mexico Profundo: Reclaiming a Civilization (Austin, University of Texas Press, 1996) C. Student Assignments and Grades Student grades will be based upon three factors: (a) participation in class discussions and student presentations of readings (20%); (b) a mid-term, take home essay assignment (30%); and, (c) a final paper on racial and ethnic relations and government policies and programs in a specific Latin American or Caribbean country chosen by the student (50%). If students have any questions about the course, they should feel free to contact the instructor at: davissh@georgetown.edu.

title: Political Leadership in Latin America
description: This course attempts to take a systematic look at the question of political leadership in Latin America. Our principal concern will focus on to what extent this factor helps account for democratic performance throughout the region. The emphasis will be on how presidents have shaped their societies and have, in turn, been shaped by them. We will explore a variety of distinctive leadership styles and analyze whether policy agendas have been pursued through a democratic approach, or more authoritarian means. We will examine some specific decisions in both domestic and foreign policy as case studies to highlight presidential behavior and effectiveness. The lens of leadership will help illuminate some of the dilemmas and tensions faced by Latin American countries over recent decades. How presidents have wrestled with the tradeoffs among good, effective performance, adherence to democratic norms, and sustainability of reforms, will get particular attention. The course will carefully consider both the merits and limitations of an approach that emphasizes leadership qualities and styles to the interpretation of political and social change in Latin America. The course will assume a multi-disciplinary perspective and will draw on relevant historical, comparative and theoretical literature, empirical studies of government performance, and biographical material on such contemporary Latin American leaders as Juan Domingo Peron (Argentina), Fidel Castro (Cuba), Augusto Pinochet (Chile), Alberto Fujimori (Peru), Hugo Chavez (Venezuela), and Fernando Henrique Cardoso (Brazil). The performance and prospects of more recent presidents such as Vicente Fox (Mexico), Lula da Silva (Brazil), Evo Morales (Bolivia) and Michele Bachelet (Chile) will also be reviewed.

title: Sustainable Development Trends: Central Amer/Carib
description: The objective of this course is to provide students with an introduction to the evolving issues that underlie sustainable development in Central America and the Caribbean Region. We will explore historical forces that have shaped and continue modeling Central Americaâs modern societies while analyzing the colonial legacy, the politics of race, ethnicity, gender and the environmental challenges in the seven countries of the isthmus and the Caribbean island nations. The course will focus on general trends affecting the region as a whole and will be divided in three main sections: first, the study of Central America through an historical perspective and the examination of political and environmental issues, including regional integration and development; second, we will have an overview of specific country studies and forces that affect environmental sustainability for the Central American nations, aspects of natural resources management, distribution of resources, migration, poverty and sustainability and also the status of the indigenous populations will be analyzed; thirdly, we will study the Caribbean Basin while exploring the logic for studying Central America and the Caribbean together. This session will focus on the Spanish speaking island nations. Finally, an independent module on action research (AR) methodologies will be part of the course, in order to provide students with the research skill and tools to conduct first hand research on topics that interest them in relation to developmental challenges in the region.

title: Economics for Latin American Studies
description: Prof. SÃ¶ren Gigler, b.gigler@lse.ac.uk Office: ICC 484, 687-5948 Office hrs: R 4-6, F 10-12 & by appt This course will attempt to fill a gap that exists for regional studies students who canât get credit for intro econ classes that are taught at an undergrad level, yet need the background acquired in micro, macro, and international economics courses in order to excel in Political Economy, Economics, or Government concentrations in a Latin American Studies masters. It is assumed that some students will have had some exposure to these topics long ago, while others will have avoided them like the plague, yet now, belatedly, realize that they really should understand concepts such as opportunity cost, elasticity of demand, and the like. In addition, they need to be able to look at graphs and tables without fainting in order to better understand the policy dilemmas captured in them. This course will address such issues. As a graduate level course, it will move briskly through a wide range of topics generally covered in a more leisurely albeit complete manner in the freshmen courses of micro and macro, focusing especially on the issues of special interest to Latin American economic policy debates, emphasizing international topics as well as how to find, interpret, and analyze basic economic data. The class will be a lecture class. Students will be expected to complete worksheets/problem sets for almost every module to help them develop, master, and apply key concepts. Unfortunately, I am forced to evaluate students and give grades at the end of the semester. In order to do this, there will be one take-home midterm, two in-class midterms, and a final exam. Grades will be determined by the two exams, one group presentation and problem sets/class participation/improvement ( 30, 30, 30, and 10% respectively â i.e., 30% for the midterm, 30% for the final, and 10% other). TOPICS: 1. Introduction: What is economics? Why do economists disagree? Thinking about markets and exchange on both domestic and international levels. Does the government matter? 2. Useful concepts and tools â opportunity cost, comparative advantage, ceteris paribus, consumer choice, prices as signals, incentives, marginal analysis, elasticity, economic rent, market power, market failure, schedules and curves 3. Interpreting data, presenting data, and economic evidence 4. Supply and demand 5. Trade â why do we trade? Similarities and differences between domestic and international exchange 6. Factor marketsâ inputs and costs, outputs and prices 7. Product markets â pure competition, monopolistic competition, oligopoly, monopoly 8. Public sector economics: externalities, public goods 9. GNP and its components 10. Keynes and Keynesian economics, government spending and taxes, budget deficits (fiscal policy) 11. Inflation and money (monetary policy) 12. International trade and intl finance: exchange rates, international debt READINGS We will be covering an extremely wide range of topics, and your grades (as explained above) will be based on your ability to demonstrate your mastery of them. I have assembled a group of readings that I think will help you achieve this understanding. You should draw on them as needed. You are all graduate students, so I assume that you are motivated to learn and will read. Specific objectives for the different topics are listed in the accompanying âObjectivesâ handout, and serve as a self-assessment tool throughout the semester. 1. Texts: Microeconomics in Context (available in bookstore) and Macroeconomics in Context (on Blackboard). Series written by Neva Goodwin, Julie A. Nelson, Frank Ackerman, and Thomas Weisskopf. The macro textbook draft (like the Micro text) is put out by the Global Development and Environment Institute of Tufts University. These books attempt to highlight the social and environmental issues in economics, and the relevant chapters for the macro text are available on blackboard under Course Documents. 2. Chapters from various other texts will be placed on Blackboard. These are noted below. 3. Every student should read at least one of the following books which explain economics in plain and direct English, written in general by economic journalists. They explain basic concepts in an applied way that I think is easier to grasp than most economic textbooks. I would most highly recommend the first book as it is the best written and most entertaining of the bunch (and also the most recent), but they are all quite good. (a) Tim Harfordâs The Undercover Economist, (b) Charles Wheelanâs Naked Economics: Undressing the Dismal Science, or (c) Frederick S. Weaverâs Economic Literacy: Basic Economics with an Attitude. I will place all three of these on reserve, although they are also readily available in bookstores and on-line. Unfortunately Harfordâs book, as it is so new, is only available in hardcover. You can find rough concordances between chapters from each book and class coverage at the end of the syllabus. 4. Additional readings will be assigned from time to time throughout the semester, I will use email to send messages letting you know, and will also likely bring short articles to class. 5. Additional Reference texts: any standard economics textbook would be a good reference, the library is full of them (most under call number HB 171.5). Examples are Mankiw, Baumol and Blinder, Samuelson, Taylor, etc. I will be drawing on Boyes and Melvinâs Fundamentals of Economics from time to time. There are also several basic texts available on line. The library has Tom Gormanâs The Complete Idiotâs Guide to Economics available electronically. Another one that I will use at times is Roger McCainâs Essential Principles of Economics: A Hypermedia Text (available at âwilliam-king.www.drexel.edu/top/prin/txt/EcoToc.htmlâ ). A comprehensive listing of on-line textbooks is available at www.oswego.edu/~economic/newbooks.htm should you wish to find others. HONOR CODE: While Georgetown undergrads have a formal honor code, for graduate students it is less formal. However, our commitment to academic integrity should not be lower. I fully expect you all to acquaint yourselves with the Honor Code and its commitments, and to live up to them. I encourage you to work in groups as you work through the exercises and grapple with the concepts, some of them difficult, of this class. However, exams (even take home versions) should be completed individually, without consulting other students. And, of course, I expect individual efforts on in class exams.

title: Energy Security: Western Hemisphere
description: The seminar is divided into four interrelated building blocks: (i) World Energy Overview, comparing the Energy Balances of United States, China, Europe, Mexico and Brazil; (ii) the dynamics of the world petroleum market, world regional petroleum balances with Scenarios to 2030; (iii) petroleum balances of the western hemisphere with country and trade blocs analysis with Scenarios to 2030 and (iv) the potential for Hemispheric Energy Cooperation as a means of fostering regional energy security.

title: Comparative Democratization
description: The study of democracy and democratization has a long history in the social sciences. Interest in this topic has expanded dramatically in recent years, because of the spread of the so-called "third wave" of democratization extending from Southern Europe and Latin America to Eastern Europe, Africa and parts of Asia. Thus, to the considerable literature on democratization as a historical process of social change has been added a number of new studies of what could be termed "quick democratization." It is primarily this latter research, and its intersection with historical work, that we will be addressing in this course. The course is guided by three goals. First, to introduce students to the major issues, theories, concepts and arguments in the literature on democratization. Second, to give students a comparative perspective on some of the major cases of recent transitions from authoritarian rule. Third, to engage the current literature on the "quality of democracy," and assess the performance of democracy in a comparative way, that is, looking at new and old democracies together.

description: Seminario exploratorio, con lectura, visionado y discusiÃ³n de un nÃºmero selecto de ficciones hispanoamericanas vinculadas con los dilemas polÃ­ticos y sociales de la regiÃ³n en los Ãºltimos diez aÃ±os. title: Regional Studies Research Methods
description: LASP 603-01: Methods for Regional Studies Research Instructor: Adriana Kocornik-Mina, PhD Phone: 301 495-9799 Email: akocornik@verizon.net (please use email for main communication) Office hours: 11:30am-1:30pm on Thursdays and by appointment Course description: Students are introduced to the basic methods of descriptive analysis, statistical estimation, hypothesis testing, and regression. In addition, the course examines the major techniques of multivariate statistical analysis in the social sciences. Applications and interpretations are stressed over mathematical foundations. Accordingly, students will work with a complex database, and learn to present and write results using an advanced statistical package, Stata. Course topics: Data representations and descriptive statistics Probability distribution and hypothesis testing for means, statistical estimation Linear (single and multiple) regression analysis Hypothesis testing for regression and other diagnostic tools Multivariate statistical analysis Evaluation: The grade will be based on three (3) homework assignments (30%), midterm (20%), final exam (25%) and research project (25%). Required texts: 1. Berman, E.M. (2007), Essential Statistics for Public Managers and Policy Analysts, 2nd edition, Washington, DC: CQ Press. 2. Hamilton, L.C., Statistics with Stata (Updated for Version 9), Brooks/Cole Publishers. The textbook can be supplemented by the following online statistical texts: 1. //davidmlane.com/hyperstat 2. //www.statsoft.com/textbook/stathome.html 3. //www.itl.nist.gov/div898/handbook (more technical) Suggested texts: Carmines and Zeller, Reliability and Validity, Sage Publications Hutcheson and Sonfroniou, The Multivariate Social Scientist, Sage Publications Maruyama, Basics of Structural Equation Modeling, Sage Publications Statistical package: Students can either buy Stata IC 10 or use the computer labs on campus. A license for Stata IC 10 can be acquired through the Universityâs website, which in turn refers students to //www.stata.com/order/new/edu/gradplan.html. Course Schedule: Aug. 28 Introduction. Overview of the course. Types of numerical data and operations that can be applied to each. Berman, Chs. 1â3 Sept. 04 Review of research design, descriptive statistics, and the introduction to data analysis Berman, Chs. 2â3, 6â7 Hamilton, Chs. 1â2 See Project Description below Sept. 11 Stata session Homework 1 distributed Sept. 18 Analysis of nominal data and contingency tables (cross-tabs). Statistical tests and measures of association for contingency tables Berman, Chs. 8â9 Hamilton, Ch. 4 Sept. 25 Uniform/Normal Distributions, Central Limit Theorem, Random Sampling Theory Berman, Ch. 11 Project deadline. See Project Description below Oct. 02 Hypothesis about means (z-score, t-statistic) Berman, Ch. 11 Homework 2 distributed Oct. 09 Least square regression Berman, Ch. 12 Midterm distributed Oct. 16 Least square regression and the diagnostics Berman, Ch. 13 Hamilton, Chs. 6â7 Oct. 23 Multiple regression and the diagnostics Homework 3 distributed Berman, Ch. 13 continued Hamilton, Chs. 6â7 continued Oct. 30 Reliability and validity of social measurement Berman, Ch. 2â3 Suggested: Carmines and Zeller Project deadline. See Project Description below Nov. 06 Principle components, factor analysis, and rotation Berman, Ch. 16 Hamilton, Ch. 12 Suggested: Hutcheson, Ch. 6 Nov. 13 Factor analysis application and factor scaling Hamilton, Ch. 12 continued Suggested: Maruyama, Ch. 7 Nov. 20 Path analysis and structural equations Suggested: Maruyama, Chs. 3â4, 6 Nov. 27 Thanksgiving Recess Dec. 04 Survey of other advanced techniques: Logistic regression and other nonlinear regression; Analysis of variance and covariance Hamilton, Chs. 5, 9â10 Suggested: Hutcheson, Ch. 4 Dec. 11 Project presentations Project reports due Final exam distributed Homework policy: The homework is collected the class following when it is distributed. Late submission will be penalized. Please work individually. Exam policy: Both exams are take-home, open book and open notes. Students are expected to work independently on their exams. The midterm exam is due October 16th. The final exam is due on December 18th. Project Description: Students are asked to conduct a team project, due on Dec 11th. Each team (minimum 2 and maximum 3 students each) is expected to undertake a study involving a statistical analysis of some data set. The only substantive requirement is that your analysis includes at least one example of advanced analysis you have learned in the class. This analysis should demonstrate a sound statistical knowledge of regression (including confidence intervals and significance tests of coefficients). Each team is expected to find a dataset to use for the project (from websites or elsewhere). Each team will submit a written report, which should not exceed 15 pages, inclusive of references, on December 11th. The report should state: (i) motivation of the problem and key question/s, (ii) descriptive analysis of your data, (iii) the model specification for your analysis, and (iv) summary and discussion of your findings. All sources (datasets and materials) used in the analysis should be cited explicitly. Each team is expected to submit both hardcopy and electronic forms (doc format) of the report. On December 11th each team will make a brief (10-15 minutes) presentation to the class. In preparation for this project I ask that you post your answers to the following questions on Blackboard by September 4th: 1) List five problems or challenges in your area of interest that you would like to research or analyze. 2) Provide at least two examples, in your area of interest, of commonly used analysis and data techniques. 3) What data exist in your area of interest that can be used in the analysis of the problems or challenges identified in question 1)? To integrate a team, students should first peruse their peersâ answers to questions 1â3. Team membership should be decided by September 25th: On October 30th each team will submit a one-page summary of their chosen research topic, key question/s, and a description of the data to be used. I will provide examples of projects before this date. .

title: Introduction to Language
description: Every year dozens of Georgetown students enroll in this introductory Linguistics course. In order to give our students a more personal, individualized class experience, we have organized Introduction to Linguistics as a multi-section course; each section has approximately 25 students. This makes the course rather different from many introductory courses with large enrollments, which often have large lectures formats, supplemented with smaller, weekly discussion sections (typically led by a team of graduate students). Each section of LING 001 is taught by a different instructor, but all instructors follow the same basic course policies and a partially shared syllabus. This allows for a good balance of comparability and individuality across the sections. For example, all sections use the same textbooks and teach the same topics in structural linguistics during the first half of the course, but each instructor teaches these topics in his or her own way. All sections have the same number of homework assignments (which make the same contribution toward the course grade), but the assignments vary to match the instruction of each section. All sections have a midterm during the same two week period, as well as either a final exam or a final project at the end of the semester. After the midterm, each sectionâs topics vary to allow each instructor to share his or her individual expertise, areas of research interest, and enthusiasm. All sections share the same evaluation framework. Our instructors are carefully selected, trained and directed. They are dedicated, knowledgeable linguists who are eager to share their knowledge (and sense of wonder) about language with you. The instruction team for the course includes regular faculty, as well as adjuncts and advanced graduate students. Our adjunct faculty have all completed their doctorates in linguistics and have taught this and/or other undergraduate courses before. Our advanced graduate student instructors have served as teaching assistants for this and/or other undergraduate courses before and completed a year-long teaching practicum. One of the regular faculty instructors coordinates the sectionsâ selecting, guiding, and observing the graduate and adjunct instructors. GENERAL OUTLINE AND POLICIES Introduction to Language provides a general introduction to the field of Linguistics, the scientific study of language. The main goals of the course are to begin to think about language analytically, apply methods of linguistic analysis, and become more aware of the important issues related to language and society. Along the way, we will also challenge some commonly held misconceptions about language. Through course readings, lectures, class discussions, exercises, and group projects, the class will address the following questions: * What is language? * How can language be studied systematically? * What are the subsystems of language, and how do they articulate with each other? * How do language use and culture interact? COURSE THEMES 1. Language as System: What do we know? 2. Analyzing Language Systematically: How do we know and how do we learn more? 3. Contextualizing Linguistics: How is what we know relevant? Evidence of cognitive structures and processes Evidence of social structures and processes Equality of languages and value of linguistic/cultural diversity COURSE GOALS 1. To gain a basic understanding of the complexity and underlying systematicity of language. 2. To perform basic analysis of linguistic data. 3. To apply this knowledge to better understand a relevant body of language data. 4. To relate linguistic knowledge and analysis to âreal lifeâ issues

title: Phonetics
description: Phonetics studies the expressions of language, physiological , physical and perceptual. In this course, it will not be assumed that everyone has had some exposure to linguistics. The basic terms and concepts of linguistics will become familiar, and most of them will be re-encountered and reviewed during this course. The emphasis in Phonetics will be on the systematic description of the sounds and processes of speech production, transmission, and perception. This course is a linguist's approach to the study of speech sounds.

title: Phonology
description: An introduction to the description and analysis of sound systems, with applications to data from a variety of languages.

title: Syntax
description: In this introduction to syntactic theory we examine the (mostly unconscious) knowledge that each native speaker has of the structure of his or her language. We work towards a model of such knowledge by analyzing several syntactic structures of English and other languages, focusing on such aspects of syntax as noun phrase structure, finite and non-finite clauses, and question formation. We adopt and refine a formal model of the grammar of languages following the tradition of Generative Grammar, a fruitful line of research that has been the mainstay of much (though not all) work in syntax since the 1950s. The course focuses on enabling students to acquire the methodology and the tools employed by researchers in linguistic theory. Requirements for the course will involve weekly homework assignments plus a midterm and a final.

title: Morphology
description: This course will explore both the unity and diversity of the worldâs languages through morphology (i.e. the study of how words are formed). Taking the perspective of scientists encountering an unfamiliar language for the first time, we will learn to morphologically analyze foreign language data, uncover linguistic patterns, and understand the grammatical relationships that can be encoded cross-linguistically. With this knowledge of word structure as a foundation, we will further investigate how morphological patterns might be explained through linguistic theory and psychology.

title: Semantics and Pragmatics
description: This course introduces linguistic approaches to the study of meaning. We will cover both semantics (the investigation of the literal meaning of language) and pragmatics (that of meaning in context). We also consider the interface between semantics and pragmatics, the relationship of meaning to grammar, and philosophical issues in the study of meaning. The course will be of interest to majors as well as students of philosophy, languages, cognitive science, computer science, and psychology.

title: Language & Religion
description: Are there distinctive forms of language use in the magico-religious domain, and if so, how much do they depart from "ordinary" communication? How do people use language when they wish to communicate with incorporeal agents (spirits, God, etc), for instance, agents who can sensorially say nothing? In addressing these questions, this course examines language practices from across the globe, from Illongot hunting spells in the Philipines to the use of noise in U.S. Pentecostal worship. Drawing on literature in linguistics, anthropology, and religious studies, we consider the special demands that religious beliefs, ideals, and objectives place on language use. At the same time, we consider how the exploration of language use in the magico-religious domain has spurred some researchers to revise their understanding of how language works generally. L anguage-use in divination, for instance, has inspired some to question the centrality of "intentionality" in theories of pragmatic meaning. Religious rituals tend to exploit the poetic function, and this has inspired some researchers to consider the "poetic" and ritual-like character of many forms of discourse, not just the religious. Toward the semester's end, we turn to large-scale sociopolitical issues on the theme of language and religion, such as the way certain nation-states have sought to define citizenship in terms of competence in a sacred language.

title: Language Acquisition
description: Course Goals & Description This course provides an introduction into theories and research which account for first, second and bilingual language acquisition. Students will learn to analyze, critically evaluate, and integrate classic and current work in these areas, and consider to what extent these findings inform current language and education practices and policies.

title: Methods of Language Teaching
description: Study of language learning and teaching from theoretical and practical points of view. Contributions of modern linguistic science to both theory and practice.

title: Language and Medicine
description: Talk about health surrounds us. We canât open a newspaper without seeing some reference to stem cell research, second-hand smoke, autism, or the Supreme Court decision regarding Oregonâs Death with Dignity Act. Reports of bioterrorism and global health scares such as the avian flu and mad cow disease flood the airwaves. In visits with our doctors, we talk our way towards diagnoses that help us understand why we havenât been feeling well and then weigh the pros and cons of different treatment options. My entire life in linguistics has been spent exploring interrelationships between language and a variety of such health issues and contexts, large and small. Iâm interested in how the voices of scientists, health care providers, politicians, attorneys, journalists and patients enter into conversations with each other. My passion for understanding the mutual effects between language and human health â how language use affects health as well as how health affects language - has encouraged me to reach across disciplines, both to the scholarly literature and to practitioners. In this course, weâll draw on a variety of primary sources in our interdisciplinary examination of health communication in public and in private. Weâll view videotapes of physician-patient visits, analyze transcripts of genetic counseling sessions, read widely within the print media, listen to congressional hearings, examine direct-to-consumer pharmaceutical advertising and public health campaigns, and engage with guest speakers from the National Institutes of Health, Georgetown University Medical Center, Johns Hopkins University, The Washington Post, and National Public Radio. This course is designed to appeal especially to students with career aspirations in the following fields: medicine, linguistics, public policy, bioethics, government, and journalism. Specific topics will be selected based on participantsâ areas of interest. Prerequisites: none Readings for the semester Ainsworth-Vaughn, Nancy. 1998. Claiming Power in Doctor-Patient Talk. Oxford: Oxford University Press. Capps, Lisa and Ochs, Elinor. 1995. Constructing Panic: The Discourse of Agoraphobia. Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press. Gwyn, Richard. 2002. Communicating Health and Illness. London: Sage. Heritage, John & Maynard, Douglas. 2006. Communication in Medical Care: Interaction between Primary Care Physicians and Patients. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press. Other articles located on Blackboard or handed out in class

title: Language and Social Life
description: 1. Course Description What is language? How is it related to social life? Over the past half a century, researchers (e.g. sociolinguists, linguistic anthropologists, discourse analysts) have examined myriad ways that language, society, and culture interact on a wide range of scales by employing a variety of research methods. This course introduces students to several key areas of this vast field of inquiry, moving from multilingualism on the macro scale of society to interactional sociolinguistics on the micro scale of interpersonal communication. It also discusses how language is related to aspects of everyday social life, e.g. media, advertising, landscape, family, pop music, food, technology, politics, and social outreach, and encourages students to think how knowledge about language use in society can be applied to understand and solve real-life problems. In addition to learning through reading and discussion, students will have the opportunity to design and implement research projects on topics of their own interests. 2. Course Goals This course will guide students to: * Understand key concepts and theories of sociolinguistics and linguistic anthropology * Discern various perspectives on the connection between language, society and culture, and form their own views * Identify major research methods, and understand why and how they are employed * Design and implement their own research projects * Present findings and analysis effectively in both oral and written communication * Apply sociolinguistics to everyday life. 3. Required Readings * Textbook. Wardhaugh, Ronald. 2006. An Introduction to Sociolinguistics. 5th Edition. Malden, MA: Blackwell. (Available in campus bookstore.) * Course reader. To complement and extend topics and approaches discussed in the textbook, a selection of journal articles and book chapters is also required. (Available on Blackboard; a complete bibliography is on the last page of the syllabus.)

title: American Sign Language
description: This course is an introduction to various topics in American Sign Language (ASL) linguistics. The semester will be divided into three parts. We will begin our investigation by situating ASL within a cultural and historical context that will include; where and how it began, how it came to be viewed as a language, where it is used and by whom. The second part of the course will be an overview of the linguistic structure of ASL to include the traditional analytical levels of phonology, morphology, syntax and semantics. We will analyze data from a collection of problem sets. In the final third of the course we will discuss topics of sociolinguistic interest; the uniqueness of language acquisition as it pertains to ASL, language variation, issues in language policy and planning, and concerns of the language community. The class will be a mixture of lecture, interaction and discussion. Prerequisite is LING 001 or permission of the instructor. Cap at 25.

title: Cross Cultural Communication
description: This course provides analysis of cross-cultural communication from the perspective of interactional sociolinguistics. Topics include conversational style; the linguistic basis of cultural and ethnic stereotypes; linguistic causes of cross-cultural miscommunication; rhythmic, prosodic, and nonverbal components of communicative style; indirectness and politeness phenomena. The concept of "culture" includes subcultural differences influenced by geographic region, ethnicity, age, class, and gender. Class activities include lecture, discussion in whole-class and small-group formats, videotape presentations, and guest lectures. Students will carry out small-scale research projects and short assignments in which they explore the implications of cross-cultural differences in their own lives and test the approaches read about and discussed. Friday class meetings consist of smaller discussion groups led by TA's. This is a nontechnical course providing a linguistic/sociolinguistic/discourse approach to communication, language, and culture. It is designed to appeal to undergraduate students from all majors and schools. There are no prerequisites.

title: Women, Men, and Language
description: Course Goals & Description This course will help you develop connections between gender and language use and to treat them both as objects of study. We will review the major theoretical approaches to language and gender and examine how each can shed light on cases of gendered linguistic practice. You will develop the ability to thoughtfully evaluate research in the field from the perspective of both the linguist and the gender theorist, at times reconciling tension or bridging gaps between the two. Assignments will emphasize your ability to link theoretical points arising from readings and class discussions to linguistic patterns you observe in the real world.

title: Language Testing
description: Introduction to the construction and use of second and foreign language tests: theory and practice.

title: Foundations of Education
description: This course is designed to provide an overview of the historical, intellectual, social, and political foundations of American education. It introduces students to the major issues impacting schools and teachers. The following themes will be studied: 1) teachers and students, 2) schools and curriculum, 3) historical and philosophical foundations of education, 4) school finance and governance, 5) the struggle of equal education opportunities, 6) tomorrow's issues and trends.

title: Language in the U.S.A.
description: Language in the U.S.A. will address language issues in this country, with emphasis on language policy issues. The course will examine various American dialects, with special attention to Ebonics (African American English), and also the non-English languages spoken here. You will find that matters of language and identity, attitude and discrimination inevitably come up. Part of the course will be about the official English question. Laws mandating the use of English for official purposes have been passed in over half of the states in the past 15 years or so. Legislation or a constitutional amendment declaring English as the official language of the United States (English is actually not official now) has been introduced into every Congress for the last 25 years or so but so far all have died in committee. Because of the importance of immigration as a political issue, I expect that English will become legally official for the first time in history, prossibly in 2009. Along with the official English issue, are some special related cases. Native American languages have been steadily dying out over the course of the history of the nation. Many of these languages would have succumbed regardless of policy, but U.S. policy on native languages was quite harsh for a long time. Over ten years ago, the Ebonics controversy burst on the national scene, following a proposal by the Oakland, California School Board to make use of the language or dialect Ebonics. This happened a long time ago, but the issues involved have not changed, although they have lain dormant since then. Because this was a policy statement by a local governing body (the Oakland Unified School District Board), it was a matter of language policy. I have done research on Ebonics for years and have at times been involved with the implications of the language variety in education, so we will spend some time on this topic. Perhaps surprisingly, language policy impacts Deaf people as well. Most Americans see Deaf people as people like everybody else, except with a physical handicap. They think that sign language is either a loose system of gestures, or is a way of speaking English with oneâs hands. Deaf people see themselves as a sociocultural group, with its own cultural norms and language, American Sign Language, which has nothing to do with English, although it is a language. Some Deaf people even feel that they would not want to be able to hear, since it would remove them from Deaf society. (I am using the capital âDâ because Deaf people who have these values use that orthographical convention). With this degree of misunderstanding, it is inevitable there will be policy conflicts and there are. We will take a look. Another policy issue that may surprise and interest you is official policy on swearing and obscene language. People have been arrested for violating state laws on public offensive language, and the issue arose in 2004 in connection with some rude signs and chants that University of Maryland basketball fans used, especially against their archrivals from Duke University. We'll look at these cases and discuss the role of government in maintaining decency in speech behavior. Before we can begin with all of this, we will have to review the linguistâs view of language, and some points about how language relates to society in general. These assumptions made by linguists have made them virtually unanimous on problems we will take up, and opposed to the opinions of the great majority of other people, even well-educated people. I share the typical linguistâs position on most of these matters (not all), but that doesnât mean that Iâor weâare correct. I have tried hard to include readings by folks who argue for contrary opinions and I hope you will consider these views carefully and not fall into the trap of just agreeing with the professor. The textbooks will be Ronald Schmidt, Sr., Language Policy and Identity Politics in the United States, Edward Finegan and John R. Rickford, Language in the USA, and other readings which will be on the course Blackboard web site.

title: Methods of Language Teaching
description: Principles and practices of teaching a second or foreign language, with concentration on recent communicative models. Readings and discussions are supplemented by classroom observation and evaluation. Graduate students only.

title: Preparation and Evaluation of EFL/ESL Materials
description: This course covers the preparation and evaluation of L2 instructional materials in use with a range of different teaching methods. Students learn skills for developing and analyzing materials in accordance with knowledge about how second languages are learned and taught. Practical classroom input issues and linguistic and pedagogical rationales for the materials and methods are key considerations. The course utilizes a practical, hands-on approach to adapting current materials and developing new materials. The focus is on materials for the different skills in current use in communicative methods in todayâs classrooms, including (but not limited to): task- based approaches to language teaching, computer-assisted language learning, and content-based instruction. Concepts such as learning styles, different teaching contexts (including child/adolescent learners, ESL vs. EFL, etc.), culture, authenticity and classroom management are also discussed in this class. All concepts presented are applicable to foreign/second language materials design in general. Students will participate in instructor- and student-led discussions, evaluate published and develop original teaching materials, and give classroom demonstrations of original materials. Objectives: Participants will (a) gain an understanding of current issues at the core of ESL/EFL materials evaluation and preparation, and (b) develop their ability to evaluate existing and develop original ESL/EFL materials. In addition, participants will improve (a) their ability to engage in academic discussions by facilitating and participating in in-class discussions and (b) their critical thinking and writing skills by carrying out the class assignments. Recommended general background reading: â¢ Byrd, P. (Ed.). (1995). Material Writer's Guide. Newbury House â¢ Tomlinson, B. (Ed.). (1998). Materials Development in Language Teaching. Cambridge University Press. Required readings will be made available on Blackboard. For this course you will write a critique/review of published materials of your choice, prepare a set of materials for a two hour lesson, and either (a) create a set of materials for an integrated unit consisting of 4 two hour lessons, or (b) write a scholarly paper on materials selection and evaluation. Your critique/review will be based on a textbook, video/audio-tapes, software or other materials selected by you. Samples of both reviews and materials will be distributed in class. The materials that you will design will incorporate a description of your target audience, clear, explicit linguistic and pedagogical rationales for the materials developed, and additional pre- and/or post-activities designed to target ongoing interlanguage development, to accompany the materials. For the final project, students are encouraged, but not required, to collaborate with a partner. The final projects will also be presented orally in the last class. Breakdown of final grade: 1. Written critique/review of materials: 20% 2. Written collection of materials: 20%. 3. Presentation of materials in final class: 15%. 4. Final Project/Scholarly paper: 35% 5. Participation/preparation/reading discussions: 10%

title: Introduction to Natural Language Processing
description: This course will introduce students to the basics of natural language processing (NLP), a field which combines insights from linguistics and computer science to produce applications like machine translation, information retrieval, and spell checking. We will cover a range of topics that will help students understand how current NLP technology works and will provide students with a platform for future study and research. We will move from simple representations of language, such as finite-state techniques and n-gram analysis, to more advanced representations, such as those found in context-free and unification-based parsing. Students who take this course will gain a thorough understanding of the fundamental methods used in natural language understanding, along with an ability to assess the strengths and weaknesses of natural language technologies based on these methods.

title: Computational Linguistics: Tools for Linguistics
description: This course is designed to increase awareness of computational tools and their applications for linguistic research. In other words, it will provide ways to learn about various tools to at least partially automate or accelerate linguistic analysis. We cannot replace linguistic intuition, but we provide students with a greater understanding of how and when to use empirical approaches to linguistic analysis. A large emphasis of the course is in how to deal with large amounts of language data and to understand practical issues in dealing with corpora, annotation and multi-lingual data.

title: Psycholinguistics: Language and Music
description: Psycholinguistics: Language and Music The primary aim of this course is to offer an introduction to psycholinguistics, i.e. to how the mind and the brain process language. The course will review basic findings on topics such as speech perception, word recognition, sentence comprehension, reading, lexicalization, syntactic planning and writing. While the focus will be on language processing, the course will also cover basic findings in music psychology. Language and music are both hierarchical, rule-based systems that can serve communicative purposes, and the comparative study of these two cognitive systems can shed light on how the mind works. Questions addressed in this course include: How does the mind process language and music? What neural structures subserve the processing of language and music? Is the mind a modular system? Single-system vs. dual-system approaches to cognition. Empiricist approaches to learning and processing (Bayesian and connectionist models) The goal of this course is to familiarize students with the main findings and debates in psycholinguistics and to enable them to design basic experiments. In terms of format, the course will be a combination of lectures and discussion of empirical research. Students will be expected to read two chapters/articles each week and to actively participate in class discussions. Students will also be required to write a literature review on one of the course topics and to give a presentation in class. Other forms of evaluation include a midterm and a final project. Course textbook: Harley, T. E. (2008). The psychology of language: From data to theory (3rd ed.). Hove; UK: Psychology Press. It is recommended that students read the following two (accessible) books in preparation for the course: Levitin, D. J. (2007). This is your brain on music: The science of a human obsession. New York: Plume/Penguin. Pinker, S. (1994/2007). The language instinct: How the mind creates language (P.S. ed.). New York: Harper Perennial. The course readings will also include several journal articles as well as selected readings from: Rebuschat, P., Rohrmeier, M., Hawkins, J. H., & Cross, I. (Eds.). (2009). Language and music as cognitive systems. Oxford: Oxford University Press

title: Language and Politics (CCTP 756)
description: The mastery of language is highly integrated with political life. The quantity and variety of spoken and written political texts, the wealth of genres and rhetorical styles, and the intensity of the political word offer rich arenas for the study of language. We will approach this task from both communication and linguistic perspectives while examining campaign communication, political speeches, advertising, debates, and media coverage. Both theoretical arguments and case studies will highlight discursive practices of framing, referencing, argumentation and manipulation, as well as the use of labeling, metaphor, speech acts and politeness. We will also consider the role of religion, culture, gender and humor in political discourse. Different approaches to the language of politics â from ethnomethodology and discourse analysis to cognitive and pragmatic methods â will help us shed light on the Aristotelian claim that we are all political animals, able to use language to pursue our own ends. The data for our case studies are drawn from American and international political discourses (including Saddam Hussein's speeches, Croatian political debates, and contemporary French presidential language, to name a few). However, we have just experienced a gripping, remarkable historical presidential campaign, and it is to be expected that much of our data will be drawn from the recent US political discourses. The new US President's inaugural address will, of course, be our first case study.

title: Language and Writing Systems
description: Language and Writing Systems. Ling. 382 Prof. Solomon Sara, S.J. Fall 2008 Class time: T-Th 10:15-11:30 p.m. Office: 475 ICC Tel: (202)687-6226/4004 E-mail: Saras@georgetown.edu Writing : Just as sound represents language in an audible form we call speech, so does writing represent language in a visual form we call writing. Sound representation is quite uniform and a [b] will be similarly pronounced as a [b] in every language. This is not true of writing. A [b] may have different visual representations in different languages, e.g. take English and Arabic, or Chinese and Egyptian or all of them together. The Course:. The course will take a historical and systematic approach to the study of writing. One can argue that the single greatest achievement in the history of human intellectual development is writing. Societies throughout history and around the globe have, by different visual means, come to similar solutions to similar problems of how to preserve a permanent or a quasi permanent record of spoken language. This course will take a historical and systematic approach to writing. It will begin with the precursors to writing and proceed to the earliest writing systems, say the cuneiform of the Sumerians, and continue on to the other forms of writing, whether alphabetic or non-alphabetic. It will consider their origins, their developments, their adaptations to other languages and their stability over time. Since writing is the preservation of language in visible form, the course will look at the principles of invention of writing, the adequacy of writing and its usefulness. Though not much discussed among linguists, writing systems are every bit as challenging to comprehend as the language systems they represent. Most likely, everyone knows of, and uses, more than one writing system. Requirements: The course is an undergraduate course. It has no specific requirements apart from interest in writing systems, their origins, their complexities, their varieties, their decipherment and a fascination in understanding their unique characteristics. Text: Rogers, Henry, 2005. Writing Systems: A Linguistic Approach. New York: Blackwell Publishing.

title: Language and multimedia discourse
description: Within social interaction, people draw upon and attend to a variety of verbal and extraverbal phenomena to communicate and understand one another. While language may be the most systematically studied semiotic resource, other aspects such as the use of space, gaze, gesture, action, and material artifacts are also important to understanding communication. Furthermore, recent advances in technology such as digital video, transcription software, audio and video corpus tools, and editing software make examining the array semiotic resources and how they are utilized in social interaction more feasible. In this course, students will read about and discuss central aspects of communication including language, gaze, gesture, proxemics, action and object use, etc. Students will also learn to use a variety of technological tools to conduct their own analyses of communication in naturalistic video and multimedia data for course projects. Prerequisite is LING 001 or permission of the instructor. Cap at 25.

title: Language/Culture/Thought
description: Language/Culture/Thought

title: World Englishes
description: LING 393: World Englishes Spring 2009 Mon/Wed, 1:15-2:30, ICC 204A TEXTBOOKS Required: The Alchemy of English by Braj Kachru University of Illinois Press, 1990 The Other Tongue: English across Cultures by Braj Kachru University of Illinois Press, 1992 English as a Global Language by David Crystal Cambridge University Press, 1997 On Reserve: English as a World Language (Lauinger Library) Bailey, R. & Gorlach, M. Ann Arbor: University of Michigan Press, 1982 About This Syllabus: TOPICS The purpose of this course is to investigate the spread of English as an international language: its historical development, socio-cultural diversity and linguistic variation. In addition to numerous readings on native and non-native varieties of English which can be found throughout the world (e.g. Indian English, Singaporean English, Chicano English, etc.), topics related to educational linguistics within a World Englishes background will also be addressed in order to better understand common pedagogical problems and concerns related to the English language teaching profession. READING: Assignments There are assigned readings for most dates that we meet as a class. You are expected to have done these readings by the date they are listed on the syllabus. The readings build on one another and serve as a general background for my lectures and the written assignments. Some of the assigned readings will also be used as the basis for your reaction papers. ASSIGNMENTS: - Presentation Students will be expected to take responsibility for presenting two required articles/chapters; one with a reflective paper and the other with an article critique. - Reflective paper On designated dates throughout the semester, students will be expected to hand in a 2-3 page reflective journal based on an assigned reading/question given to them. The reflective paper is NOT meant to be a research paper and students are not expected to do any additional reading or library research. Rather, the journal is an opportunity to reflect in depth on a particular reading/question. - Article critique The presentation of the article should be more than just a summary of the article and include a critique of the article which should lend itself to thoughtful discussion the class. Students should be sure to include three to four opinion/discussion with questions that will help the class understand how the reading relates to material that has been covered in previous weeks. The student will also hand in a written critique of the chapter that they will present. FINAL PROJECT Students will be expected to engage in original research on a topic related to World Englishes. Each student will have 15-20 minutes of presentation on their final project at the end of the semester. Projects may include, but are not limited to: 1. An analysis/comparison of some linguistic features (phonology, morpho-syntax, discourse) of a specific variety of English (especially if access to a speaker of that variety is available). 2. Comparing Englishes of a given genre (such as newspapers, talk show, radio, news reports, etc.) from two or more varieties. 3. The influence/impact of English on other languages and vice versa. 4. The role of English in language education, language policy, language planning, etc. 5. The impact of English on some socio-cultural/political aspect such as language and law, language and business, language and the media, etc. 6. Any topic approved by the instructor EVALUATION -Reflective Papers 20% -Paper: 10% -Presentation: 10% -Article Critique 20% -Paper: 10% -Presentation: 10% -Final Project 50% -Paper: 35% -Presentation: 15% -Attendance/Participation 10% Tentative Schedule of Topics and Readings (*designed chapter for pre-assigned discussion questions) Dates Topic & Readings Homework Assignments 1/7 (F) Introduction to the class Introduction to World Englishes -
