title: Cross-Disciplinary Discourse Analysis
description: Over the past thirty years or so, scholars of language and social life have been analyzing discourse within a variety of professional contexts, most notably medicine, law, education, government, and business. For some of these scholars, these research sites offer intriguing real-life contexts in which their favorite discourse phenomena can be examined in situ; others are lured by a concrete place in which they can investigate the role of language in the relationship between ideology and practice; still others are attracted by an opportunity to work towards the solution of important societal problems, such as those found in relationships (and talk) between physicians and their patients, judges and jury members, teachers and their students, government employees and citizens, or employers and their employees. Weâve learned a good deal from this cross-disciplinary work. In fact, in recent years more and more professionals in other disciplines know about discourse analysis and its power to illuminate real-life problems. Many of us are called on to work as consultants in cross-disciplinary projects. And â through this involvement â many of us are learning that such work is rarely straightforward; itâs not usually enough just to be trained as a discourse analyst and then to carry out oneâs work in another field. Accommodation and compromise are usually necessary. Working across disciplines can be a challenge. Different disciplinary paradigms dominate decisions regarding what kinds of research questions are thought to be both answerable and useful or important. These paradigms also influence what participants and settings can be included in the study, what kinds of language data can be collected and how, what types of theoretical frameworks and analytical units can be brought to the research, as well as what counts as research findings, and how these findings should be reported. But â for many of us â seeing our work impact society in significant ways is well worth this extra effort. Being prepared for the kinds of differences that can exist when undertaking cross-disciplinary research and being armed with suggestions regarding how to make the most of oneâs role in such research can make the crucial difference between a discourse analyst leaving a project in frustration or being able to contribute to lasting changes. This seminar on Cross-Disciplinary Discourse Analysis offers this kind of preparation to the interested student. Although specific areas of focus and seminar activities will be attuned to seminar participantsâ interests, preparation, and experience levels, it is expected that they will include all major phases of cross-disciplinary involvement: -Getting involved: Establishing the researcher's role -Setting the course: Developing the research question and project design -Working on the ground: Observing, interviewing, and taping -Making sense of the discourse: Analyzing and reporting Throughout the semester weâll discuss the challenges and rewards of carrying out discourse analysis in the 'real' world, drawing on experiences of guest lecturers, my own consulting experiences, and the emerging experiences of students in the seminar. The discussion will revolve around the theme of balance: as a researcher working effectively across disciplines, one must be ready both to learn and to teach; to take alternate perspectives as insider and outsider as the project progresses; to serve one's own disciplinary goals at times, but to allow those goals to take a secondary place when other professional goals need to be served; to assert one's position strongly at times and to be ready to compromise at others; to be patient when disciplinary differences or practicalities of teamwork do not allow the most expeditious resolution of a problem, all the while holding to the conviction that the extra effort that goes into this kind of research endeavor will ultimately impact society in some of the ways the researcher anticipated. Prerequisite: at least one course in discourse analysis Course activities will include: Reading seminal and innovative studies by discourse analysts working in other disciplines Interacting with guest lecturers - discourse analysts who work either fulltime or as consultants with individuals from other disciplines Identifying how linguistic discourse analysis complements and extends analyses from other disciplines by participating in multidisciplinary team investigations of interaction Learning how to position qualitative work and explain key concepts within discourse analysis and linguistics to people outside the field Learning about different disciplinary conventions in abstract-writing, academic presentations and publications Designing and implementing a cross-disciplinary discourse analytic study

title: Managerial Communication
description: Understanding effective communication is important for success in any organizational environment. The goals of this class are to improve the quality of your verbal and written communication and also identify internal and external barriers to communication effectiveness. You will have the opportunity to give presentations individually, and in groups, and will receive feedback from the professor and your peers. Overall, this class should provide a theoretical and practical basis for you to be more effective in any organizational setting.

title: Management & Organizational Behavior
description: This course is a comprehensive overview of the management process and organizational behavior from a behavioral and social science perspective. Topics include: management across cultures; managing with ethics and social responsibility; fundamentals of organizing; organizational culture and design; leadership; motivation; communication; interpersonal skills; teamwork and group dynamics; goal-setting; alternative work arrangements; power and politics; conflict and negotiation; managing change; and management development. Emphasis is on the analysis and understanding of human behavior in organizations.

title: Intercultural Communication
description: As the marketplace becomes more "global," smart managers will be the ones who understand the role of culture in getting work done. Culture challenges us by assaulting some of our most basic assumptions about working, that is to say, how we communicate with each other to complete work successfully. Anyone interested in working internationally--or even those just interested in better understanding their communication with international friends--would benefit from the course. Each student is asked to do an in-depth study on communication in one culture while learning the principles of intercultural communication. The course explores the rules that govern communication in other cultures, how people in different cultures give feedback, plan and make decisions.

title: Imagination & Creativity
description: Businesses operate in a competitive global marketplace that judges them against the standards of "more, better, faster." If a business is not doing more, or doing it better, or doing it faster, the chance of it surviving in the competitive global marketplace will become remote. Those companies that not only survive, but also thrive, recognize that to do it "more, better, faster" requires imagination and creativity. The purpose of this course is to help you master the skills of imagination and creativity. Toward that end, we will identify common "blocks" to creativity and innovation in business. In addition, we will upgrade your imagination skills and identify strategies and techniques to enhance your personal and professional creativity. To achieve these objectives will not only require hard work and discipline, but also a renewed appreciation for foolishness and play.

title: Management Seminar: Organizational Design and Change
description: This is an advanced course in managing people and organizations for high performance. The course is divided into two main topics, organizational design and change management. Specific topics include open systems organizational analysis, technology, effects of national culture on organizational forms, problem diagnosis, change implementation, resistance to change, the role of change agents, implementing mergers and acquisitions, changing the ethical climate, and managing organizational culture.

title: Management Seminar: Management of Human Resources
description: The primary goal of this course is to enhance your ability to recognize and address HR issues faced by managers. We will focus primarily on the operational level, rather than the strategic (top management) level, and will examine HR problems from the perspectives (a) of the organization and its managers, and (b) the employees.

title: Negotiations
description: Negotiation is the art and science of securing agreements between two or more parties who are interdependent and who are seeking to maximize their outcomes. The purpose of this course is to understand the theory and processes of negotiation in a variety of settings. Objectives include evaluating notions of when to negotiate and what assumptions, beliefs, values, and habits influence negotiation behavior; understanding the central concepts in negotiation; developing confidence in negotiating; and providing experience in negotiating. The course is experiential. A great deal of emphasis is placed on simulations, role plays, and cases.

title: Leadership
description: This course examines the process of leadership and the use of power as essential ingredients for making a difference in business, government, and not-for-profit settings. One objective of this course is to improve inspirational skills to persuade people to focus their efforts toward achieving a shared purpose or goal. A second objective of this course is to understand power and identify strategies and tactics of influence. The third objective is to improve the ability to "manage" the dilemmas of leadership and power to promote productive actions.

title: Internship in Business
description: The Internship in Business course permits the student to gain practical work experience in a business environment. Students select a specific area within the field of business and explore it in greater depth outside the normal classroom situation. The purpose of the internship is to provide the student with an understanding of how a business actually operates and how theories learned in school are applied in the business world. The internship course is a three-credit honors seminar available to juniors and seniors with a minimum GPA of 3.00. Students must gain the approval of his/her dean and the professor to register for the course. Approved internships must be in the field of business and must provide a significant learning experience for the student. Please see the MSB Undergraduate Deans' Office for required paperwork for additional information on gaining approval.

title: Internship in Business
description: The Internship in Business course permits the student to gain practical work experience in a business environment. Students select a specific area within the field of business and explore it in greater depth outside the normal classroom situation. The purpose of the internship is to provide the student with an understanding of how a business actually operates and how theories learned in school are applied in the business world. The internship course is one-credit and available to sophomores, juniors, and seniors with a minimum GPA of 2.00. The course is pass/fail and does not count towards the forty courses required to graduate. Students must gain the approval of his/her dean and the professor to register for the course. Approved internships must be in the field of business and must provide a significant learning experience for the student. Please see the MSB Undergraduate Deans' Office for required paperwork for additional information on gaining approval.

title: Organizational Behavior
description: Additional content will be added soon to reflect the changes to the new curriculum. MBA Core According to an influential study conducted by McKinsey & Co., the most important corporate resource over the next 20 years will be talent: smart, sophisticated businesspeople who are technologically literate, globally astute, and operationally agile. This course is intended to help students develop as global business leaders. In particular, this course focuses on mastering key leadership skills, such as power and influence, working effectively in teams, making decisions with abundant yet imperfect information, dealing effectively with cross-cultural diversity, managing conflict, fostering creativity, motivating others, managing performance, and shaping an organizationâs culture. COURSE OBJECTIVES Upon completion of this course, you should be able to: 1. Understand the factors that influence behavior in organizations, 2. Apply behavioral theory to organizational settings to create alignment between a firmâs strategy, systems, people and culture, 3. Diagnose critical management issues in organizations through the process of observation, interpretation, and analysis of contributing factors, and 4. Communicate theoretically sound, yet practical, recommendations in a persuasive and timely manner.

title: Management Communication
description: Additional content will be added soon to reflect the changes to the new curriculum. MBA Core Effective presentation skills contribute to success in many areas of business as men and women in business are called on to brief someone or to sell an idea, a product, or a service. This module helps students capitalize on these opportunities by becoming more effective presenters. The course will provide experience with planned and impromptu presentations, emphasize the importance of audience analysis, and consider the use of visual aids within presentations.

title: Managing Organizational Change
description: Additional content will be added soon to reflect the changes to the new curriculum. This course is an integrated approach to large-scale change in organizations. Change is analyzed from three levels: top management, where leadership and vision are critical, middle management, where implementation is the focus, and lower levels where receptivity and upward influence are the emphases. Cases will provide opportunities to develop diagnostics skills and intervention plans, while experiential learning and a team project is used to develop a tool box of specific intervention techniques and skills.

title: Human Resources Management For Line Managers
description: The primary objective of this course is to enhance your ability to recognize and address HR issues faced by managers, through cases and readings focused on research on HR and legal developments. We will focus primarily on the operational level, rather than the strategic (top management) level. We will examine HR problems both from the perspective of the organization and its managers, and from the perspective of the employee.

title: Georgetown means Business
description: Additional content will be added soon to reflect the changes to the new curriculum. MBA Core MBA first year students only. Before Fall Classes in August. This class is a residency held off-campus. The Integrative Course is a required off-campus residency that marks the beginning of the Georgetown MBA program. The incoming class is transported to the National Conference Center (NCC) where they stay for the week. This course will focus on a single international company in a âlive case.â In this experience, company executives discuss current situations within their company. The course will be designed to provide students with an overview of international business (including how functional areas work together), to introduce the case method, and to encourage an appreciation for quantitative and qualitative issues and for multi-cultural learning styles. This course provides an introduction to working in teams â a concept used widely throughout the Georgetown MBA program. In addition to meeting your new classmates, this is an excellent opportunity to interact with professors and administrators of the Program.

title: Global Experience: Dubai
description: This course is a 2nd year core course about international business practice. It consists of (1) on-campus classes in the 3rd module in Spring 2008 and (2) a foreign residency between the after the 3rd module in February. The objectives of the course are to (1) increase your understanding of business in a foreign setting; (2) increase your ability to conduct international business with comfort and confidence in a foreign culture, and to improve your managerial mobility â your ability and willingness to do a job wherever it needs to be done; (3) improve your problem-solving ability, analytical skills, skills of synthesis, and communication skills; and (4) provide an opportunity for you to integrate knowledge obtained from prior course work and apply it to the solution of a real current business problem.

title: Sports Leadership
description: The following topics will be covered in class: 1. Principles of Sports Leadership: Leadership is a critical element to success in any field, and sports is no different. We will discuss in class the basic elements of good leadership that are exhibited by Coaches and General Managers of sports teams. We will also discuss how to develop leaders. The principles discussed in class will be related to the business world. 2. Developing Teamwork: In sports, it is critical for all members of the team to understand their role on the team and the importance of their ability to work together towards a common goal of winning the game. In the class, we will show how a sports team achieves this and how this can be applied in the business world. 3. Motivation: It is easy to say that a company will only hire âself-motivatedâ individuals, but that is not always the case. Furthermore, those who are âself-motivatedâ are human and will still need motivation at times. In sports, you likewise try to recruit only âself-motivatedâ individuals, but you do not always end up with an entire team of them. 4. Media Relations and Crisis Management: A big part of sports is media relations. Everything done in professional sports â and to some extent, college and high school sports â is covered extensively by TV, radio, Internet, and print media. It is critical in sports to be able to deal with the media on a daily basis. In business, it is no less critical to be proficient in media relations, but the difference is that it is done on an infrequent basis. We will discuss in class the principles of media relations that will help the students have guidelines to deal with the media. In sports, when a crisis occurs with a team or player, it rarely escapes the news. In class, we will discuss how to deal with a crisis internally, as well as externally (with the media). 5. Selecting Talent: In sports â as well as business perhaps â the most critical factor in the success of a team is the talent on the team. The difference in sports, however, is that selecting talent for a team is much more restrictive due to the mechanisms you have to deal with, such as a draft, salary cap, and player contract restrictions. There are also restrictions on hiring front office personnel away from other teams so it is also important to be proficient in selecting talent, but also it is important to develop talent. In class, we will discuss what sports teams look for in selecting talent â both in players and head coaches (CEO). 6. Dealing with Different Situations: I have picked six common situations that happen in sports and will discuss how to handle each situation. These six situations are: Newly-named coach; Extended losing streak; Extended winning streak; Sustaining a winning streak; Sustaining success after a big season; and Rebounding from a poor season.

title: Leadership
description: Additional content will be added soon to reflect the changes to the new curriculum. This course offers the student an opportunity to consider the intent of leadership; what effective leaders do; how power is utilized to support effective leadership; and the role of the leader in one-on-one, group, and organizational leadership. The opportunity also exists for the student to further define his/her own preferred leadership style. Preference given to MBA 2nd years.

title: Internship: Management
description: Additional content will be added soon to reflect the changes to the new curriculum. This course is an independent study and aims to allow students to engage in practical consulting-management methods for targeting and acquiring clients, negotiating project scope, managing clients during project analysis, and presenting findings and recommendations. As a direct aid to practical learning, students will work in teams on consulting projects sponsored by companies typically associated with their summer work internships. Enrollment by permission of department.

title: Negotiations
description: Additional content will be added soon to reflect the changes to the new curriculum. Negotiation is central to business, and this class will teach you the basic tenants of successful negotiation, to help you to understand the strategies and tactics that work best for you. This course is designed to complement the technical and diagnostic skills learned in other courses at Georgetown. Although business requires a broad array of analytical skills in marketing, management, finance, accounting, and operations, these skills will only go so far. As a complement, managers need a broad array of negotiation skills in order to get their proposals accepted and implemented. This course will develop participants' negotiation skills experientially. We will spend a considerable amount of time practicing techniques so that you feel comfortable using them. I will also teach you the most up-to-date theories and frameworks, so that we can analyze what strategies work, and under what circumstances they are most effective. Preference given to MBA 2nd years.

title: Community Development Finance & Strategy
description: This course addresses how to promote investment, entrepreneurial actions, and creative development in inner cities, communities undergoing structural dislocation, or stuck in long term stagnation, and any other areas, urban or rural, that are chronically underperforming, deteriorated and under invested. This course offers an excellent opportunity to develop and practice consulting skills. And it provides a chance to complete a consulting assignment, and produce a strategic study that can add value, and produce findings and recommendations that can be implemented, or produce business plans that can make a lasting tangible difference in growing business. We use case studies and extensive readings, both analytical and practical. Preference given to MBA 2nd years.

title: Investing in Africa
description: This course offers opportunities to apply the tools of the MBA core curriculum in a setting that presents extra challenges. And it provides opportunities to sharpen the general management skill set. There is also a chance to practice consulting skills, and complete a study that will be useful to a company in real terms. This course addresses the issues from the points of view of established company management, new entrepreneurs, development policy makers, and multinational corporate managers. The course will deepen your understanding if you want to better grasp economic events in Africa, or are thinking of going to work as entrepreneurs, managers, consultants or financial advisors, lenders or investors. It also has value for students in Law, Public Policy and Foreign Service. Preference given to MBA 2nd years.

title: Writing Winning Proposals
description: Additional content will be added soon to reflect the changes to the new curriculum. This course is designed to help you improve your ability to plan and write longer business documents such as proposals, position papers, white papers, and reports. The class meets on a non-traditional schedule: Once a week for 12 weeks. Each participant has an opportunity to develop longer documents on topics that he or she chooses to coordinate with personal career interests.

title: Principles of Marketing
description: A first course on tools and approaches for making marketing decisions. Marketing is viewed as a broad technology for influencing behavior, beyond functions like selling and advertising. Topics covered include consumer behavior, marketing research, and marketing planning, with emphasis on marketing mix decisions: product strategy, communications, pricing, and distribution. Prerequisite: Sophomore standing.

title: Marketing Research
description: The theory and application of marketing research as a tool for the decision-making process. Special emphasis on problem definition, research design, sampling procedure, data collection, statistical analysis, interpretation of data and reporting of research findings. Computer lab assignments and projects.

title: Buyer Behavior
description: This course familiarizes students with concepts and findings from the behavioral sciences and their application to marketing decision making. Topics include perception, learning, memory, motivation, persuasion, decision making, and group influence, as relevant to consumer response to marketing mix elements. The goal of the course is developing student knowledge of consumer psychology theories and their practical applications in marketing.

title: International Marketing
description: Fundamental concepts, principles, and theories of marketing in an international setting are considered. The course focuses on the technique of entering foreign markets; conducting marketing research studies in foreign markets; and the development of product, pricing, promotional, and distribution policies and strategies for foreign markets. The material is presented from the point of view of marketing managers. Cases and original studies are discussed.

title: Promotional Strategy
description: A socioeconomic orientation to the theories and practices of promotion. As an integral part of the communication process, the topics covered will include advertising, message, media selection effectiveness, and advertising organization. Special attention will be given to new media.

title: Sports Marketing Strategy
description: This course presents an overview of the marketing of sports at the professional and collegiate levels, as well as the use of sponsorships by commercial enterprises in the marketing of products and services. Class projects emphasize original research into sports marketing topics in collaboration with industry professionals.

title: Marketing for Public and Nonprofit Organizations
description: The course is designed to provide future managers of nonprofit organizations with methods of assessing consumer needs, marketing products and services, planning positioning and controlling operations. It presents new concepts, techniques, strategies and tactics for managing effectively in such nonprofit markets as health, educational and public services, the arts, social action, and political marketing.

title: Advertising Campaign Planning
description: The essentials of advertising campaign planning including media strategy, positioning, and advertising design culminating in a comprehensive plan for a specific product. Especially valuable for students planning a career in advertising. Students participate in the American Advertising Federation Student Competition.

title: Brand Management
description: The course examines the broad topic of brand equity and brand management. Its focus will be to explore and understand the importance of brands, what brands mean to consumers, and how they should be managed to the greatest benefit of the concern. For many companies their most valuable asset is the brand name associated with their products or services. Yet too often insufficient attention is paid to this subject. Too often marketing decisions regarding product policy, pricing, advertising or distribution are taken without taking into account their impact on brand equity or, conversely, optimally leveraging it. In this course we will examine marketing strategy from a branding perspective, including the key issues involved in building strong brands and maximizing the value of existing as well as new brands. The basic objectives are as follows: to understand the key issues in planning and evaluating brand strategies to describe appropriate theories, models and other tools to make better branding decisions to use a variety of methods to develop effective applications of these principles.

title: Marketing Strategy
description: Capstone course for Senior Marketing Majors only. Goal-oriented analysis, planning, implementation, and control of marketing activities. Focus on practical operations, decision-making and complete marketing programs of firms. Cases and projects.

title: International Business
description: A first course in the theory and practice of international business. After building a foundation of international trade, foreign direct investment, exchange rates, and government policy, the course emphasis is on the application of concepts to the solution of international business problems. It focuses on areas such as international market entry, the internationalization of the marketing, finance and management functions within the firm, and the development of global business strategies.

title: Advanced Seminar in International Business
description: The objective of the course is to advance your analytic skills and your understanding of concepts and issues in international business. The course can be especially helpful to you as you prepare for the next few years of your career and for effective functioning as an international business executive in subsequent years. It is an integrative course that combines the perspectives of strategy, marketing, finance, government relations and other managerial processes. It is also an integrative course in the sense that it combines several levels of analysis. The pedagogical approach of the course combines lectures and case analyses. Both the lectures and the cases provide examples of key concepts and data sources that are widely used in international business. An individual research paper receives much emphasis, and it is the subject of two tutorials with the instructor.

title: Principles of Marketing
description: A first course on tools and approaches for making marketing decisions. Marketing is viewed as a broad technology for influencing behavior, beyond functions like selling and advertising. Topics covered include consumer behavior, marketing research, and marketing planning, with emphasis on marketing mix decisions: product strategy, communications, pricing, and distribution.

title: Marketing
description: Additional content will be added soon to reflect the changes to the new curriculum. MBA Core This course will study the managerial processes involved in marketingâprocesses that are heavily influenced by the accelerated movements of capital, technology, people, products, logistics and distribution, services, and ideas. As a business discipline, marketing is the process of planning and executing the conception, pricing, promotion, and distribution of ideas, goods, and services to create exchanges that satisfy individuals and organizational objectives. This course views marketing as both a general management responsibility and an orientation of an organization. Fundamental to marketing programs is the managerial process of creating and delivering value. Within this social context, marketing management is the art and science of choosing target markets and getting, keeping, and growing customers through creating, delivering, and communicating superior customer value. This course will use a mix of cases, lecture/discussion, and outside speakers. This material will be integrated throughout the course with four over-riding themes. First, that successful marketing strategies deliver superior customer value; second, that successful implementation of these strategies requires a high level of market orientation (a market-driven firm has superior skills in understanding, attracting, and keeping customers); third, that the internet will transform most markets (and media) by introducing new business models and enabling personalization of offerings; and fourth, that the market arena in which strategies are implemented is increasingly global.

title: Marketing Research I
description: Marketing research is a process for obtaining information to support management decision-making and strategic recommendations for serious consulting projects. The course structure follows the scientific method, which includes problem definition, research design, data collection, data analysis, and the interpretation and communication of findings. It is taught as a workshop with a live project and requires students to use statistical software for analysis. The course is designed primarily for first-year MBA students who are interested in pursuing careers in marketing and consulting.

title: Advanced Marketing Strategy
description: Additional content will be added soon to reflect the changes to the new curriculum. Advanced Marketing Strategy is a 6-week elective course that explores, from both managerial and analytical perspectives, the development and assessment of marketing strategies in highly competitive environments. In general, this course complements industry, corporate and SBU strategy courses, and builds on core marketing management concepts, principles, and analytical frameworks. More specifically, the course applies the 6-Ps of marketing â people, product, price, place (distribution), promotion, and performance - to cases, with a particular emphasis on profitability drivers, value creation, segmentation & positioning, life cycle management, and integration of the Ps for competitive advantage. The central thesis of this course is that good strategic thinking is built on a strong foundation of principles, concepts, and frameworks that are applied in a consistent manner (externally focused, fact based, economically rational, futures oriented, proactive, dynamic, internally aligned). Great strategic thinking goes beyond understanding and conforming to existing market environments (âmore and better of the sameâ) or identifying and capitalizing on ânaturalâ opportunities (âfinding the white spacesâ) to creating and exploiting unique opportunities particularly well suited to a firmâs strengths (ânew gamesâ). Therefore, great strategic thinking requires a knowledge base, process discipline, and aggressive creativity. The overarching objective of this course is to create an enduring mindset geared to creating great marketing strategies.

title: Applied Marketing Management
description: Applied Marketing Management is a 6-week elective intended primarily for 1st year MBAs who are prospective marketing managers or consultants (though the course may be substantially beneficial to 2nd year MBAs and students in other interest areas such as finance and operations). In general, the course objective is to sharpen studentsâ focus on key marketing decision variables, and the analytical frameworks and methods that support decision-making. Accordingly, the emphasis is on marketing decisions that are made (both mega-strategic and day-to-day), the data and analytical structures that typically underlie the decisions, and ways to improve decision-making by infusing more sophisticated analytical methods and pragmatic realism. A broad, experience-based management perspective is provided, with particular emphasis on decisions and analyses that MBA graduates are likely to experience in their first few years as marketing managers or consultants. A marketing simulation (MARKSTRAT) is the foundation of the learning experience. The central thesis of this course is that good marketing decision-making is built on a strong foundation of principles, concepts, and fact-based analysis that, when applied in a rigorous and consistent manner substantially improves the likelihood of success in the marketplace.

title: Price, Value & Profitability
description: PVP is a 6-week multidisciplinary pricing course that emphasizes marketing economics, value creation, and program profitability. The course builds on the quantitative skills developed in Applied Marketing Management, and will complement and extend the strategic concepts covered in Advanced Marketing Strategy. The overarching objective of the course is to provide the conceptual underpinnings and analytical tools required to make reasoned pricing decisions in highly competitive, dynamic environments. More specifically, PVP will explore topics including: â¢ Understanding the link between marketing and economics â¢ Calibrating demand curves based on market research â¢ Determining economic value to customers (a.k.a. âvalue in useâ) â¢ Understanding the behavioral and psychological aspects of pricing â¢ Developing and interpreting value maps â¢ Assessing the profitability of pricing alternatives via PVA (Price Value Analysis) â¢ Customizing prices for diverse market segments â¢ Bundling products and features to maximize profitability â¢ Evaluating price promotions and EDLP strategies â¢ Understanding revenue and price yield management â¢ Managing in price war environments â¢ Evaluating the impact of the Internet on price strategies PVP is intensively analytical and extensively case-based. Typical cases include: â¢ Land Rover (value mapping, market positioning, pricing) â¢ Cumberland Industries (value in use pricing) â¢ American Airlines Revenue Management (yield management, competitive dynamics) â¢ Virgin Mobile (linking pricing & customer lifetime value) In addition to the traditional cases, students perform focused analyses based on a series of âroad-testedâ, quantitatively-oriented mini-cases specially developed for this course. Course requirements include extensive readings (primarily short ânotesâ on analytical methods), active class participation in case discussions, some written case analyses, and tests (probably a short in-class exam and a case-based take home final exam). PVP builds on the analytical skills developed in Applied Marketing Management (MARK 555), and the conceptual frameworks covered in Advanced Marketing Strategy (MARK 553), but neither is a formal prerequisite.

title: Consumer Behavior
description: Additional content will be added soon to reflect the changes to the new curriculum. This course takes an in-depth look at the social science processes and phenomena that underlie the behaviors of consumers in traditional and e-commerce marketspaces. The purpose is to provide you with background into the theories that attempt to explain behavior so that you might explore the implications of those theories for improving marketing practice. While focusing on behaviors of final consumers, the concepts developed throughout the course will provide insights into decision making more generally, and should lead you to develop better communications, policies and programs in any exchange setting. The research being examined has focused on Western culture and students are encouraged to explore and discuss how some of the findings may not apply in all international settings. The goal of this course is to introduce you to the concepts that will assist you in achieving that understanding and to provide you with opportunities to apply such concepts when analyzing marketing problems and developing marketing strategies. Preference given to MBA 2nd years.

title: Marketing of Consumer Products/Services
description: Additional content will be added soon to reflect the changes to the new curriculum. The course is designed to provide a very practical introduction to what it is that Marketing and Brand Managers actually do in their day-to-day roles. As such it will build on and apply the theoretical teaching from other courses. In itself it will be very light on marketing theory, but will deal in detail with application techniques, and the rationale behind them, that lead to successful marketing practice. Discussion of real and practical cases will thus form a major component. It will take as axiomatic that the marketing function has prime responsibility for driving growth in the organization. The course will emphasize the vital balance of âartâ and âscienceâ that characterizes its most successful application, and will stress the critical roles of intuition and creativity in its most effective practice. It will take the process through from initiation through to execution and ultimate evaluation. It will do this in a deliberately international context, and with appropriate recognition of both the product and service sectors.

title: Spec Topics: Market Segmentation
description: Additional content will be added soon to reflect the changes to the new curriculum. Segmentation is a dynamic decision process that leads to improved allocation of resources for strategic competitive advantage in complex global environments. The course emphasizes the central role of man-agers in driving the segmentation process according to strategic deciÂ¬sions. The course builds hands-on computer competence with statistical software to identify and describe target segments as a basis for mar-keting strategy. (Evening MBA only)

title: Special Topics: Internet Advertising and Marketing
description: The internet has gone through some significant changes as an advertising and marketing platform for advertisers of all sizes. This course has been designed to discuss real life examples of what advertisers, agencies and the internet publishers have all worked on together in creating the most easurable advertising medium ever. In this course we will review case studies and key issues that the industry has started to solve and to get the students involved in real time business situations. There will be a number of guest speakers who are key players in the internet advertising industry that will present a real sense of the advertising issues of the day from monetizing social media to local advertising trends to looking at the largest advertiser issues of the day . We will look at what went wrong with the advertising model as the bubble burst in 2000 to how this industry has grown at a 30%+ CAGR in the last 7 years with emphasis on search, display, video and mobile internet advertising and why this industry is poised for significant growth in the years ahead.

title: New Product Development
description: This course develops a practical managerial approach to new product development for consumer marketing, B2B marketing, and entrepreneurial marketing. It focuses primarily on the innovative processes necessary to support the design, testing, and marketing of new products and services for competitive advantage. Topics include creating and screening new product ideas, writing new product concepts and concept testing, new product forecasting, design challenges, prototyping, product refinement, market entry strategy, and launch marketing programs. The course uses lectures and the case method of teaching and is designed primarily for second-year MBA students who are interested in pursuing careers in marketing and consulting.

title: Social Marketing
description: Social marketing is the adaptation of commercial marketing technologies to programs designed to influence the voluntary behavior of target audiences in order to improve their personal welfare and that of the society of which they are a part. Through the use of text, cases, discussions and guest lectures, students will learn the underlying concepts and principles of social marketing and how to develop and carry out effective social marketing programs. Similarities with â and differences from â private sector marketing will be emphasized. A secondary benefit of the course is that students will have the opportunity to assess the value of their MBA training to date and its flexibility to address management problems in unconventional domains. Preference given to MBA 2nd years.

title: Special Topics: Climate Change
description: The purpose of the course is to prepare students to address more effectively the opportunities and challenges posed by a new array of business issues concerning climate change and energy. The course is appropriate for students with a wide range of professional backgrounds and interests. The course covers numerous managerial issues, diverse industries and many regions of the world.

title: Services Marketing
description: No course information available at this time.

title: Social Enterprise
description: This module explores approaches taken by the business and nonprofit sectors to address social problems around the world. It examines the potential roles for businesses in civil society beyond providing profits and jobs, and it considers the role of the nonprofit sector and the unique issues of managing in this sector. It also considers cross-sector collaborations and the special challenges inherent in seeking effectiveness and efficiency in such enterprises. An underlying premise of this course is that most students in the class will, at some point in their careers, have the interest and/or opportunity to work on social issues either within a business or as a board member, volunteer, or staff leader of a nonprofit organization. The course enables students to both examine the applicability of for-profit business approaches to social challenges and to expand their knowledge of effective management practices in the complex environment in which nonprofits operate. As a result, students should become better prepared to achieve social objectives as leaders in business, the social sector, or the public sector. By means of cases, guest speakers, and discussions, the course will address such issues as social entrepreneurship, capacity building, managing multi-site organizations, capital markets for social enterprises, business-nonprofit partnerships, branding, social marketing, performance measurement, nonprofit governance and venture philanthropy.

title: Ethics in Journalism
description: Spring 2009 The MPS program aims to educate the whole person and believes that this process starts with the study of ethics. Looking at ethics through a professional lens, this course asks students to explore their own values and challenges them to codify and commit to their own code of ethics in relationship to professional codes of conduct and best practices. Students will gain a solid foundation in ethics and relate what they learn to their own professional situation through class discussions, case studies, and guest lectures conducted by Georgetown University ethicists as well as communications professionals. This course is required for all MPS students and must be completed by the second semester in the program. Class open to journalism students in the MPS only.

title: Feature Writing
description: Summer 2009 This course will explore the art of short-form, creative nonfiction--a favorite of readers and writers alike. Students will be introduced to techniques for effectively brainstorming ideas. They will be coached through the reporting and writing process and participate in peer review editing workshops. Through class discussions, students will also be introduced to the habits, practices and process of professional reporting and writing. By the end of the semester, students will have acquired more tools to transform a conversation about sometimes fleeting cultural phenomena into a window into the times we are living in. Class open to journalism students in the MPS only.

title: Feature Writing
description: Summer 2009 This course will explore the art of short-form, creative nonfiction--a favorite of readers and writers alike. Students will be introduced to techniques for effectively brainstorming ideas. They will be coached through the reporting and writing process and participate in peer review editing workshops. Through class discussions, students will also be introduced to the habits, practices and process of professional reporting and writing. By the end of the semester, students will have acquired more tools to transform a conversation about sometimes fleeting cultural phenomena into a window into the times we are living in. Class open to journalism students in the MPS only.

title: Journalism of Conscience
description: Spring 2009 As a journalist, you have the power to shine a light on under-reported people and issues. You will use that power in this feature writing workshop to report and write a substantive long-form article that reflects the journalism of conscience. We will explore the craft of writers whose stories give voice to the voiceless including Katherine Boo, Adrian Nicole LeBlanc and Alex Kotlowitz. From story pitch to final period, we will use the workshop format to give intensive feedback to help each student produce a publishable story. There will be several guest speakers and shorter writing exercises. Class open to journalism students in the MPS only.

title: Journalism of Conscience
description: Spring 2009 As a journalist, you have the power to shine a light on under-reported people and issues. You will use that power in this feature writing workshop to report and write a substantive long-form article that reflects the journalism of conscience. We will explore the craft of writers whose stories give voice to the voiceless including Katherine Boo, Adrian Nicole LeBlanc and Alex Kotlowitz. From story pitch to final period, we will use the workshop format to give intensive feedback to help each student produce a publishable story. There will be several guest speakers and shorter writing exercises. Class open to journalism students in the MPS only.

title: Journalism of Identity
description: Spring 2009 By mid-century, the United States will be majority minority, bringing the personal and cultural narratives of a much wider population into sharper focus. This course will explore how journalists can turn the specific personal narrative into a deeper commentary that allows everyone to explore issues of diversity, connection, fear and alienation. It will encourage students to mine their personal journeys, develop listening and discernment skills to probe the stories of others, and find points of universal agreement and connection in a society that often seems to be moving toward fraction and balkanization. Through writing exercises, class discussions and workshopping specific pieces, and guest speakers to represent a variety of diverse experiences, students will be encouraged toward frank and honest discussion that fosters cultural dexterity and a greater ability to move as journalists through an ever more disparate nation. Class open to journalism students in the MPS only.

title: Expeditions, Executions & ExposÃ©s: Place and Personality in Travel, Crime and Investigative Reporting
description: Injecting a sense of "place" and "personality" as a "character" in a story is essential to effective, award-winning journalism, especially in travel writing, crime/terrorism and criminal justice reporting and investigative journalism. Students will learn this craft by studying historical and contemporary illustrative pieces, and analyzing the skills of writers, reporters, producers, commentators and adventure chroniclers such as Stanley, Capote, Kerouac, Mencken, Twain, Walter White, Sebastian Junger, John Berendt, Barbara Erhenreich, Marie Brenner, Don Hewitt, Jack Anderson, Harriet Washington, Barton Gellman, Carl Bernstein & Bob Woodward, Edward R. Murrow, Paul Theroux and others. Source materials will include exemplary pieces from National Geographic and National Geographic Traveler; historical and contemporary narrative nonfiction; true crime articles and coverage of selected local, national and international crime/criminal trials; major fraud, "muckraking" and investigative articles and exposÃ©s. Students will prepare a short mock piece or television narrative copy in the crime and investigative reporting areas, a brief analytical research paper and a final publication-ready travel piece.

title: Business Reporting
description: This course teaches students the range of skills needed for smart reporting on business, financial markets, the economy and the private sector. Topics include business excesses and failures such as the recent housing-sector meltdown; the vitally important relationships between business, government and society; and emerging trends in the U.S. and world economies. The class will feature frequent guest speakers, including figures from the business world and government, as well as some of today's best reporters and editors. Materials will come primarily from newspapers and magazines but will include TV and film, as well as reports, public filings and academic texts. Skills to be emphasized include story conceptualization; source development and interview techniques; use of official records; and writing. Course requirements will include several short pieces and a final project. There are no formal prerequisites, but some reporting background is recommended. Students also will need energy, enthusiasm and insatiable curiosity about the real world.

title: The Art of the Interview: A Wary Seduction
description: A face-to-face interview is one of the most basic, most meaningful types of research, especially for journalists and authors of books and articles dealing with current affairs and real-life issues. The same applies to documentary filmmakers, and even to people who write PR. In all these fields, you need to gather information directly from people â not from documents or reports, but orally. This course will address how to research an interview, what to expect from an interview, how to conduct an interview, the ethics of an interview, and how to distill information from an interview and write it up in a coherent, compelling fashion. Taught by a journalist whose interviews include Louis Farrakhan, Peter Jennings, Allen Ginsberg, Joseph Heller, Cat Stevens, Jerzy Kosinski, Roz Chast, Calvin Trillin and the Dalai Lama.

Taught by a journalist whose interviews include Louis Farrakhan, Peter Jennings, Allen Ginsberg, Joseph Heller, Cat Stevens, Jerzy Kosinski, Roz Chast, Calvin Trillin and the Dalai Lama.

title: Digging Up Controversy--Investigative Reporting and the Law
description: Spring 2009 How do great journalists put together groundbreaking, high-impact stories? And how do they use the law to both uncover secrets and protect themselves from lawsuits? This class combines methodology and law for state-of-the-art investigative reporting. Students will focus on the tools and techniques of in-depth journalism â pursuing paper (and digital) trails, back-grounding individuals and companies, following the money, searching the âdeep Web,â and more. Because reporters increasingly publish on their own websites or freelance without a newsroom lawyer behind them, knowledge of the fundamentals of media law and how they apply to investigative journalism (including FOIA, libel and invasion of privacy, subpoena defense, eavesdropping and use of hidden cameras) is essential for todayâs media entrepreneurs. This team-taught class will be led by media attorney Bruce Brown and investigative journalist David Kaplan. Class open to journalism students in the MPS only.

title: Computer Assisted Reporting
description: Spring 2009 Students will learn the basics of computer-assisted reporting, including the acquisition, analysis and presentation of electronic data for news and enterprise stories on multiple delivery platforms. Course content will include hands-on technical training leading to proficiency in spreadsheet and relational database applications. We'll explore strategic use of free and fee-based Internet resources and search techniques, and how Freedom of Information laws relate to data acquisition. We'll also examine the journalistic decisions and values that accompany data-oriented stories, including communicating appropriate levels of precision and uncertainty in narratives, polls and graphics, and using data ethically. For certain topics, we'll take an overview approach aimed at essential understanding and readiness for additional learning. These topics include GIS mapping, Google Maps, Flash and server-based searchable database applications. Instruction will be augmented with case studies, written materials and real-world examples. By the end of the course, students will be required to acquire a database, determine its journalistic merits and perform a basic analysis. Students will write a detailed story memo based on the analysis. Class open to journalism students in the MPS only.

title: Introduction to Video Production
description: Spring 2008/Fall 2008 This course will equip students with the skills needed to communicate effectively in the medium of video. Students will learn techniques and aesthetics of photography, computer editing, basic script writing and segment producing. Students will learn the skills that will enable them to tell a story with a video camera, and have fun while doing it! Grades for the semester will be determined by quizzes, homework assignments, class participation, and video projects. Students will conceptualize, write, photograph, and edit several short "films", which will reflect the students' creativity and technical abilities as the semester progresses. These video projects will also provide a body of work for each student to utilize as resume material when they enter a professional video-production working environment. Class open to journalism students in the MPS only.

title: Advanced Digital Video Production
description: Spring 2009 This course will enhance and broaden the studentâs basic video production skills in video photography, non-linear editing and segment producing. Working with digital video production tools, students will individually create sophisticated video projects. As an exercise for a corporate video client, the class will create a complete television program. **Prerequisite: âMPJO-725 Intro To Video Productionâ required; Students with prior video production experience accepted subject to approval by instructor.

title: Crafting Narrative Non-Fiction
description: Spring 2008/Fall 2008 What do David Sedaris, Jon Krakauer, Michael Lewis, and Jeannette Walls have in common? They all have enjoyed immense success in the most popular style of writing in today's market: narrative nonfiction. The class will cover all the major genres of narrative non-fiction: web writing, short and extended magazine articles, and books. It will delve into the most popular forms of narrative writing, from historical to adventure to food and travel to the extended essay and business. Students will read from a diverse list of journalists, authors, pundits, and teachers. They will learn to identify what makes an idea the best idea for a piece. They will also learn to craft tone and voice according to their style, their intended audience, and their venue. They will learn why narrative non-fiction relies so heavily on counter-intuition, alternative points of view, and above all else, credentials. Class open to journalism students in the MPS only.

title: Crafting Narrative Non-Fiction
description: Spring 2008/Fall 2008 What do David Sedaris, Jon Krakauer, Michael Lewis, and Jeannette Walls have in common? They all have enjoyed immense success in the most popular style of writing in today's market: narrative nonfiction. The class will cover all the major genres of narrative non-fiction: web writing, short and extended magazine articles, and books. It will delve into the most popular forms of narrative writing, from historical to adventure to food and travel to the extended essay and business. Students will read from a diverse list of journalists, authors, pundits, and teachers. They will learn to identify what makes an idea the best idea for a piece. They will also learn to craft tone and voice according to their style, their intended audience, and their venue. They will learn why narrative non-fiction relies so heavily on counter-intuition, alternative points of view, and above all else, credentials. Class open to journalism students in the MPS only.

title: Journalism Upside Down
description: Spring 2009 The newspaper world is changing fast. Readership and advertising revenues are shrinking nationwide. Newspaper publishers are struggling to develop internet outlets, and to find ways to make money offering on-line media products. Meanwhile, the blogosphere is mushrooming, forcing sweeping changes, and making it necessary to rethink and redefine fundamental concepts in journalism: What is news today? Who defines what news is? How do we sort out respected authority? How do we balance the flood of information available with the ephemerality of that information? This seminar will begin by setting the transition in journalism into a historical perspective, reading classic journalistic texts by Walter Lippman and John Dewey. We will rely on writings by modern media observers to track the rise of blogs, their impacts on news-gathering and dissemination, and the dangers posed. How do blogs like The Huffington Post promote democracy in journalism? How do those same blogs dampen opportunities for â and the publicâs interest in â original reporting? What kinds of job options arise â or disappearâas newspapers give way to blogs and other forms of on-line media? Students will be encouraged to write for blogs, as well as for traditional newspaper and magazine outlets, and in addition, to develop critical perspectives on blogs, analyzing the ways in which internet-based media outlets are changing the face of journalism.

title: Media and Social Justice: International Investigative Project
description: Nomani, Asra; Feinman Todd, Barbara Media and Social Justice: International Investigative Project Graduate course #: MPJO 752 Fall 2008 A select group of undergraduate and graduate students will work collaboratively in a newsroom setting, in the spirit of the 2007-2008 Pearl Project investigation that sought answers regarding the 2002 kidnapping and murder of Wall Street Journal reporter Daniel Pearl in Karachi, Pakistan. Similarly, this yearâs course will seek answers regarding the death of another slain journalist, as yet not chosen, or a journalist whose life and work is in peril. Applicants to the class are asked to submit a 500-word essay suggesting either (1) a journalist who died in the line of duty whose murder, as well as unfinished journalistic endeavors, could become the classâs focus of investigation, or (2) a journalist in peril whose unfinished journalistic work could become the classâs focus of investigation. The class project will seek to answer a number of questions. In the case of a journalist who has been killed: Who killed the journalist? Why did they kill him or her? What are the underlying international and domestic politics surrounding the case? What unfinished work was left behind to be tackled? In the case of a journalist in peril: Why is the journalist being targeted? Who is targeting the journalist? What are the underlying politics surrounding the case? What is the work of the journalist still be to completed? The project will also seek to look at the wider relationship between the press and the government and political climate of the journalistâs nation state. Led by Visiting Professor in the Practice of Journalism Asra Nomani and Associate Dean of Journalism Barbara Feinman Todd, this class will teach the investigative reporting tools necessary to do the patient work that an in-depth endeavor such as this requires, including the techniques of computer-assisted journalism. Students follow leads that come in from the public as well as pursue sources among the appropriate intelligence and law enforcement communities. The class's progress will be chronicled in various media forms including a website and video journal and the results of its reporting will be published. Permission by instructor only. Interested students should submit their 500-word essay to aqn@georgetown.edu and to feinmanb@georgetown.edu as soon as possible. Deadline: June 1, 2008. This course is very rigorous and requires an intense time commitment. Credits: 3 Prerequisites: None though âIntroduction to Journalismâ (ENGL 470) for undergrad applicants strongly recommended. Participants of the 2007-08 Pearl Project cannot enroll in this course.

title: Covering Global Health
description: Spring 2009 The aim of this course is to introduce you to global public health and medical reporting and provide you with some of the basic skills you'll need to specialize in this rich and growing field of journalism. You do not need a background in science or medicine to cover global public health, but you'll need to understand basic concepts in medical science, cultural diversity, politics and health policy. We'll examine the globalization of disease. For instance, as HIV, West Nile Virus and SARS migrate from developing nations to Europe and the U.S., heart disease and other illnesses of prosperity are spreading in developing countries that are adopting Westernized lifestyles. The course will be hands-on. You will be asked to select a beat, write stories based on research in medical journals and complete a reporting and writing project. Guest speakers will include leaders in the fields of public health and journalism. Class open to journalism students in the MPS only.

title: Covering Capitol Hill
description: Spring 2009 Paul Singer Covering Capitol Hill Students will essentially serve as Congressional correspondents for the semester. Class presentations will include discussions of the procedures of Congress, what members of Congress actually do all day, the confluence of money and politics and techniques for reporting on the people, the institution and the process. Emphasis will be given to understanding the interests of your readers when determining what is really newsworthy. Students are expected to cover events on Capitol Hill, and to keep up with news and issues on their "beat." Class open to journalism students in the MPS only.

title: Sports Video Production
description: This course is designed to develop advanced skills in the three forms of Sports Video Production: event coverage, highlights and feature packages. The course will be hands on. Examples will be set up to shoot on class nights and assignments will be given for the students to shoot during the week. The students will edit their shot pieces. The class will also develop skills in shooting and conducting interviews with athletes especially after competition. There is an expectation of basic camera and editing skills but instructions and help will be available. Students will be graded on their projects, attendance and class participation.

title: Sports Video Production
description: This course is designed to develop advanced skills in the three forms of Sports Video Production: event coverage, highlights and feature packages. The course will be hands on. Examples will be set up to shoot on class nights and assignments will be given for the students to shoot during the week. The students will edit their shot pieces. The class will also develop skills in shooting and conducting interviews with athletes especially after competition. There is an expectation of basic camera and editing skills but instructions and help will be available. Students will be graded on their projects, attendance and class participation.

title: Multimedia- Theory and Practice
description: Spring 2009 This class will examine multimedia creation for the online community. We will look at interactive content from the joint perspectives of why and how - focusing on the cultural influences that shape and inform our multimedia choices. Works of William Gibson, Jessica Helfand, John Maeda, Hillman Curtis, Ira Glass, W. Eugene Smith, and Elaine Ullman will be discussed. The class will examine multiple media through literature and essays, and then apply our findings to the act of content creation; photography, audio storytelling and video being our primary means of expression. Our goal will be to apply the non-linear art of experimentation and discovery to the linear art of sharing information online. The class will be conducted in a seminar format, relying on discussion to move our examination of multimedia forward. There will also be instruction in several content creation tools and students are expected to use non-class time to master these. Additionally, students will be expected to use these tools to create a complete multimedia project as the final examination for this class. Class open to journalism students in the MPS only.

title: Web Soup: Creating Content in The Digital Age
description: This course will cover and explore the fundamentals of content creation in the digital world. From creating offline/online content packages for mainstream media, to Web-only content efforts, to multimedia, to creating and marketing your own persona on the Web, this course is intended to give students a broad and deep understanding of the content, technology, marketing and business basics of Web and mobile content. Students will learn how to communicate effectively online, build content packages that enhance and expand print, radio and television programming, and how to create and use video and other multimedia. Included with be overviews of creating and engaging in âwebisodesâ, blogs, lifestreaming and social networks. The course will also review the core principles of marketing content on the Web, from Search Engine Optimization to link building to viral campaigns, as well as an overview of the spectrum of ways businesses monetize their content.

title: Web Soup: Creating Content in The Digital Age
description: This course will cover and explore the fundamentals of content creation in the digital world. The course will also review the core principles of marketing content on the Web, from Search Engine Optimization to link building to viral campaigns, as well as an overview of the spectrum of ways businesses monetize their content.

title: Media Techniques
description: Spring 2009 This elective is for students who will be participating in an internship at a local media outlet (print, broadcast or Internet) during the summer or fall. It is the only way for students to receive credit for a media internship. This workshop-style course will give students an opportunity to advance their reporting and writing skills while sharing their internship experiences with other students, both graduate and undergraduate, engaged in similar journalistic pursuits. Written assignments will include a semester-long journal, two reported articles, and a final project that directly grows out of the internship experience. Students will have the opportunity to post their stories on a GU journalism web site. Required readings will focus on issues common to any journalistic endeavor such as ethics and fairness and the future of the profession in the new media era. Guest speakers will include prominent local journalists. Students must arrange the internship before enrolling in the course.

title: Broadcast Investigative Reporting Project
description: Students will participate in an Investigative Reporting project with working professionals who will foster the enterprise from inception to publication. Students will help select the topic or topics and then assist in the discovery of related documents and data. The entire course will be devoted to teaching the practical aspects of investigative reporting while actually preparing an investigative report for broadcast on WJLA-TV. Students will learn research skills, writing for broadcast, journalistic ethics, and interviewing skills. They will be expected to follow paper trails, mine information from human sources, search the Internet and learn the value of computer-assisted journalistic research. They will also be introduced to basic libel, defamation and copyright laws. They will be exposed to the photography, graphic presentation and video editing required in the production of a top level broadcast investigative story. Noteworthy professional journalists will appear as occasional guest lectures. The course will culminate with the broadcast publication of the project along with a sidebar explainer of the role students played in the project. Class is open to anyone approved by the Associate Dean of Journalism.

title: Broadcast Investigative Reporting Project
description: Students will participate in an Investigative Reporting project with working professionals who will foster the enterprise from inception to publication. Students will help select the topic or topics and then assist in the discovery of related documents and data. The entire course will be devoted to teaching the practical aspects of investigative reporting while actually preparing an investigative report for broadcast on WJLA-TV. Students will learn research skills, writing for broadcast, journalistic ethics, and interviewing skills. They will be expected to follow paper trails, mine information from human sources, search the Internet and learn the value of computer-assisted journalistic research. They will also be introduced to basic libel, defamation and copyright laws. They will be exposed to the photography, graphic presentation and video editing required in the production of a top level broadcast investigative story. Noteworthy professional journalists will appear as occasional guest lectures. The course will culminate with the broadcast publication of the project along with a sidebar explainer of the role students played in the project. Class is open to anyone approved by the Associate Dean of Journalism.

title: Storytelling 2.0
description: "Storytelling 2.0" is an interdisciplinary course designed to teach students the process for creating compelling narratives and the tools for promoting them in the current media environment. Students will work with an Emmy-award winning filmmaker, Pulitzer Prize-winning journalist, and prominent Entertainment PR strategist on a documentary film project focusing on the HIV/AIDS epidemic here in Washington, D.C. Like the year-long Washington Post series on which it is based -- read the articles -- the film will give a face to the epidemic in our nation's capital, which is currently experiencing the same rate of new HIV infection as Angola and the Congo. Most people are unaware that the District has the highest rate of HIV/AIDS of any capital city in the world. The film will also serve as a call to action. Students will participate in the different phases of the film-making process; they will also help create a Web site and develop a festival, press, outreach and marketing strategy for the project. Students will be given concrete assignments directly related to the making and marketing of the documentary. Students will be expected to do online and field research, assist with basic film production on location, utilize basic interviewing skills, and create and provide content for the Web site. The hands-on documentary work will be accompanied by watching and discussing documentary films with emphasis on understanding and learning about the basic principles of storytelling and film-making. Class discussions, which will include guest lecturers from the film community, will focus on such topics as 1/ What makes a good story 2/ Developing characters and plot 3/ The legal and business aspects of documentary filmmaking 4/ The use of new media in documentary filmmaking 5/ Using documentary films to effect social change 6/The current state of the documentary marketplace and 7/How to package and promote a film project throughout its entire life-cycle. Class open to MPS and CCT students only

title: Open/ Shut Project
description: Spring 2009 Students will examine the disruptive impact of "open source" thinking on industries from journalism to politics to education to business. Over the course of the semester, studentsâbased on their own career goals and expertiseâwill research, develop, and write case studies that examine the emerging demands for the "TAO of openness": Transparency, Accountability, and a sense of Ownership. As part of the class, students will use and collaborate with social media tools ranging from blogs, wikis, and social networking to podcasts, video, and more, so knowledge of these areas is a prerequisite. The answers to the "Age of Open-ism" lie in the declining costs of technology and the ease of communication in a wired world. The demand for Open-ism has always existed but the costs of achieving that have been too high. Today all those barriers are gone. There is no excuse not to be openâunless you're making a fundamental choice to turn your back on the future. Students must apply to enroll the class. Please apply by emailing Garrett Graff (gmg33@georgetown.edu) before [December 1st] a short statement (300 - 400 words) explaining your interest in the class, your background, and subject areas you'd be interested in exploring. Please also cite any previous SCS classes that have included social media. Class open to journalism students in the MPS only.

title: Digital Campaigns
description: Summer/Fall 2008 The First Campaign: Covering Presidential Politics in the Internet Age This course will cover and explore the first presidential campaign of the information age, from the revolution in online fundraising to voter-generated content. Beginning with a brief overview of technology's role in political history and the technological advances that have transformed the world's playing field in the early 21st Century, this course will cover topics from the Ron Paul blimp to the Obama "Yes We Can" video, as well as introducing students to technologies like Twitter, Second Life, and mobile campaigning. Aimed at aspiring political reporters and junkies, this course will introduce students to the groundbreaking texts and personalities that have ushered in a new era in presidential campaigns. While primarily domestically focused, this course will include a unit on international politics and the use of technology, as well as e-government, citizen journalism on the campaign trail, and the role of bloggers as watchdogs of democracy. Highlighted skills will include effective online organizing and advocacy as well as online communications from email to blogging. Students will be required to participate in the social web and to read extensively on their own.

title: Digital Campaigns
description: Summer/Fall 2008 The First Campaign: Covering Presidential Politics in the Internet Age This course will cover and explore the first presidential campaign of the information age, from the revolution in online fundraising to voter-generated content. Beginning with a brief overview of technology's role in political history and the technological advances that have transformed the world's playing field in the early 21st Century, this course will cover topics from the Ron Paul blimp to the Obama "Yes We Can" video, as well as introducing students to technologies like Twitter, Second Life, and mobile campaigning. Students will be required to participate in the social web and to read extensively on their own.

title: Capstone
