It provides an introduction to the specialty of public health nursing and public health sciences. Public health and nursing concepts provide the framework for the study and care of communities/populations with an emphasis on health promotion, protection, and disease prevention in communities and populations.

title: Complex Nursing Problems Across the Care Continuum
description: The Focus of this course is on the therapeutic and restorative care of client with Nursing problems of increasing complexity across the care continuum. Students will collaborate with members of the health care team in planning, implementing and evaluating care to clients with complex needs. Students will synthesize curriculum concepts to guide decision-making for individuals and families. Clinical experience will be provided in complex acute care settings with a related home health care experience.

title: Complex Problems Across the Care Continuum II
description: The focus of this course is the continuation of the discussion on the therapeutic and restorative care of clients with nursing problems of increasing complexity across the care continuum. Students will synthesize curriculum concepts to guide decision making for clients of specific populations.

title: Complex Problems Across the Care Continuum II
description: The focus of this course is the continuation of the discussion on the therapeutic and restorative care of clients with nursing problems of increasing complexity across the care continuum. Students will synthesize curriculum concepts to guide decision making for clients of specific populations.

title: Nursing Care of Vulnerable Populations
description: This theory/service learning course challenges graduating seniors to implement the professional competencies they have developed to date as they deliver care to a vulnerable population as a means of addressing inequities in the local health care delivery system. The aim of the course is to identify what cultures and infrastructures perpetuate health care inequities and to discuss the nursing leadership interventions that might contribute to a resolution of these inequities. Students will use the clinical portion of the course to utilize and evaluate a strategic initiative to address currently ignored or underserved health needs of an identified vulnerable population. The course will develop the studentâs ability to analyze contemporary health needs and problems, strategize to resolve recurrent problems, and demonstrate the difference professional nursing leadership makes. The Georgetown Nursing, Values Based Health Care Model will serve as a guide in developing nursing leadership interventions at both the clinical level and in the health care system.

title: Transitions to Professional Practice
description: This course builds upon the core nursing courses and prepares the student for the various roles and responsibilities of the professional nurse. The course examines leadership and management theories and their application to the role of nurse as care coordinator, charge nurse and leader. Leadership skills with emphasis on team building, interdisciplinary collaboration, change, conflict resolution, negotiation, and delegation are discussed. Clinical experiences will provide students the opportunity to apply theory to practice. Additionally, the course will prepare the student for transitions to independent practice and licensure through critical review of nursing knowledge, coordinated review and submission of the licensure application.

title: Nursing Care of Vulnerable Populations
description: This seminar challenges graduating seniors to examine and reflect on professional competencies they have developed to date. The aim of the course is to identify what cultures and infrastructures perpetuate health care inequities and to discuss the nursing leadership interventions that might contribute to a resolution of these inequities. The course will develop the studentâs ability to analyze contemporary health needs and problems, strategize to resolve recurrent problems, and discuss the difference professional nursing leadership makes.

title: Ethics, Quality and Decision-making in Nursing
description: NURS 254 builds upon previous coursework in the nursing major and is intended to be a forum in which the student integrates concepts relevant to professional judgment and decision-making. Using a case study and discussion format students will be guided through contemporary challenges in healthcare encountered by professional nurses that require critical thinking and problem solving skills. The course will bring into alignment the studentâs previous coursework in ethics, evidence-based practice, quality care standards, policy, social justice, and experiential learning as a framework for evaluating initiatives that affect health status and care delivery. The course will assist the student to synthesize knowledge and skills that supports decision-making in the health care environment ranging from clinical care sites to public policy.

title: Senior Nursing Practicum
description: The goal of this final undergraduate clinical practicum is to demonstrate the studentâs achievement of knowledge and skills in nursing practice as they enter into professional practice. Clinical experiences in N. 256 include seven weeks of practice in an area of student interest with guided but minimal faculty supervision followed by seven weeks of practice under the guidance of an agency preceptor. Clinical experiences in N. 257 include 84 hours of clinical under the guidance of an agency preceptor. Students integrate knowledge and skills from the humanities and basic, behavioral, social, leadership and nursing sciences in developing the professional role in a clinical area of special interest.

title: Senior Nursing Practicum
description: The goal of this final undergraduate clinical practicum demonstrates the student's achievement of knowledge and skills in nursing practice as they enter into professional practice. Clinical experiences include seven weeks of practice under the guidance of an agency preceptor. As assigned NHS clinical faculty member (2) will complete at least two site visits with the student and the agency preceptor during the semester. Students integrate knowledge and skills from the humanities and basic, behavioral, social leadership and nursing sciences in developing the professional role in a clinical area of special interest.

title: Honors Thesis
description: This seminar is designed to challenge upper division honors students in nursing who have achieved a 3.3 cumulative GPA by the end of the sophomore year and demonstrated the potential for honors level scholarship. Discussion will center on the significance of values and models in guiding healthcare professionals and shaping the health care delivery system in the U.S. or abroad. The student will work with a faculty mentor who is an expert in the subject matter chosen for the studentâs Honors Thesis.

title: Honors Thesis
description: This one credit guided tutorial will allow the nursing honors student to further develop the area of interest he/she has identified under the guidance of a faculty mentor.

title: Child and Adolescent Behavioral Health in Primary Care
description: This is a theory based elective. Enrollees need not have a Child & Adolescent Psychiatric/Mental Health clinical practice background. Upper division undergraduate students must have the permission of the faculty to enroll in the course. The focus will be on providing a beginning understanding of clinical assessment, diagnosis and management strategies for common behavioral health concerns encountered in primary care settings treating 5-18 year olds. Relevant theories, evidence-based practice, culturally congruent principles, psychopharmacological and psychotherapeutic interventions grounded in a health promotion framework will be presented. **This course does not provide the enrollee with skills making them eligible to diagnose in the specialty of child and adolescent psychiatric-mental health nursing.

title: Anatomy for Health Care Professionals
description: This course provides the student with an overview of the human body by the exploration of the human dissected cadaver. The students will examine organ systems by region following a brief lecture on the topic. The lecture will focus on the relationship between form and function, while the lab will allow the student to relate his/her previous clinical knowledge to the 3-dimensional aspects of anatomy.

title: Introduction to Reproductive Healthcare for Women
description: Introduction to well woman gynecology and care of the pregnant woman. Appropriate anatomy, physiology, pathophysiology and common deviations from normal are taught. Includes conduct of annual examinations, assessment and management of STDs, contraceptive methods, preconception care and prenatal care and management in the ambulatory setting. No Prerequisites.

title: PRIMARY CARE OF WOMEN
description: The student builds on the assessment skills acquired in Advanced Health Assessment. A review of the major organ systems is carried out with emphasis on common disorders that are managed in the primary health care setting. Open to Nurse-Midwifery Program students only

title: Advanced Health Assessment
description: The focus of this course is the pre-anesthesia evaluation of the adult patient. The student nurse anesthetist will utilize a holistic approach to patient evaluation, including history taking and physical assessment, in order to analyze the patient's health state and anesthetic risk. This information will be integrated with other aspects of the surgical procedure and knowledge gained from the course co-requisites; physiology, pathophysiology, and pharmacology in order to prepare for and deliver a safe anesthetic. The laboratory component will utilize simulation experiences to reinforce concepts covered within this course and covered within the co-requisites listed above.

title: Gross Anatomy
description: This course introduces students to the anatomy of the human body by presenting the fundamental concept of the body as an interrelated set of organ systems. Students will examine these systems in the laboratory through the dissection and observation of the human cadaver. Lectures will present the relationship between form and function. Although all organ systems will be covered, special emphasis will be given to the head, neck and thorax, the respiratory, cardiovascular, and nervous systems.

title: Lab - Fundamentals of Human Physiology
description: The goal of the Fundamentals of Human Physiology course is to provide the student with a basic understanding of the physiological basis of medicine. the essential concepts of physiology and mechanisms of body function are presented at various levels of organization ranging from the cellular and molecular, to the tissue and organ system level. Emphasis is also placed on understanding the integrated regulation of various body processes among several systems.

title: Health and Illness Education
description: This course applies the principles of teaching and learning to the education of healthcare clients and their families. Emphasis is placed on meeting individuals where they are, determining their readiness for learning, and working as a partner with them to establish what they need and want to know and how they learn best. Learner characteristics are explored in relationship to: age, development, health and stress of illness, socioeconomic issues, education & literacy, family functioning, motivation to learn, and culture. Establishing educational programs in institutional settings and community settings are explored. Models of both health promotion/disease prevention and disease management are discussed. Interdisciplinary collaboration in healthcare education is emphasized. Student learning experiences will include a community-based learning component in which students will amplify their class learning by working collaboratively with a community group to plan an educational project that meets their needs.

title: Chemistry and Physiology of Biological Processes
description: This course examines basic concepts in biochemistry and molecular biology particularly germane to students in the health professions. The course aims to provide the student with an overview of the biomolecules involved in energetics, metabolism, signal transduction, and protein synthesis.

title: Advanced Health Assessment
description: This advanced health assessment course covers content for students in advanced nursing practice specialties with a clinical component. Content includes theory and clinical practice in health history, risk appraisal, physical and mental status, psychosocial assessment, and nutrition assessment with an introduction to disease prevention. Health assessment skills are presented for all age groups from the newborn through aged populations. Supervised clinical experience is provided.

title: Analytical Tools & Research Applications
description: This course provides students with an overview of the research process, including topic selection relevant to nursing, identification of research questions and hypotheses and ethical issues. Students will complete a literature review and develop background and significance for their project. Content builds on principles of scientific thinking and concepts in research as they pertain to evidence based practice and translational research in nursing. The student is introduced to quantitative and qualitative methods and analysis including common non-parametric and parametric statistical tests. An experience with data analysis using an established data set and SPSS is provided.

title: Professional Aspects of Advanced Practice Nursing
description: This course focuses on advanced roles for professional nursing practice. Emphasis is on philosophical inquiry, theory analysis, and the development of leadership skills for working with individuals and groups in advanced nursing practice.

title: Advanced Concepts in Pharmacology
description: This course focuses on the pharmacologic concepts important to advanced practice nurses. These concepts include receptor theory, receptor-effector coupling, dose- response relationships, mechanisms of action of important classes of drugs, pharmaco- kinetics, and pharmacodynamics. Course content is geared toward providing the student with an understanding of basic pharmacologic principles that can be applied to new drugs as they become available in the future. Application of these principles to real-world situations will also be highlighted.

title: Advanced Concepts in Physiology and Pathophysiology
description: This course presupposes a knowledge of human anatomy and undergraduate physiology. Subjects are covered from a molecular, cellular, tissue, organ, and system perspective, and related to issues of nursing practice. Since an exhaustive treatment of physiology is not possible in the time allotted, the philosophy of this course is to impart concepts of pathophysiology which can be used by the student to study any aspect of pathophysiology they might wish to acquire in the future. Emphasis is also placed on acquiring skill in the use of scientific literature and in conceptualizing information gained from many sources.

title: Health Systems & Policies in Transition
description: This course examines the complex political, economic, and social forces that shape health care systems. It provides an introduction to general principles of health care organization, financing and regulation, and then sets forth a dynamic comparison of various approaches to health care delivery systems in different parts of the world. Current policy initiatives are analyzed for their impact on cost, quality, access and the health of populations. Students develop beginning skills in the epidemiological approach to population-based health care systems.

title: Advanced Concepts in Pharmacology
description: This course focuses on the pharmacologic concepts important to nurse anesthesia practice. These concepts include receptor theory, receptor-effector coupling, dose-response relationships, mechanisms of action of important classes of drugs, pharmaco-kinetics, and pharmacodynamics. Course content is geared toward providing the student with an understanding of basic pharmacologic principles that can be applied to new drugs as they become available in the future. Application of these principles to real-world situations will also be highlighted.

title: Pathophysiology for Anesthesia Practice
description: This course presupposes a knowledge of biochemistry, human anatomy and physiology and focuses on representative aberrations in normal physiology which produce disease. Subjects are covered from an organ and system basis. Emphasis is also devoted to acquiring skill in the use of scientific literature and conceptualizing information gained from many sources to develop a basic understanding of the diseases discussed.

title: Advanced Principles of Nurse Anesthesia Practice I
description: This Advanced Principles of Nurse Anesthesia course is designed to expand on the students' knowledge obtained from Basic Principles of Nurse Anesthesia Practice, Anesthesia Pharmacology and Advanced Pathophysiology. The course examines anesthetic and nursing considerations for patients with pathophysiologic disruptions requiring surgical interventions in orthopedic, otorhinolaryngologic, renal and urologic disorders utilizing conventional as well as laparoscopic and laser techniques. The course will also provide insights into the anesthetic management for patients with difficult airways, acute and chronic pain, undergoing regional, general and monitored anesthesia care, recovering from anesthesia. Special considerations for pediatric, elderly, outpatient, obstetric patient and anesthetics delivered in remote locations, and newborns requiring resuscitation are also explored. The course is taught during the clinical practicum and allows the student to integrate advanced physiologic and nursing knowledge, skills and critical thinking into their daily practice.

title: Advanced Principles of Nurse Anesthesia Practice II
description: This Advanced Principles of Nurse Anesthesia course is designed to expand on the student's knowledge obtained from Basic Principles of Nurse Anesthesia Practice, Anesthesia Pharmacology and Advanced Pathophysiology. The course examines anesthetic and nursing considerations for patients with pathophysiologic disruptions requiring surgical interventions of neurosurgical, vascular, thoracic, cardiac disorders utilizing conventional as well as advanced neurosurgical, cardiovascular, respiratory monitoring. Cerebral, cardiac and respiratory physiology is examined in depth. Appropriate utilization of crystalloids and colloids is differentiated. Special considerations regarding anesthetic depth, pulmonary function, respiratory care and acid base balance are also explored. The course is taught during the clinical practicum and allows the student to integrate advanced physiologic and nursing knowledge, skills and critical thinking into their daily practice.

title: Advanced Principles of Nurse Anesthesia Practice IV
description: This Advanced Principles of Nurse Anesthesia course is designed to expand on the students' knowledge obtained from Basic Principles of Nurse Anesthesia Practice, Anesthesia Pharmacology and Advanced Pathophysiology. The course examines anesthetic and nursing considerations for patients with pathophysiologic disruptions requiring surgical interventions utilizing standard as well as advanced methods of anesthesia care. The course will also provide insight into the anesthetic management for patients utilizing pharmacologic principles, particularly barbiturate, non-barbiturate, neuromuscular blockade and antagonism, opioid and inhalation agents. Principles related to patient safety involving monitoring, positioning, environmental, ethical and legal factors are explored. Historical and professional aspects of nurse anesthesia practice are examined. The course is taught during the clinical practicum and allows the student to integrate advanced physiologic and nursing knowledge, skills and critical thinking into their daily practice.

title: Anesthesia Concepts Seminar I
description: This introductory course focuses on concepts and theories relative to the practice of nurse anesthesia. An integration of anesthetic concepts with the knowledge base gained in the courses of Gross Anatomy and Chemistry provides students with a beginning foundation to: assess patient status, begin decision making relative to the holistic care of the patient undergoing surgery and/or diagnostic procedures, and understand the rationale for the implementation of scientifically based anesthetic regimes.

title: Acute Care Advanced Practice Nurse I - Advanced Assessment and Clinical Decision Making for Acute Care Advanced Practice Nurses
description: This first level Acute Care Advanced Practice Nursing course Focuses on integrating the American Association of Critical Care Nurses Synergy Model and APN practice with advanced assessment techniques and parameters with clinical decision making for patients with complex needs in the acute care setting.

title: Acute Care Nurse Practitioner II:  Research-based Interventions for Acute Care Nurse Practitioners in Collaborative Patient Care
description: This second level acute care advanced practice nursing course focuses on the scientific basis of interventions for complex, acutely ill individuals. The American Association of Critical-Care Nurseâs Synergy Model for contemporary APN practice is the framework upon which this course and clinical are based. The eight characteristics and three spheres of influence are integrated throughout the clinical, research and theoretical components of this course. The clinical practicum provides opportunities to: witness and demonstrate contemporary ACNP and CCNS practice, be in a clinical setting focusing on the three spheres of influence (patients/families, staff, system) and witnessing and demonstrating the clinical expert role. The clinical practicum provides opportunities to practice on a multi-disciplinary acute/critical care team, determine appropriate interventions, utilize specific expanded practice skills patients, and evaluate outcomes for the acutely ill.

title: Acute & Critical Care Clinical Nurse Specialist I
description: This course focuses on the introduction and beginning implementation of the Critical Care Nurse Specialist role. Actualization of the theoretical components of the CCNS role as observed in the clinical setting will be discussed. Students will have assignments and projects designed to assist them in gaining expertise in presenting, consulting, teaching and writing. Additionally, students will learn to integrate advanced assessment techniques and parameters with critical thinking to solve problems related to the care of patients with complex needs in acute and critical care settings.

title: Acute Care Nurse Practitioner I
description: This is the first level acute care nurse practitioner clinical course. The focus is on integrating advanced assessment parameters with clinical decision making for patients with complex needs in the acute care setting.

title: Acute Care Nurse Practitioner II
description: This second level acute care nurse practitioner course focuses on the scientific basis of interventions for complex, acutely ill individuals. The clinical practicum provides opportunities to: practice on a multi-disciplinary acute/critical care team; determine appropriate interventions; utilize specific expanded practice skills patients; and evaluate outcomes for the acutely ill.

title: Primary Care of the Family I
description: The first primary care course focuses on analysis of family health. Students explore family concepts and theories for their relevance to nursing practice and research. Emphasis is placed on utilizing advanced health assessment skills, preventive health care practices, and health promotion throughout the lifespan. The concepts of family systems, development, culture, environment, learning, and the objectives of Healthy People 2010, are integrated into the principles and practices of primary and secondary prevention. Experiences are provided in clinical decision-making and the implementation of scientifically based therapeutic regimens.

title: Primary Care of the Family II
description: The second course in primary care of the family focuses on the theoretical and clinical basis for the advanced nursing management of health care problems common to families and family members of all ages. Emphasis is placed on the diagnosis and management of acute episodic and chronic conditions of families in the context of the primary care setting and the community. The student's collaborative role with other health care providers is promoted. Clinical experiences include a variety of primary care settings under the supervision of faculty and preceptors.

title: Philosophical & Theoretical Foundations of Nursing Education
description: This course provides the student with an overview of the philosophical and theoretical foundations that underpin nursing education. Content builds on philosophical traditions and influences, nursing theory, pedagogy and andragogy. These underpinnings provide the basis for an examination of the development of conceptual frameworks, curricula, and the teaching-learning process.

title: Instructional Design, Methodologies and Evaluation
description: This course expands upon the content learned in Education I and focuses on bringing the theoretical foundations of nursing education into the planning, implementation, and evaluation stages. The content includes a description of a variety of teaching strategies aimed at promoting critical thinking and active student learning in both the classroom and clinical settings. The student will develop beginning skills in the evaluation process including test construction and clinical evaluation tools used to assess student learning. Class includes one credit hour of precepted experiences culminating in classroom instruction, test construction and student evaluation.

title: Nurse-Midwifery IV
description: This course provides the student with the opportunity to demonstrate comprehensive knowledge and skills in advanced practice. Emphasis is placed on the integration of all previous coursework into full scope nurse-midwifery practice in appropriate nurse-midwivery settings. Students will have the opportunity to practice leadership and participate in the professing role.

title: Seminars in Advanced Women's Health Care
description: Students will build on their basic understanding of well-woman gynecological care and expand their knowledge and skills base to include the wide diversity of ages, psycho-social needs, specific acute health issues, and the need for specialized procedures and case management.

title: Primary Care of the Family IV
description: This course is the final integration and synthesis of advanced practice in primary care of the family. There will be integration of professional role and practice issues, and updates on legislation and health policy affecting Nurse Practitioners. All necessary clinical experiences specified for Family Nurse Practitioner certification are completed. Individualized and selected family-centered practice settings are identified according to student needs. Faculty and preceptors provide guidance for this experience.

title: Research Methodology
description: This course provides the student with intermediate skills in the practical aspects of developing, writing and submitting a research proposal. Student will identify a theoretical/conceptual framework, devise a sampling plan, describe methods and instrumentation for the project. The student will learn the IRB permission process.

title: Research Methodology
description: This course provides the student with intermediate skills in the practical aspects of developing, writing and submitting a research proposal. Student will identify a theoretical/conceptual framework, devise a sampling plan, describe methods and instrumentation for the project. The student will learn the IRB permission process.

title: Scholarly Project
description: The scholarly project is based on integration of core and specialty content within the major area of study in the graduate program. The integration of content includes education & health care issues, roles of advance practice nurses in educational roles, clinical instruction and evaluation, curriculum development, nursing/educational theory, evidence-based practice, or the legal, professional, and ethical dimensions of patient or higher education. The primary focus of the scholarly project should be on translational research. Examples of scholarly projects include: a critical analysis of effective teaching utilizing various approaches including on-line instruction or various technology, educational components of practice in a selected advanced nursing practice area; preparation of a policy position paper on a selected key legal, professional or ethical issue related to nursing education; participation in an ongoing research project as a member of the research team or development and implementation of a research project within the time and resource limitations of the course.

title: Statistical Models for Business
description: Statistical Models for Business is designed to introduce business students to the concepts, techniques and language of regression analysis. Statistics provides the tools for making better business decisions in the face of uncertainty.

title: Business Statistics
description: Hoell, Jacqueline Business Statistics is designed to introduce business students to the concepts, techniques and language of statistics. Statistics provides the tools for making better business decisions in the face of uncertainty.

title: Management Information Systems
description: This course prepares the student to interact with, manage within and be sensitive to the current and emerging trends in information technology. The course material will cover computer hardware and software, database management systems, telecommunications, and the systems analysis process. The course also examines the strategic importance of computing and related social and ethical issues. Computer laboratory assignments will be used to illustrate information systems concepts and applications. Formerly offered as MGMT 250.

title: Electronic Commerce
description: This course focuses on electronic commerce over the Internet. Topics include infrastructure issues for building e-commerce applications and issues related to business strategy and marketing on the Internet. Examples are offering products and services on the Internet, Internet advertising, building electronic communities, the fit between the technology and the organization's marketing strategy, and ethical and policy issues such as privacy. Students will learn website design and tools, such as Front Page and XML. Formerly offered as MGMT 256.

title: Developing/Managing Databases
description: The objective of this course is to introduce the student to the key principles and activities associated with the design, use and administration of business databases. The course will discuss important database design principles and sophisticated data retrieval and mining techniques. These principles and techniques will be demonstrated using various computerized database tools, including Oracle and Access. The course will cover critical issues related to database development and administration in the context of consumer-oriented, electronic commerce (Internet and World Wide Web) environments. Formerly offered as MGMT 257.

title: Decision Support Systems
description: Management Support Systems are playing increasingly important roles in the business world. This course is a general introduction to the managerial and technical aspects of such systems. Topics covered include Decision Making, Decision Support Systems, Management Information Systems, Artificial Intelligence (Expert Systems and other advanced concepts), and Executive Information Systems. Besides the exams, completion of an individual programming assignment and a group project are a requirement for this course. For the programming assignment, students will build a Visual Basic for Application (VBA) program in Excel. The ability to program, especially in Excel (and/or Access) is a highly marketable skill for all business majors. For the group project, students will build a Decision Support Application. Details will be provided during the first week of class. This course will be interactive in nature. Students are expected to engage in critical discussions, applying their knowledge from the readings. You should be prepared to participate actively in class discussions. Please be sure that your comments and questions are conducive to a respectful and comfortable environment for others to contribute their ideas.

title: Project Management
description: This course provides an overview of project management. The course addresses (1) the culture, (2) the principles, and (3) the basic techniques of project management. The course reviews the general stages of a project in rough chronological order and describes how the stages interrelate. Basic tools of project management, such as work breakdown structure, scheduling, earned value analysis, and risk management, are introduced. The elements of project management critical to the success of a project also are identified and explained.

title: Global Logistics
description: The emphasis of the course is the logistics or Supply Chain function in firms that source, produce, distribute and market in multiple nations. The management of the logistics in such firms differs from its domestic counterpart along several key dimensions. First, there is the need to be able to identify and analyze factors that differ across nations, which influence the effectiveness of this function. These include worker productivity, process adaptability, governmental concerns, transportation availability, and culture and so on. In addition, because of the distances involved, transportation and distribution are of greater significance. Finally, these geographically dispersed set of facilities and markets must be integrated and manage to enhance the strategy of the business unit. Therefore, some of the sessions of the course will focus on cross-national decisions and others will focus on managing across nations. Throughout the course we will emphasize the role that emerging technologies are playing within the supply chain and e-commerce. For the purposes of the course, the perspective will be that of the director of logistics or Supply Chain, vice-president of logistics or other, senior level general managers with primary responsibility for the production and distribution of manufactured products. A major theme throughout the course is that sourcing, manufacturing and distribution are of primary importance in the formulation of business and corporate strategy. We want to look at the strategic decisions within logistics as they impact the ability of the firm to achieve its selected overall strategies.

title: Management Science
description: Management Science is the application of quantitative modeling techniques to the managerial decision making process. The course will be taught by example and adopts a practical spreadsheet-based approach to the modeling of a wide variety of business problems from finance, marketing, and operations. The approach taken in this course concentrates on problem solving in an interdisciplinary context that also develops spreadsheet skills. Formerly offered as DSCI 284.

title: Operations Management
description: A firm has the opportunity to create competitive advantage through proficient management of its operationsâmanufacturing and services. To do so, the firm must first recognize and establish the strategic role of its operations within the organization. Then, at the more detailed operational level, the firm must execute effectively and efficiently. This course examines the strategic role that the operations function can play, and offers specific tools and techniques that the firm can use during implementation.

title: Service Operations
description: Services account for more than 50% of the worldâs GDP, 65% of the US GDP, and 80% of jobs in the US (Business Roundtable). This course is designed to provide the participant with the concepts and principals needed to design, manage, and improve service delivery processes. Utilizing the Socratic method, we explore business models, conceptual frameworks, and the use of qualitative and quantitative analyses for enhancing the effectiveness of service operations. An important aspect of the course focuses on the development of participants' skills to learn, analyze, synthesize, and apply new developments in service management across a broad range of service industries. In addition, we consider the evolving effects of technology and globalization on services. We learn how to apply these concepts and principles to specific service settings through class discussion, class members' experiences, and a group project involving an actual service organization.

title: IT and Business Integration
description: OPIM 550: IT AND BUSINESS INTEGRATION The course is open to all MBA students. No pre-requisite. Information technology (IT) is constantly changing many aspects of the business world. This course aims to introduce IT concepts, and to overview the issues, difficulties and opportunities facing organizations. This course is designed to familiarize you with IT's: 1) Strategic impact on the organization 2) Interrelationship with business processes. 3) Role in achieving a competitive advantage. 4) Use for organizational decision making at different levels. 5) Social and ethical issues.

title: Applied Business Statistics
description: Additional content will be added soon to reflect the changes to the new curriculum. MBA Core Topics Covered in this Class: 1) Statistical Inference: Estimation and Testing Hypotheses Statistical Inference is the art of making statements about the general (the Population) based on the specific (the Sample). Any such attempt has two sobering properties: â¢ Our answer is almost certainly incorrect â¢ We never know by how much we are wrong. Nevertheless, most human endeavor proceeds by making such inferences and it is therefore, important to conduct the process as effectively as possible. The two general categories of statistical inference, estimation and hypothesis testing, can be distinguished by different purposes: estimation is concerned with estimating the value of an unknown population parameter; hypothesis testing is concerned with making a decision about a hypothesized value of an unknown population parameter. In chapter 5 âInferences Based on a Single Sample: Estimation with Confidence Intervalsâ, we estimate population means and proportions based on a single sample selected from the population of interest and, more importantly, show how to attach a measure of uncertainty to that point estimate. In chapter 6 âInferences Based on a Single Sample: Tests of Hypothesis,â we show how to utilize the sample information to test whether a population parameter conforms to a pre-specified value. 2) Applied Regression Analysis: Regression analysis is a quantitative tool for modeling the relationship between a set of explanatory or independent variables and a dependent variable. The explanatory variables may be controlled (e.g. advertising expenditures) or observational (e.g. responses to a survey). The value of such models lies both in direct prediction and in âwhat ifâ analyses. Chapter 10 âSimple Linear Regression,â covers the simplest situation, the linear relationship between a single explanatory variable and a dependent variable. Chapter 11 âMultiple Regression and Model Buildingâ extends the basic concepts developed in chapter 10, modeling the mean value of dependent variable as a function of two or more explanatory variables. The emphasis in the course is the development of model-building skills and the ability to interpret the results of a regression analysis.

title: Management Science
description: Management Science (MS) is a quantitative approach to managerial decision making. It is a body of principles, techniques and methods concerned with providing solutions to managerial problems. Management Science is not statistics. However, as in many other fields of study it may employ statistics to obtain model inputs, analysis and outputs. In our approach to Management Science we will draw from topics that immediately link to and have an interface with your courses in the program. In particular, our Management Science course is designed to offer a well integrated sequence of quantitative topics in support of select concepts encountered in your Operations Management, Finance and Marketing courses. This approach is consistent with our objective of teaching an integrated course where the topics covered serve to support, reinforce and assimilate the conceptual teachings of your first year core courses. Related material, assignments and cases illustrate topical concepts and their integration. The material covered include: Break-even Analysis; Inventory Models; Statistical Process Control; Decision Analysis; Black & Scholes and Binomial Option Pricing Models; Linear Optimization; Non-Linear Optimization portfolio risk minimization (time permitting).

title: Computer-Based Decision Support Systems
description: Additional content will be added soon to reflect the changes to the new curriculum. This course explores advanced topics in management support systems with a focus on decision theory. The course will present insights into key issues and problems in decision support information systems. The Visual Basic Applications (VBA) programming language within Microsoft Excel will be used as the primary tool to build basic decision support systems. Class deliverables will be systems built with VBA code.

title: Special Topics: Applied Decision Theory
description: Additional content will be added soon to reflect the changes to the new curriculum. Suppose you have been given the responsibility of determining whether your firm should expand its sales region to include the southwestern part of the United States, or India. Before making your decision, you would probably want answers to many questions. Even if it were possible to obtain accurate answers (perfect information) to these and other pertinent questions, your decisions problem would be extremely complex and, realistically, you cannot expect to receive perfect information. Thus, in making your decision, you will face the more complex problem of having to deal with answers about which you are uncertain. The first part of this course will center on Decision Analysis. Decision Analysis is a systematic approach to solving problems under conditions of uncertainty. It does not describe how or why an individual makes a decision; rather, it prescribes a decision for the individual that is consistent with his/her preferences and attitudes towards risk. The second part of this course will be based on Design of Experiments, analysis of variance, the chi-square test and analysis of Contingency tables. This is an applied course, i.e. cases and exercises of different areas of business will be considered. MBA second year students only.

title: Advanced Regression Analysis
description: Additional content will be added soon to reflect the changes to the new curriculum. This course provides the advanced yet extremely applied perspective on business statistics. Two specific topics account for the majority of its content: regression analysis and analysis of variance. Additional topics include deviating from model assumptions, multicollinearity, transformations, residuals and lack of fit, heteroscedasticity, detecting outliers/influential outliers: Jack Knife, detecting residual correlation: Durbin-Watson test, piece-wise linear regression, inverse prediction, and weighted least squares. MBA second year students only.

title: Business Forecasting
description: Our objective is to introduce various approaches to forecasting, to understand their strengths and weaknesses, and to develop a framework for identifying an appropriate method for a particular forecasting problem. Along the way, we will also see that some methods, although widely used, may be flawed and/or inefficient. Topics to be covered will include: â¢ Judgmental forecasting â¢ How to measure forecasting performance â¢ Exponential smoothing for regular and seasonal series â¢ Time series regression â¢ Autoregressive models â¢ Intervention analysis

title: Special Topics: Real Options
description: Additional content will be added soon to reflect the changes to the new curriculum. âReal Optionsâ is a new valuation standard for evaluating and analyzing capital investment decisions in the face of uncertainty. The real options approach is an extension of option pricing theory for managing real assets. This course is about the real options approach to strategic investments. It aims to develop a real options thinking rather than focusing only on the technical aspects of modeling in a real options framework. In that vein, our objective is to introduce real options as a viable means to evaluation of investment opportunities. Selected case studies and material will ensure an in-depth understanding of various concepts in a realistic setting. MBA second year students only.

title: Operations Management
description: Additional content will be added soon to reflect the changes to the new curriculum. MBA Core This course is designed to introduce you to concepts, tools and techniques for managing this function. Moreover, since this is a new field for many of you, we also want to give you a new perspective to look at organizations. If we are successful, many of you after this course will change the way you look at a restaurant, a hospital, a bank, a factory, a repair shop, a daily newspaper, an airline, a warehouse, a government agency, an airport, or even a consulting company! Youâll observe more closely and critically how they are producing their product (goods or services), and will notice that some of their production processes are smart and some not-so-smart (sometimes irritatingly inefficient). We believe this course will give you a better appreciation of the real drivers behind success or failure of many of these organizations. To use our usual business school jargons, youâll learn about the strategic importance of the firmâs supply chain and production systems.

title: Project Management
description: The course is open to all MBA students. No pre-requisite. This course provides an overview of project management. The course addresses (1) the culture, (2) the principles, and (3) the basic techniques of project management. The course reviews the general stages of a project in rough chronological order and describes how the stages interrelate. Basic tools of project management, such as work breakdown structure, scheduling, earned value analysis, and risk management, are introduced. The elements of project management critical to the success of a project also are identified and explained. Specific Objectives: 1. To describe, demystify, and formalize project management so that students are aware of the Project Management discipline and can utilize new knowledge and skills in their professions. 2. To enable the immediate practice of project management in any workplace, independent of discipline. 3. To lay a foundation for those students interested in the PMP Certification. 4. To enjoy the class and have spirited, knowledgeable debates with classmates and instructor.

title: Global Logistics
description: Additional content will be added soon to reflect the changes to the new curriculum. The emphasis of the course is the logistics or Supply Chain function in firms that source, produce, distribute and market in multiple nations. The management of the logistics in such firms differs from its domestic counterpart along several key dimensions. First, there is the need to be able to identify and analyze factors that differ across nations, which influence the effectiveness of this function. These include worker productivity, process adaptability, governmental concerns, transportation availability, and culture and so on. In addition, because of the distances involved, transportation and distribution are of greater significance. Finally, these geographically dispersed set of facilities and markets must be integrated and manage to enhance the strategy of the business unit. Therefore, some of the sessions of the course will focus on cross-national decisions and others will focus on managing across nations. Throughout the course we will emphasize the role that emerging technologies are playing within the supply chain and e-commerce. For the purposes of the course, the perspective will be that of the director of logistics or Supply Chain, vice-president of logistics or other, senior level general managers with primary responsibility for the production and distribution of manufactured products. A major theme throughout the course is that sourcing, manufacturing and distribution are of primary importance in the formulation of business and corporate strategy. We want to look at the strategic decisions within logistics as they impact the ability of the firm to achieve its selected overall strategies.

title: International Operations
description: Additional content will be added soon to reflect the changes to the new curriculum. Few courses in a typical business school delve into the operational issues of producing and delivering goods and services in multiple countries. These are issues like how you organize an international expansion project, manage a factory or a service center abroad or a business process that has been outsourced in India or China. But, given the growing spread of transnational operations, companies, especially strategy consultants, will need more people with knowledge and expertise in this area. This kind of knowledge can create a nice edge for an MBA. Look at aggregate statistics. For most of the last fifty years, world trade has been growing faster than world output. In other words, each year a larger share of goods and services produced in one country is transferred across national borders. A significant and increasing share of these transfers occur within the same company--for sale in a foreign market, further work (such as R&D, engineering, assembly, packaging, storage, and so on), servicing major customers who have set up operations in new countries, or for back office operations, repair, remanufacture, reuse, recycle, or disposal. Clearly, management of all these transnational operations demands increasing attentionâby managers themselves and their consultants. Moreover, foreign direct investment has been growing even at a faster rate than world trade. There was a lull in 2001 and 2002, but it is now back on its normal upward trend. Multinationals are putting more assets outside their home bases. So the chances that youâll find job offers in foreign affiliates of these companies are on the rise, and even if you donât end up in a subsidiary, youâll need to know how to work with them. This course is designed to prepare you better for these jobs. It is not a continuation of the core OM course; it is more interdisciplinary and examines the operational issues from a more strategic perspective. Here are the main topics: â¢ Approaches for setting up new operations abroad â¢ Operational issues in offering a service internationally â¢ Strategies for outsourcing and offshoring â¢ Managing internationally outsourced operations, and more generally, working with suppliers and strategic partners in the companyâs global supply network â¢ Designing and managing a global network of design, engineering, production, and service centers â¢ Challenges faced by operations managers in various positions in the global network â¢ Leveraging the internationality of the firmâs operations to create competitive advantage.

title: Operations Strategy
description: Additional content will be added soon to reflect the changes to the new curriculum. A firm derives a competitive advantage either by achieving a cost advantage, or through product differentiation (Michael Porter, Competitive Advantage, Free Press, NY, NY, 1985). That is, either the firm must be able to produce its goods at a lower cost (through process innovation), or it must offer features that customers desire and competitors lack (through product innovation). We explore specific operational strategies that the firm might pursue to achieve this superior process or product innovation. Within each topic, we develop a framework, or theory, that the firm can use as an aid in its decision-making process, and also tackle a real-life problem through a case study. The topics are as follows: Introduction: The Dynamics of Innovation; Competing Through Operations; Strategic Implications of the Learning Curve; Encroachment Strategies; Outsourcing; New Product Development; Strategic Quality Management; Capacity Management; Capacity, Flexibility and Timing; Path to Profitability; Value Creation in Private Equity.

title: General Pharmacology
description: This course is designed to provide the student with a knowledge of the fundamental principles of pharmacology and a comprehensive experience with the effects of drugs on mammalian organisms. The subject is taught by lectures, conferences, and demonstrations, and is primarily for medical students. Prerequisites: BCHB-501, 502 or equivalent; PBIO-502 or equivalent; ANAT-512 or equivalent.

title: General Principles of Pharmacology
description: This course consists of that part of PHAR-501 which deals with the fundamentals of drug absorption, distribution, and elimination. This course begins in mid-August. Permission of instructor required.

title: General Principles of Neuropharmacology
description: This course consists of that part of PHAR-501 which deals with the fundamentals of drug absorption, distribution, elimination and the action of drugs on the central, autonomic, and peripheral nervous systems. This course begins in mid-August. Permission of instructor required.

title: Graduate Pharmacology
description: This course includes both a broad survey of pharmacology and detailed discussions of experimental approaches used in pharmacological research.

title: Neuropharmacology
description: The pharmacology and neurochemistry of neurotransmission in the autonomic and central nervous system. This is the second portion of PHAR504, Graduate Pharmacology, and meets MTWRF 10-12 from Feb 3 through Mar 6. It can be taken as a stand-alone course. It is recommended that students have previously taken either PHAR591, Princ Pharm/Mol Targets, or NSCI501, Cell Mol Neuroscience.

title: Modern Methods in Molecular Biology
description: Introduction and evaluation of most of the commonly used methods in molecular biology.

title: Applied Statistical Principles in Pharmacology
description: To provide students with a working knowledge of how to design and analyze pharmacological experiments. Course Structure: Each week, students will be given a take-home problem set with a brief (30-min.) introduction to the new topic area. This problem set will be discussed in a âroundtableâ format during the first 90-min. of the following session, and then a new problem set will be introduced for the following week. Students will be evaluated based on their level of participation, homework, and quizzes.

title: Ethical Issues in Scientific Research
description: Discussions of ethical questions and dilemmas facing scientists today.

title: Introduction to Pharmacology
description: This minicourse presents an overview of some aspects of pharmacology. It begins with basic principles of pharmacodynamics and pharmacokinetics. Neuropharmacology, including the autonomic nervous system and the central nervous system are then covered. Cardiovascular pharmacology and its central control is discussed. Finally, there is a specialized discussion of the role of voltage and ligand-gated channels in controlling cellular function. The course meets from mid-semester to December. Check with bwolfe01@georgetown.edu for exact start date.

title: Princ Pharm/Mol Targets
description: This course is the first section of PHAR504 (Graduate Pharmacology) and covers basic principles of pharmacology, including pharmacokinetics, pharmacodynamics, drug metabolism, and drug-receptor interactions. It also covers cellular and molecular targets for drug action such as receptors and enzymes. The course meets MTWRF 10-12 from Jan 7 to Feb 2.

title: Seminar / Journal club
description: Research progress reports by staff, students, and distinguished visitors. Analyses of recent articles and current concepts in research.

title: Seminar / Journal club
description: Research progress reports by staff, students, and distinguished visitors. Analyses of recent articles and current concepts in research.

title: Pharmacology Tutorial
description: Individualized tutorials between students and faculty to address topics not covered by other courses.

title: Thesis Research--Pharmacology
description: Research for Doctoral Dissertation

title: Thesis Research--Pharmacology
description: Thesis research leading to a PhD dissertation. This section 03 is for the student who is still in coursework, but also in thesis research.

title: Introduction to Ethics
description: Introduction to Ethics is composed of multiple sections. Choose from the following: Sections 01-11 Professor Stohr 12 Professor Reuscher 13 Professor Riley 14-16 Professor Lichtenberg Sections 01-11: Professor Stohr This course is an introduction to the study of ethics, understood as both a subject for theoretical reflection and a practical guide for how to live. We will explore the views of the Westâs greatest ethical thinkers, both past and present, and we will use narratives from literature and film to illuminate the ethical issues those thinkers raise. Readings will range from Greek tragedy to contemporary articles on sweatshop labor. The course begins with an exploration of the moral self, including conceptions of character, the role of conscience, moral education, moral luck, responsiveness to evil, and the meaning of integrity and self-respect. We will move from there to a discussion of ethical issues that arise out of interactions with others, with a special focus on our immediate Georgetown community. We will discuss the moral ramifications of academic honesty, alcohol use, and sexual relationships, using philosophical readings to help us think reflectively about how these issues influence life at Georgetown. Finally, we will broaden our outlook to consider global ethical issues, including our relationship to the world's poor. Requirements: two papers and two take-home exams (midterm and final) Section 12: Professor Reuscher The point of this course is to provide the students with an in-depth introduction to two of the most influential ethical theories in the canon of Western philosophy. The focus will be on the foundations of these theories. We will give the primary ethical texts in both systems a close reading and we will investigate the reasons why the two systems are in such contrast to each other. This is a text oriented course. You will be responsible on the mid-term and the final for the content of all the texts covered in the lectures targeted by the exams. The course will close with a consideration of the challenge, if any, posed by evolutionary theory, neuroscience, and related disciplines to the positions defended by Aristotle and Kant. Information Required Texts: Aristotle Introduction to Aristotle (0-07-553652-5) McGraw-Hill Kant Critique of Pure Reason (1-40391-195-9) St. Martinâs Press Grounding for the Metaphysics of Morals (0-915145-00-6) Hackett Critique of Practical Reason (0-02-307753-0) Macmillan Paperbacks Metaphysics of Morals (0-521-56673-8) Cambridge U. Press Office Hours etc.: T/R: 3:15-4:00 218 New North reuschej@georgetown.edu reuschej@yahoo.com (H) 202-388-0681 (There is an answering machine.) Requirements A 6 (BALS) or 8 (MALS) page paper due on the date in the Registrarâs schedule for an exam in this course. If you are graduating this semester, the due date may be earlier. There will be a penalty for absences not approved in advance. Section 13: Professor Riley This class is an introduction to moral philosophy in the Western tradition, for the interested student. We will read, think through, and talk over some selections from the works of eminent philosophers in the tradition including Plato, Aristotle, Hobbes, Hume, Kant and Mill. (Our main text for these is the reader Moral Philosophy, edited by Louis Pojman, in the third edition.) In doing so we intend to make the subject live for us by continuously renewing the conversation around such questions as: Why be moral? How are right action and the good life related? We will also work our way through Rosalind Hursthouse's Ethics, Humans and Other Animals. This book provides a) an introduction to contemporary ethical theory, b) a sustained discussion of what we might or might not owe to nonhuman animals, and c) some useful instruction on working through philosophical arguments and doing academic reading Sections 14-16: Professor Lichtenberg: We examine basic problems in ethics, as well as some current, controversial moral issues. Are human beings ultimately selfish and thus incapable of acting morally? Even if people can act morally, do they have good reason to? Does morality depend on God and religion? If not, what does it depend on? What is the good life for human beings? We investigate issues such as the death penalty, abortion, euthanasia, and the value of nonhuman animals and the environment. The course has several goals. One is to introduce you to the discipline of philosophy, by acquainting you with the way philosophers approach problems. The second is to improve your skills in thinking, arguing, and writing. Doing philosophy involves taking a critical stance toward your own and others' beliefs, and learning how and to what extent these beliefs can be justified. The third goal is to see how much progress we can make in resolving disagreements about important ethical questions, some of which are public and political issues in our society today.

title: Introduction to Philosophy
description: Introduction to Philosophy is composed of multiple sections. Choose from the following: Sections 01-10 Professor Blattner 11 - Professor Houck 12 - Professor Ver Eecke 13 - Professor Ambrosio 14 - Professor Alonso Sections 01-10: Professor Blattner What is human freedom? Is there such a thing anyhow? We will look at several of the conceptions of human freedom offered in the Western philosophical tradition, including: the traditional thesis that freedom consists in choosing in accordance with reason and morality, Nietzsche's aestheticist view that freedom consists in autonomous self-creation analogous with artistic creation, Hume's determinist skepticism about the reach of human freedom, and James's pragmatist defense of pure free will. Students will tangle with these positions by evaluating their plausibility and the force of the arguments offered to support them. (This course uses weekly discussion sections.) Section 11: Professor Houck This course will examine interesting concerns as they arise in an historical context. What is real? What can I know? How shall I live? These intriguing and persistent questions, and their various answers, make up a debate covering continents and centuries. We will join this great conversation. Course assignments include reading, class participation, quizzes, in-class essay, and short writing assignments. Section12: Professor Ver Eecke I and the Other This course will address philosophically the relation of a human being to other human beings. In a first chapter, we will analyze the characteristics of philosophy and explain the different fields of philosophy. We will make use of religion and economics to illustrate the characteristics of philosophy and several of its fields. We will read Plato as an example of philosophical thinking. In a second chapter we will study the interdependence of single humans beings by means of a classic passage in Hegel's philosophy: the master-slave dialectic. This passage analyzes the tendency to dominance/subordination in human relationships and the presence of violence and aggression. We will use Frederick Douglassâ Memoirs as an illustration of Hegelâs ideas. In chapter three we will study the relation of one human being with privileged others. We will do that by first studying the complexity of the human subject (jokes, slips of the tongue, denials) and then by studying the function of privileged others (parents) in the complexity of the human subject. We will make use of philosophy of psychoanalysis which will introduce us to the meaning of both mental illness and sexual difference. A member of the counseling center will address the class as part of the Engelhard program. In a fourth and last chapter we will analyze the relationship of individuals in the capitalist system. We will, first, reflect on the relationship between economic actors in the economic theory using the concepts of private, public and merit goods. We will argue that individuals in the free market must compete (private goods), co-operate (public goods) and must submit to the will of the community (merit goods). Second we will study the consequences for a human person of being a member of a special class. Here we will use Marx' analysis of class conflict and class exploitation. Readings: Plato. Great Dialogues of Plato. Mentor. ISBN: 978 045 153 0851. Hegel: Phenomenology of Spirit: âLordship and Bondageâ (Electronic) Douglass, Frederick. Narrative of the life of Frederick Douglass. On the web: //docsouth.unc.edu/douglass/douglass.html Section 13: Professor Ambrosio 1. Objective of the Course: The objective of the course is to introduce students to the human experience of questioning and seeking to understand the meaning of our existence as persons. The course will approach this goal in three ways: first, by reading a number of the dialogues of Plato, we shall attempt to identify those basic human questions which are important to all of us as persons and which have become the enduring issues of philosophy. Second, we shall examine how Aristotle developed a very different but complementary approach to these same questions. And third, we shall become acquainted with ways some of the major figures in the subsequent history of philosophy up to our own time have responded in very different ways to these questions. Throughout the course equal emphasis will be placed on trying to learn from great philosophers and trying to become aware of how their questions are still at issue in our own society and individual lives. The goal, in other words, is to learn from them how to think more effectively for ourselves. 2. Required texts: Plato, The Collected Dialogues, ed. Cairns and Hamilton, Princeton Guthrie, W.L.C., The Greek Philosophers, Harper and Row. Aristotle, An Introduction to Aristotle. Frankl, V., Man's Search for Meaning. 3. Assignments: --One short reflective paper (4 pages typewritten) --Two examinations --Take home final exam in the form of a 7-8 page typewritten essay. --Brief written outlines of each major reading assignment due on the first day reading is discussed in class; cf. schedule. 4. Consultation: All students are strongly encouraged to meet with the instructor outside of class hours several times during the semester to discuss their progress in the course. 5. Class Attendance and Participation: Regular attendance is presumed. Class participation will be considered in the final grade, as will participation in discussion sections, although attendance at these is not required. Section 14: Professor Alonso This course will introduce students to central problems of philosophy in the areas of metaphysics, epistemology, and philosophy of mind. These problems include the existence of an external world; the possibility and grounds of knowledge; the nature of causation; the relation between the mind and the body; the possibility of thinking machines; the nature of consciousness; the idea of personal identity; and the relation between freedom and determinism. We will study some classic texts from the history of philosophy as well as some important contemporary writings.

title: Political & Social Thought
description: Sections 1-6: Professor Murphy This course is an introduction to political philosophy. We will focus on two perennial issues of political thought: political authority and distributive justice. The problem of political authority is that of explaining the source and extent of the lawâs power to bind members of the political community to obedience. The problem of distributive justice is that of determining the appropriate criteria by which scarce resources ought to be allocated within a political community. We will emphasize two strands of theorizing about politics: contractarianism and Aristotelianism. Grade will be based on four course papers and participation in discussion section. Sections 7-12: Professor Mara This course introduces first year students to the different ways in which the problems and possibilities of social life have been treated within some of the central texts of Western political philosophy. The authors examined include Plato, Aristotle, Thomas Hobbes, John Locke, Karl Marx, Hannah Arendt and Jurgen Habermas. Their works will be studied not only as historical statements but also as contributions to an ongoing set of conversations about justice, power and political purpose. More generally, the course will encourage students to think about the intellectual project of political thought and about its connectons with and differences from the concerns and practices of citizens. Sections 70-71 (SFS Qatar): Professor Koons In this course, you will be introduced to some of the major ideas in political philosophy. Much of the course will center around two topics: the nature and source of political authority (What gives a government authority over its citizens? What is the best form of government?), and the question of distributive justice (How should societyâs wealth be distributed to its members? What role, if any, should government play in this distribution? What is the relation between justice and the free market?) The goal of this class is to engage the students in active discussion about these and related issues.

title: Ethics: Life & Death
description: The fact of human mortality raises a host of challenging ethical questions. Since it is a fact with which we all make efforts to come to grips, we tend to be familiar with, and to have considered, many such questions about the implications of death. These include, but are not limited to, the following: When, if ever, is it morally permissible to deprive a being of life? If voluntary killing is sometimes permissible, whose lives may be taken, under what circumstances, and why? For instance, do only human beings have a special claim to continued existence? If not, what is the moral significance of killing non-human animals? Do the lives of all human beings enjoy protection? What are the rights, if any, of unborn human beings? Can we be entitled to death as well as to life? Questions such as these are relevant for a range of ethical issues of enduring and contemporary interest, such as abortion, euthanasia, suicide, capital punishment, and vegetarianism. They all concern what is the appropriate basis for, and extent of, our responsibility for the manner in which we and others live and die. It is a guiding assumption of this course that philosophical reflection can assist us in our efforts to develop a deeper and more sophisticated understanding of the ethics of life and death. In making this effort, we shall primarily rely upon contemporary readings in the fields of bioethics and moral philosophy.

title: Ethics: Philosophy of Medicine
description: This course will examine human choice and action in terms of ethical theory. We will study issues of human meaning in health and illness, suffering and aging, strength and mortality in the literature of novelists, poets and play writes. Philosophical inquiry will focus on humanistic experience rather than on scientific medicine. Assignments include reading, class discussion, quizzes, in-class essay and short writing assignments.

title: Ethics:  Autonomy in Bioethics
description: The course will focus on the ethical problems surrounding respect for patient autonomy in contemporary clinical medicine and research. The goals of this course are two-fold. First, it aims to help students think critically and constructively about a range of practical ethical issues and real-world cases such as paternalistic actions and policies, refusal of treatment, nonadherence to treatment, assisted suicide, transgender surgery, clinical trials, cognitive enhancement, the status of the demented elderly, the mentally ill and the mentally disabled, among others. Second, it aims to help students gain an understanding of the way in which the philosophical investigation of the nature and scope of autonomy, the ethical assessment of the relative importance of respect for autonomy versus other moral considerations, as well as the ethical and legal theories of informed consent, decisional capacity, and physician-patient communication can be used to illuminate those issues. In order to get a rich and nuanced picture of the patient-health-caregiver relationships, we will rely on studies in psychology of decision-making, public policy, social sciences, and medicine, in addition to narrative material (fiction and film). This body of empirical and narrative literature will contribute to assessing the relevance and acceptability of the ethical theories we will discuss. This is an introductory course to bioethics and does not presuppose any familiarity with this domain (except having been to a doctor at least once in your life!). Students will be encouraged to think critically about the writersâ views, and to develop and defend their own views. Assignments 2 small papers (4 pages): 20% each Final paper (8 pages): 35% Class Participation: 20% Weekly question on Blackboard: 5%

title: Ethics:  Autonomy in Bioethics
description: Yashar Saghai: Autonomy in Bioethics (PHIL-117) Course Description The course will focus on the ethical problems surrounding respect for patient autonomy in contemporary clinical medicine and research. First, it aims to help students think critically and constructively about a range of practical ethical issues and real-world cases such as refusal of treatment, nonadherence to treatment, reproductive choices, assisted suicide, transgender surgery, immunization, clinical trials, the status of minors, the demented elderly, the mentally ill, among others. Second, it aims to help students gain an understanding of the way in which the philosophical investigation of the nature and scope of autonomy, the ethical assessment of the relative importance of respect for autonomy versus other moral considerations, as well as the ethical and legal theories of informed consent, decisional capacity, and physician-patient communication can be used to illuminate those issues. In order to get a rich and nuanced picture of the patient-health-caregiver relationships, we will rely on studies in psychology, public policy, social sciences, and medicine, in addition to narrative material (fiction and film). This is an introductory course to bioethics and does not presuppose any familiarity with this domain (except having been to a doctor at least once in your life!). title: Moral Psychology
description: TBA

title: Knowledge/Pleasure/Good
description: This course introduces students to ethical and moral thought from Plato to the twenty-first century. In particular, this term we will focus on the tension between pleasure and the good, on the one hand, and on the sometimes-uneasy relation between reason and ethics, on the other. To broach our two central themes we will raise a number of questions regarding the conditions of possibility for ethical inquiry and action, the nature of the individual and collective good, and the main threats to ethics today (e.g. egoism, relativism, scientific determinism, etc.). The thinkers whose works we will study in an attempt to answer these questions include Plato, Aristotle, Spinoza, Kant, Nietzsche, and Blackburn.

title: Ethics: Ancient/Modern Theory
description: The goal of this course is to encourage students to reflect on the meaning of âbeing moralâ within the context of the development of ethical thought in the West. What is happiness? What is a good life? What does the life of a virtuous person consist in? These fundamental questions, posed by the ancients but persisting in their relevance, will be our starting point. Next we will explore the ways in which the questions of morality have changed over time as a result of the interplay between culture and ethical theory. In particular, we will see how the ancient contemplation of the good life is replaced in modern ethical theory by different sorts of moral questions, such as âHow should we live?â and âOn what principles should we act?â We will explore the similarities and differences between the ancient and modern approaches to ethics, covering a variety of great philosophers in the process, ranging from Plato and Aristotle to Nietzsche, Hume, Kant, and Mill.
