title: Law, Morality, and Liberty
description: This class is a bridge course in ethics. It focuses on a part of moral philosophy sometimes referred to as political morality. Political morality is concerned with the moral relationship between the state and the individual. Among its concerns are questions about the moral justification for using law as an instrument for interference with individual thought, expression, or action for the sake of any number of familiar social aims, e.g., inculcating or enforcing private morality, preventing harm to others, reducing inequality, maintaining social cohesion, or promoting individual well-being. There are three course segments, each devoted to an in-depth examination of various writings of a single author. Our selections include Aristotle, Locke, and Mill. The choice is based on the (admittedly controversial) assumption that these authors, more than any others, provide the most illuminating theoretical perspectives from which individual liberty and its moral limits are currently understood within Western liberal democracies (and perhaps more widely as well). Essays of roughly 10 pages (3000 words) will be required for each segment. Each essay will count as 25% of the grade, with the remaining 25% based on class discussion and possibly other short assignments or in-class exercises. Required books for the course are listed below, and additional readings will be posted under the Assignments Tab for the course listing posted on Blackboard. Additional readings will include works by our primary authors as well as short excerpts by writers influenced by or contrasting with the central claims of the primary authors. 1)The Nicomachean Ethics, Aristotle, Terrence Irwin, trans, 2nd edition, Hackett, 1999. title: Ethics: Debates about Reason
description: PHIL-134. Ethics: Debates about Reasons People often ask 'Why did you do that?'. And we usually reply by offering a reason for why. (We say: 'Well, here is why.' And then we give a reason.) Much of ethics focuses on these reasons that we give to others (and to ourselves). This class will be about these reasons. There will be four parts to this class. In Part 1 we will be focusing on various abstract questions about reasons. Where do reasons come from? What, if anything, grounds them? Are they compatible with the natural scientific worlview? Are reasons real or not? And what does that question even mean? In Part 1, then, we'll cover, among other things, normative realism (in its supernaturalist, non-naturalist, and naturalist forms) and normative anti-realism (error theories and non-cognitivism). Part 2 will cover different consequentialist views of reasons. Here we will discuss the debate between act and rule consequentialists. And here we will also cover the debate between egoists and utilitarians: Should we be egoists? How much, really, do we owe to other people? Part 3 will cover different deontological views of reasons. Here we will examine (1) debates within Kantian ethics about how reasons work, (2) the debate between generalists and particularists about reasons, and (3) some natural law deontologies. In Part 4 we will try to apply ethical theories to particular cases. This is hard to do, for there seems to be a black box of sorts between our theories (which are abstract) and the details of particular cases.

title: Ethics: Autonomy
description: The idea of autonomy is of central importance as an element in our conception of a good life and a just polity, as a condition of moral responsibility and effective consent in medical ethics, and as an aspect of successful human action more generally. We will examine the idea of autonomy, or the family of ideas that go under this label, from all three points of view: from the point of view of normative theory (moral and political), the theory of moral responsibility, and the philosophy of action. In action theory, we will look at ways in which the idea of autonomy, or self-rule, can help elucidate the nature of human choice. In examining the issue of moral responsibility, the question will be whether autonomy is an important condition of such responsibility and, if so, how it should be understood. Finally, we will make a foray into political theory to examine questions raised by feminist thinkers about autonomy and oppression, as well as the ways in which democracies can offer a form of collective self-rule, or autonomy. Examining each of these different guises of autonomy will illuminate all of the others. Crosscutting issues that we will examine include the relation between autonomy and âhigher-orderâ reflection, the relation between autonomy and objectivity, and the question of whether the concept of autonomy stands free of specific normative presuppositions.

title: Ethics: The Untold War
description: In this course, we examine issues in the moral psychology and moral philosophy of war. While just war theory has long been a central focus in understanding war from a moral and legal point of view, and traumatic illness (posttraumatic stress disorder) a focus in the clinical treatment of soldiers, little attention has been given to the moral psychology of soldiering itself. How do civilians take up the role of soldier? How do they leave that role behind? What sort of role duties are involved in being a citizen soldier? What moral quandaries and emotional conflicts do individual soldiers face as they rev up for war, deploy, and return home? Are feelings of revenge morally justified in war? Is survivor guilt or luck guilt a rational response to loss? In short, how do we conceive the interior moral landscape of soldiering? By listening to soldiers own narratives and exploring a wide range of readings in the moral philosophy and psychology of war, we shall try to understand the untold internal war that soldiers often fight.

title: Introduction to Logic
description: Instructors: Robert Leider: section 01 Diana Puglisi: section 02 We gain most of our knowledge from reasoning. Logic is the philosophical discipline that teaches us how we ought to reason. The first part of the course will introduce syllogistic logic. If I told you that all men are mortal and that Socrates is a man, what could you properly infer from the argument and each individual statement? On the flip side, what could you not validly infer? Of course, the problems we work on in class will get more complex, but the basic idea will be the same. After studying syllogistic logic, we will move on to propositional logic. Propositional logic uses symbols to represent propositions, statements that have a truth value. In this section of the course, we will focus primarily on creating truth-tables for statements and arguments, and completing proofs, which will demonstrate how certain premises lead to a given conclusion. We will end the second part of the course by introducing quantification theory. We will end the course with inductive reasoning. Sound inductive arguments have conclusions that follow with probability but not necessity. Most inferences that we make in everyday life fit into this category, relying on such premises as statements from authority figures or the strength of an analogy. During this part of the course, we will examine several common inductive argument forms and the properties of good (and fallacious) inductive reasoning.

title: Kant's Aesthetics
description: This course will provide its students with an interdisciplinary approach to aesthetics. Having studied Kantâs theory of beauty and art as a model of mind and philosophical aesthetics, we will examine both the neurological architecture that supports this model and the evolutionary story behind that architecture. In the first part of the course, I will lecture on the Kantian text and the grade for this section will be determined by a mid-term examination. How we will proceed in the second section will be decided by the number of students who sign up for the course. The grade will be based on either an 8-10 page paper or a book report to the class. (The studentsâ choice.) The class will be given a bibliography of most of the latest books from the top academic publishers on neuroscience and evolution. Required texts: The Critique of Pure Reason and the Critique of Judgment will be available in the book store. You will have to order the following two books from Amazon: If not God, Then What? Neuroscience, Aesthetics, and the Origin of the Transcendent. Joshua Frost. (978-0-6151-6106-8) The Psychology of Art and the Evolution of the Conscious Brain. Robert Solso (o-262-69332-1)

title: Metaphysics
description: Some of our questions about the makeup of the universe are beyond the scope of empirical inquiry. (Or, at least, they are beyond the scope of present empirical inquiry.) This course addresses just these sorts of questions as a survey of contemporary metaphysics. We shall cover three topics: (i) causation, (ii) mereology, and (iii) the mind/body problem. Students will be graded on a paper for each topic, as well as a final paper building on ideas from earlier papers.

title: CBL: Philosophy of Education
description: In this course, we will examine perennial, philosophical questions about the nature, purposes, content, control, and methods of education and schooling. These questions include: â¢ What is it to be an educated person? â¢ What should the social functions of education be? â¢ What should children learn and how should have the authority to make this decision? â¢ Do we have obligations to provide equal education to all children? â¢ How does education impact self-identify and how should we respond to this? â¢ How should we respond to segregation and separatism in education? You will also be introduced to ongoing disagreements about specific policies and practices of schooling in the U.S. today Policies and practices to be examined include: â¢ No Child Left Behind â¢ Ability grouping and tracking â¢ Homeschooling â¢ Deschooling â¢ School funding â¢ Sex education â¢ School vouchers You will study your own and your classmates' beliefs about education and schooling, and subject these beliefs to critical evaluation. The course will integrate community-based learning (CBL) experiences that will immerse you in off-campus settings in which activities relevant to education take place (for instance, schools, classrooms, policy meetings, and community organizations). This component of the course will require that you devote a total of 12-14 hours during the semester participating in these activities. These hours must come from activities approved in advance by the professor. The other course assignments reflect a recognition of the time commitment required to complete the community-based learning work. Much of the course will be conducted through discussion and small- and whole-group activities. The primary assignments for the course will consist of short written responses (in the form of blog entries) to the professor's prompts which will require that you reflect on themes from the course readings and engage in an online interactive discussion with your classmates. You will also guide small group discussions about your community-based experiences, and submit short written analyses of the experiences as well.

title: Plato/Aristotle: Political Philosophy
description: The course will focus on a close reading and active discussion of Plato's REPUBLIC & LAWS, & Aristotle's POLITICS. Course requirements include: (1) a number of short essays; (2) group presentations; (3) regular attendance and active class participation.

title: Philosophy of Language
description: This course will be a survey of contemporary work in the philosophy of language, with an emphasis on theories of meaning. Central questions will be What is a language? What is the relation of language to thought? What is meaning? How does meaning relate to use? Does language function to create social reality. Students will write 3 short papers and a final exam. Class discussion is required.

title: Philosophy of Power
description: In Revolt of the Masses (1930), Jose Ortega Y Gasset entitles the final substantive chapter of his sociological critique of modernity with a question: âWho rules the world?â This will be the question of this course. We will explore the answers given by a selection of political philosophers since Machiavelli. We will investigate princely power, democratic power, communist power, mass power, totalitarian power, manly power, and institutional power. We will ask of these authors who they think does rule the world, and who they think should rule the world. We will do exegesis of the texts with the intent to explore both the structure of each authorâs argument and the substantive merits of his philosophical ideas and ideological assertions. Throughout our readings, we will explore the extent to which âculture mattersâ in shaping political philosophies and ideologies, and at the end of the course we will look at how the discipline of international relations theorizes power.

title: Semantic Externalism
description: Here is a seemingly obvious truth: linguistic entities, like words and sentences, have meanings. What is not obvious is what it means to say linguistic entities have meanings. To many, it seems plausible that the meaning of a term has something to do with the psychology of the person using the term. In a famous paper entitled "The meaning of 'meaning'" Hilary Putnam takes up this question and comes to a startling conclusion: "meanings just ainât in the head." That is, meanings are not determined by the speakerâs psychological state. They are, rather, determined by factors that are external to the speaker. This position has come to be called externalism. The view that meanings are determined by the psychological (or otherwise internal) properties of the speaker is called internalism. In this class, we will be investigating the dispute between internalists and externalists. The course will begin with a historical overview, where we will look at the important developments in the philosophy of language leading up to the introduction of semantic externalism. In the second portion of the course, we will investigate the thesis of semantic externalism in depth. Finally, we will look at some of the consequences of semantic externalism and consider some criticisms of the view. Students will be expected to complete two take-home tests and two papers, as well as contribute to in-class discussions. Some background in logic or philosophy of language will be helpful but is not required.

title: Semantic Externalism
description: Here is a seemingly obvious truth: linguistic entities, like words and sentences, have meanings. What is not obvious is what it means to say linguistic entities have meanings. To many, it seems plausible that the meaning of a term has something to do with the psychology of the person using the term. In a famous paper entitled "The meaning of 'meaning'" Hilary Putnam takes up this question and comes to a startling conclusion: "meanings just ainât in the head." That is, meanings are not determined by the speakerâs psychological state. They are, rather, determined by factors that are external to the speaker. This position has come to be called externalism. The view that meanings are determined by the psychological (or otherwise internal) properties of the speaker is called internalism. In this class, we will be investigating the dispute between internalists and externalists. The course will begin with a historical overview, where we will look at the important developments in the philosophy of language leading up to the introduction of semantic externalism. In the second portion of the course, we will investigate the thesis of semantic externalism in depth. Finally, we will look at some of the consequences of semantic externalism and consider some criticisms of the view. Students will be expected to complete two take-home tests and two papers, as well as contribute to in-class discussions. Some background in logic or philosophy of language will be helpful but is not required.

title: Phenomenology: Being & Other
description: Phenomenology has emerged as one of the most promising and fertile currents in 20th century philosophy. This course considers the work of the early representatives of the phenomenological movement (Husserl, Heidegger, and Levinas) and explores the practical implications of the method nascent in their works. In particular, we will concentrate on two significant poles of twentieth-century phenomenology: Being and the Other, reconstructing the transitions from the problem of consciousness in Husserl to the questions of the being of consciousness, the meaning of Being, and death in Heidegger, and, subsequently, to Levinasâs metaphysical desire for the other that âmodifiesâ the Heideggerian being-toward-death. Our goal will be not only to become familiar with the phenomenological vocabulary, but also to ascertain the rigor and relevance of its findings for the contemporary practice of philosophy.

title: Philosophy of History
description: Course Description: What is human freedom? Is there such a thing anyhow? Are you free when you choose what to do? Or does freedom require that you choose rationally? Or alternatively, does freedom require that your choices reflect your character, who you are? If freedom does require that you express yourself in your choices and/or actions, are there any moral or aesthetic constraints on such self-expression? Or finally, does freedom require that whatever you choose, you choose in a completely unconstrained manner, free of all determining and limiting factors? We will examine these alternative conceptions of the freedom of action and choice by working through four models of freedom present in the Western philosophical tradition: those of St. Thomas Aquinas, David Hume, William James, and Friedrich Nietzsche. In this course, we will place a premium not just on learning these views, but putting them into debate with one another. We will try to sort out our understanding of human freedom and the arguments that can be marshaled for or against these several conceptions of it. You will practice the art of philosophical analysis and argumentation in your papers, in which you will learn how to take a philosophically informed stand on the issues and defend it with argument. You will also practice this art in weekly mandatory discussion sections. Philosophy 20 is part of the Main Campus core curriculum and satisfies the general philosophy half of the philosophy requirement. (After completing Phil. 20 successfully, you may finish off your core requirements in philosophy with any ethics course, i.e., any Philosophy course numbered 050-149. See the Phil. Dept.'s guide to the core curriculum for further information.) Course Requirements: Mandatory (see for more info: //www9.georgetown.edu/faculty/blattnew/2/missedsections.htm) weekly discussion sections and whatever paper assignments are listed for the current semester. Course Prequisites: You may not enroll in Philosophy 20 if you have passed any philosophy course numbered 001-049. Seniors may enroll only with special permission. Students who have completed the Liberal Arts Seminar in the College should not take this course, or any other First Philosophy course. Rather, they should take a Bridge Course next. SFS students who have completed Philosophy 99 are strongly discouraged from taking this course. Course Format: Two weekly "plenary" lectures (for students in any of my sections of Philosophy 20) and one weekly discussion section. The plenary lectures are large, attended by all students in all sections of my Philosophy 20, between 150 and 250 students. Weekly discussion sections are smaller, consisting of somewhere between 10 and 25 students each. Required Texts: * Custom Readings Packet produced by Thomson-Cengage. * Friedrich Nietzsche. On the Genealogy of Morals. Trans. Walter Kaufmann. New York: Vintage. * William Strunk, Jr., and E. B. White. The Elements of Style. Longman.

title: Philosophy of History
description: Course Goals & Description In this course we will examine some of the fundamental questions that arise in philosophical reflection upon human history and the study of it (the discipline of history). We will examine these questions in both classical literature (of the modern era) and in the contemporary context. The general themes we will (likely) cover are these: (1) Grand philosophical narratives of history, such as Enlightenment stories of progress and Hegel's conception of reason in history; (2) The rise of "scientific history" and historical positivism, which objects to grand narratives and insists on detailed, causal explanations of historical events; (3) The historicist rebellion against positivism, and in particular the three dominant forms of historicist understanding: hermeneutics, subjective reconstruction, and narrative; (4) Post-modernist rejections of the very idea of unifying historical narratives. Texts & Readings We will rely on electronic reserves as well as the following four books ordered through the bookstore: SÃ¤ltzer, ed., _German Essays on History: Hegel, Ranke, Spengler, and others_ (Continuum, 1991) Hegel, _Introduction to the Philosophy of History" (Hackett, 1988) Mill, _The Logic of the Moral Sciences_ (Open Court, 1987) Lyotard, _The Post-Modern Condition_ (Univ. of Minnesota Press, 1984) Assignments & Expectations of Students Students will write three short papers (5 pp.) spaced fairly evenly through the semester. Attendance in class is mandatory. What Students Should Know The precise schedule and reading list will be available on the first day of classes and is subject to revision during the course of the semester. Prerequisites: completion of Univ. core requirements in philosophy. Graduate students may not enroll in this course.

title: Text Seminar
description: This course will be an in-depth examination of Charles Taylor's "A Secular Age" (2007). It asks the following question: "In the year 1500 it was nearly impossible not to believe in God; in the year 2000 it is quite possible not to believe in God. How did we get from there to here?" A number of explanations/narratives of secularity have been proposed, and Taylor examines some of the most popular and then proposes his own. We will slowly read the text, chapter by chapter, and examine various reviews that have recently appeared on his book.

title: CBL: Foundations of Education
description: This course is organized as a group investigation into the following question: Is public education necessary? To address the question, we will first need to spend time figuring out what the question could be taken to mean. Our inquiry will also lead us to examine our most basic assumptions about education and learning, such as the necessity of compulsory formal schooling for young people, and the capacities and rights of people under 18. We'll ask whether schools are good for children or and our society, and whether education should be organized and distributed in the way that it is. Since the specific questions to be raised through the class's inquiry will be determined in part by the students themselves as the semester unfolds, the specific content of the course will also be determined in part by the students. While the specific content is currently indeterminate, the course (in any case) will be a cross-disciplinary examination the ideals and practices of American education and schooling: to address the question that the course raises we will need to delve into the philosophical, historical, sociological, and political disciplinary foundations of education. We will read the original writings of individuals who have influenced the practice and theory of schooling and education in order to gain insight into the historical expectations about the role of public schooling in the United States and to see if those ideas have merit. We will look at public policies as artifacts that represent particular views about the purposes of schooling and education, the nature of the good society and life, and the role of the state. Given the ideals of liberal democracy that permeate our culture and our system of schooling, we will focus much of our attention on the nature of these ideals, their implications for education, and the challenges they present. We will also need to be attentive to the fact that schooling has been and continues to be a means of both empowering and denying power, of transmitting privilege across generations, and providing opportunity. Throughout our inquiry we'll need keep in mind the distinction between facts and norms, between questions about what is and those about what ought to be, and between actual practices and ideals. Since our inquiry relates to things that occur in the world and things that we want to see happen in the world rather than just the realm of ideas, in addition to philosophical argument and analysis, our inquiry will need to be attentive to the world around us. For this reason, this course has been designated a community-based learning (cbl) course; students will be required to commit a total of 12-14 hours during the semester participating in off-campus activities related to the course. The local schools and the many community groups focused on education provide a rich environment for our inquiry, and the hours you spend in the community will provide you with direct experiences in that environment in order to enhance your understanding of education in a way that is simply not possible through readings and classroom based experiences alone. The specific community activities to satisfy the cbl requirements will be selected by the professor. The other course assignments reflect a recognition of the time commitment required to complete the community-based learning work. Much of the course will be conducted through discussion. The primary assignments for the course will consist of short written responses (in the form of blog entries) to the professor's prompts throughout the semester; these prompts will require that you reflect on themes from the course readings and engage in an online interactive discussion with your classmates. You will also guide small group discussions about your community-based experiences, and submit short written analyses of the experiences as well.

title: Psychology Before Psychology
description: While the term 'psychology' was coined at the end of the 16th century, and while psychology as an empirical science was a product of the 19th century, texts dealing with psychological contents formed an important part of philosophy from antiquity. In this course we will read and examine texts presenting ancient, medieval and Renaissance theories such as the 'passions of the soul', the theory of the four humors as the basis for the taxonomy of personality types, the internal senses hypothesis, the 'mental words' doctrine, and the division of the intellect into active and passive parts. We will discover some early answers to problems such as: various explanation of dreams, how our senses work, how abstract concepts and ideas are formed, the different parts of the soul and how they relate to our bodily organs, how language and thought are connected, whether animals think, speak or have souls, and the diagnosis and treatments of mental disorders.

title: Ethics
description: TBA

title: History of 20th Century German Philosophy
description: German philosophy in the twentieth century had an immense impact not only on departments of philosophy but far outside of it in a range of other disciplines. This course will give an overview of some of the major currents in German philosophy in this period. The aims of the course are both to situate the development of German philosophy in its twentieth century context and to get an understanding of the inner dynamics of the systems of thought that played a major role in that development. The key themes of the course will be the development of phenomenology by Edmund Husserl and its transformation by his student, Martin Heidegger; Heideggerâs own later development into a mode of philosophy that is markedly different from that of Husserlâs phenomenological approach; neo-Kantians, as represented by Ernst Cassirer; the rise of âWestern Marxismâ in German thought (LukÃ¡cs, Bloch); the post-war rebuilding of German philosophy and the rise of neo-Hegelian thought; and Frankfurt School Critical Theory (Adorno, Habermas, Honneth).

title: Ethics
description: This course is an advanced undergraduate course in philosophical ethics. The first half of the course will be devoted to metaethics, which is concerned with providing an adequate account of the character of moral concepts, properties, and judgments. The second half of the course will be devoted to normative ethics, which is concerned with providing a formulation and defense of informative criteria for the exemplification of moral properties (e.g. for an actionâs being morally right, or an outcomeâs being morally good, or a character traitâs being a moral virtue). There will be a high level of preparation and participation expected in this course. Students will be expected to come to each class prepared for discussion, and each student will lead or take a share in leading discussion. The final grade will be determined by this participation and by two course papers.

title: Ethics

title: History of Ancient/Medieval Philosophy
description: This course surveys some of the major themes of ancient Greek and Medieval Philosophy: Knowledge and Opinion, Being and Becoming, God and the First Causes, Cosmos, Soul and Immortality, Reason and Faith. The continuity between the two periods will be stressed. We will read works of Parmenides, Heraclitus, Plato, Aristotle, Plotinus, Proclus, Augustine, Boethius, Anselm, Pseudo Dionysius the Areopagite, and Aquinas. Medieval Philosophy was born from the creative interaction of biblical faith with Greek philosophy. The issue, the relationship between philosophy and religious faith, is complicated because among western Christians, medieval philosophy mostly existed in the context of various theologies. But, throughout the course, we will consider the implications of Ãtienne Gilson's remark: "A philosophy may draw inspiration from a revelation and be true, and if true, it is because it is good philosophy" (Spirit of Mediaeval Philosophy [New York: Charles Scribner's Sons, 1940], p. 406). Until the thirteenth century, Neoplatonism (which attempted to unify the Platonic and Aristotelian traditions) provided the dominant philosophical framework for speculative theology. The most illustrious of the Neoplatonists, Plotinus (205 270 A.D.), profoundly influenced Augustine (354 430), especially in regard to the mind's approach to divine reality through interior recollection. Augustine himself dominated subsequent western theology, as can be seen in the writings of Anselm of Canterbury (1033 1109), until, in the late twelfth century, the translation and introduction of Aristotle into the Latin west provoked a philosophical revolution in regard to the prevailing Augustinianism. But the âChristian Aristotelianismâ of Thomas Aquinas (1225 1274), which marks a new stage in the development of medieval philosophy, was, in fact, a creative synthesis of the Augustinian Neoplatonic tradition (especially as mediated through the Procline Neoplatonism of Pseudo Dionysius) with Aristotle.

title: History of Modern Philosophy
description: Classic philosophical texts of the 16th through the 18th centuries will be examined in the context of major historical developments in the philosophy of the period. The emphasis in lectures will be on textual analysis and the historical development of modern philosophy. The figures of this period traditionally highlighted in scholarly writings are Hobbes, Descartes, Locke, Leibniz, Spinoza, Berkeley, Hume, and Kant. These philosophers will be examined, but the works of less renowned figures will also be examined.

title: History of Modern Philosophy

title: Environmental Ethics
description: This course will explore the moral and philosophical dimensions of the ecological and environmental sciences. It will consist of four parts. In the first section of the course, we will consider the relationship between human beings and nature. We will examine the human-animal boundary, identify continuities and discontinuities in the causal processes which gave rise to human and non-human traits, and assess the viability of the nature-culture dichotomy. This will put us in a better position to analyze key concepts in environmental ethics, such as 'natural' and 'wild.' Part 2 will be dedicated to the delineation of the moral community. Here we will consider philosophical challenges to the premise that human beings are the only subjects worthy of moral consideration, to the exclusion of non-human animals, plants, and ecosystems. We will critically evaluate moral perspectives ranging from traditional capacity approaches to human and animal rights, to the land ethic, ecofeminism, and deep ecology. We will pay especially close attention to two things: first, the limitations of classical ethical theories in the context of animals and the environment; and second, the relationship between scientific facts and normative judgments. In Part 3, we will explore the conceptual foundations and moral value of biodiversity, and the ethical implications of human-caused extinction. In the final section of the course, we will think about public policy dimensions of the environment, including theories of risk assessment, the limits of cost-benefit analysis, and problems of distributive and intergenerational justice.

title: Mental Illness & Philosophy
description: In the first part of the course, we will address the question of biological or psychological causation of mental illness. We will do that for schizophrenia, hyperactivity, and depression. This will raise the problem of the body/mind distinction and also the question of the distinction between the organic body and the lived body. In the second part, we will study more in depth one of the most profound forms of mental illness: schizophrenia (psychosis). We will read the Memoirs of Judge Scheber. Then we will study Freudâs analysis which is a marvel of phenomenological analysis. We then will move to Lacanâs interpretation of Judge Schreber who stresses the conformity of Freudâs analysis with phenomenology. Lacan will then go further by stressing the crucial function of language and sexual difference in the creation of the human subject. We will see how Lacan, contrary to many other psychoanalytic schools, gives the father figure a crucial function in understanding how human subjectivity emerges. In a third part we will study several of the crucial concepts of Lacan which will help us to understand his theory of the human subject and how therefore talk can be understood as a crucial healing method for the mentally ill. We will study some of the following concepts: imaginary, symbolic and real; aggressivity; alienation; anxiety; Borromean knot; castration complex; discourse; drive; ego; enunciation; father; foreclosure; graph of desire; jouissance; lack; law; letter; metaphor and metonymy; mirror stage; paternal metaphor and name-of-the-father; phallus; psychosis; sexual difference and sexual relationship; sinthome; time; truth. In a fourth part we will study how there are ambiguities in Lacanâs early conceptualization of schizophrenia (psychosis). This will allow us to understand Lacanâs different conceptualization of psychosis later in his life. Here the concepts of Borromean knot and sinthome are crucial. Lacanâs later reinterpretation of psychosis relies a lot on Lacanâs interpretation of James Joyce. This course should allow the students to understand the psycho-social dimension of human beings and of mental illness. In the first part, a practicing psychoanalyst, interested in neuroscience, will give a presentation. Readings: References Evans, Dylan 1996 An Introductory Dictionary of Lacanian Psychoanalysis. London and New York: Routledge. Harari, Roberto 2002 How James Joyce made his Name: A Reading of the Final Lacan. Luke Thurston, trans. New York: Other Press.

title: Euthanasia in Nazi Germany
description: Goal This course will explore how approximately 300,000 people with disabilities were murdered in a program that deceptively claimed to provide its victims with a âgood death.â Overview The content of this course will be divided into four major sections: (1) the plight of German institutionalized patients after World War I and initial discussions of âeuthanasiaâ (1918-1933); (2) Hitlerâs views about people with disabilities; the Nazi eugenic sterilization program; and cost-saving measures introduced at the expense of institutionalized people (1933-1939); (3) the Nazi âeuthanasiaâ programs for âAryanâ children and adults, as well as for other targeted groups (1939-1945); and (4) post-war trials of participants in Nazi âeuthanasiaâ (1945-1950). General Requirements Weekly readings; periodic journals; a field trip; a biographical presentation based on the life of a perpetrator, opponent, or victim of âeuthanasiaâ; and a final term paper. There is no final examination. Textbooks Michael Burleigh, Death and Deliverance: âEuthanasiaâ in Germany 1900-1945 (Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 1994). ISBN 0-521-47769-7. Henry Friedlander, The Origins of Nazi Genocide: From Euthanasia to the Final Solution (Chapel Hill: University of North Carolina Press, 1995). ISBN 0-8078-4675-9. Hugh G. Gallagher, By Trust Betrayed: Patients, Physicians, and the License to Kill in the Third Reich (revised edition: Arlington, VA: Vandamere Press, 1995). ISBN 0-918339-36-7 J. Noakes and G. Pridham, eds., Nazism 1919-1945, Volume 3: Foreign Policy, War and Racial Extermination: A Documentary Reader (revised edition; Exeter: University of Exeter Press, 2001). ISBN 0-85989-602-1.

title: Political Philosophy
description: PHIL 423: Nineteenth Century Social Critics We will explore central works of three giants of 19th century social thought: John Stewart Mill, Karl Marx, and Friedrich Nietzsche. Each of these thinkers may be seen as attempting to work out the proper way to conceive of and help practically realize âmanâs interests as a progressive beingâ. Millâs way of working this out in his social thought generates both a utilitarian moral theory, and a staunch and potentially quite revolutionary liberalism. Marx and Nietzsche each rejected this way of proceeding, albeit in very different ways. We will consider Millâs Utilitarianism, On Liberty and On the Subjection of Women. We will also read some of Marxâs corpus both early and late and conclude with Nietzscheâs On the Genealogy of Morality. We will be considering Marx and Nietzsche as critics of Mill and of the style of thought he represents. We will also consider their work as social criticism in its own right.

title: CBL: Youth Activism & Education
description: PHIL 457/AMST 457 CBL (Advanced Studies in Youth Activism and Education) Class size: 8 Enrollment requires instructor approval. This course is an advanced community based learning seminar for students who have previously completed academic and field-based work focused on the theoretical and practical challenges of mobilizing young people who have been civically disenfranchised through their experiences in settings such the schools they attend. This course will be co-developed and co-taught by the students in collaboration with professor. Students will also be responsible in part for evaluating the progress of other students in the course. The course will integrate readings in the philosophy of education, social theory, political theory, and research/theory about effective practices for working with adolescents of color from low income urban communities and in under performing public school environments. The class will meet as a group on campus once a week for a discussion-based seminar; each student will also spend a minimum of 4 hours per week off campus engaged in field-based work through a collaboration with a partner public school located in Southeast D.C.. Additional activities may be required, depending on the decisions made by the group during semester about what it ought to do. While the specific nature of the field work that each student pursues within the context of our partner school will vary, all students will have opportunities to work closely with high school aged youth, educators, school administrators, parents, and representatives from education-focused community based organizations. This work will focus on building collaborative relationships, structures, and activities related to the school in order to make available more opportunities for the young people from this school and community to engage in meaningful expressions of civic voice. Due to the nature of this course, students who enroll will take on significant responsibilities, and that actions within the context of this course will affect the lives of others, including others who enroll and the professor, the young people we work with, our partners within the school, and members of the community). With enrollment comes an obligation to perform at a very high level. In return, students can expect that their work will lead to considerable academic and personal growth. The course is especially appropriate as a form of preparation for students who plan to work with young people in organizations such as Teach for America, DC Teaching Fellows, or the Peace Corps after graduation. ___________________________________________________________________________________________________________

title: Metaphysics
description: In contemporary philosophy the term âmetaphysicsâ is used to mean the philosophical inquiry into the nature and/or reality of what seem to be the most pervasive features of both reality in general and of ourselves, and into questions of ultimate explanation. This course will take up a selection of such issues as they have been treated in Anglo-American philosophy (with some incursions from the Antipodes) in this and the last century. In particular, our concern will be with whether what seem to be certain pervasive features of reality really are as they seem, and if they are not, what we should take reality really to be like. We shall start with the idea of existence itself and the notion of possibility. We shall then consider: time and its apparent flow and the status of past and future; the apparent commitment we have to there being universal or common features present at more than one place at a given time (as when we say that this piece of paper here has the very same color as that one over there); causation, whereby one item seems to bring about or make another happen, both in the natural world and also in our voluntary actions; and our conception of ourselves as comprised, somehow, of what seen to be two very different sorts of things: a mind and a body. Assessment will be based on presentations to the class and essays.

title: Moral Theory of Aquinas

title: Philosophy of Biology
description: This course will survey conceptual and methodological issues raised in contemporary biology. Topics will include the logical structure of evolutionary theory, hierarchical theories of selection, the distinction (in theory and in practice) between selection and drift, the nature of biological chance, and the relationship between micro and macroevolution (as well as that between biology as a whole and the physical sciences). We will explore such questions as: Are species natural kinds? Are there any laws in biology? Is there progress in evolution? Is the history of life contingent or predictable? Knowledge of basic biology and/or philosophy is required.

title: Leibniz & Locke
description: The two great philosophers, Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz (1646-1716) and John Locke (1632-1704), developed very different philosophies. We classify Leibniz as one of the rationalists (with Descartes and Spinoza) and Locke as one of the British empiricists (with Berkeley and Hume), and draw this distinction on the basis of their different approaches to knowledge and concepts. The rationalists defended a doctrine of innate knowledge and concepts, whereas the empiricists rejected such a view, claiming that all concepts derived from sense experience or introspection, and that all substantive knowledge of reality likewise rests on experience. Lockeâs Essay Concerning Human Understanding is the founding text for the presentation of the empiricist view and attack on the rationalist alternative. Besides his views on knowledge, Leibniz also developed a highly original metaphysical system, according to which reality is comprised of a system of so-called monads, each one reflecting but not interacting with, every other in a harmony pre-established by God. Locke, on the other hand, did not develop a metaphysical system, but his philosophy has important metaphysical implications on a wide range of topics, many quite incompatible with the views Leibniz proposed. Given the vast differences between the thought of these thinkers it might reasonably be asked why they should be treated in a single course. The answer is that Leibniz thought so highly of Lockeâs Essay that he produced his own paragraph-by-paragraph commentary on it, his New Essays on Human Understanding, in which he critically appraised Lockeâs views and defended his own take on the issues Locke considered. So we have before us a debate, albeit one-sided, between two of the greatest thinkers of the 17th century. It will be the concern of this course to read Lockeâs Essay side by side with Leibnizâs commentary. Although the sheer size of their works means we will have to skip over a fair bit of material, we shall consider in some detail a range of issues of fundamental importance, including: the nature and origin of our concepts and knowledge; the nature of the physical world; freedom and voluntary action; identity over time; the nature of language; generality and the nature of kinds (or genera and species); and the nature of the mind. Since Leibnizâs New Essays does not provide a systematic overview of Leibnizâs thought, we shall start by getting a sense of Leibnizâs late philosophy as it is expressed more systematically in such works as the Monadology and Principles of Nature and Grace. The remainder of the course will consist of a study of selected parts of Lockeâs Essay and Leibnizâs commentary thereupon, with occasional forays into Leibnizâs other philosophical works. Assessment will be based on presentations to the class and essays.

title: Existentialism
description: This course will be concerned with the question of human freedom; that question primarily involves asking what "human freedom" means and secondarily "whether" or how human persons are, or can be, free. The investigation of this question will be guided by the philosophical testimony of three major Existentialist philosophers - Nietzsche, Sartre and Heidegger, in conversation with other philosophers who have written in the existentialist spirit: Kierkegaard, Dostoevsky, Camus, de Beauvoir, Weil. Our investigation of the question of freedom will take the form of a personal reflection on both the historical and the uniquely contemporary significance of a series of tensions that characterize human existence in the 21st century: The individuality of experience/the universality of reason; the objectivity of truth/the subjectivity of meaning; the limitations of human finitude/the desire for transcendence; personal fulfillment/social and moral responsibility; autonomy/relationship. This is a course in philosophy, not about philosophy. Our task is to make the question of human freedom our own, guided by whatever we can learn from the existentialist philosophers. This task requires that we reflect on our own experience of the problematic tensions that the existentialists emphasized and that made existentialism the important episode it was in the history of philosophy and culture. The benefit of such a reflection on our parts will not be primarily knowledge about that episode; rather, it will be understanding what the question of human freedom means for us as persons in the time that is uniquely our own. 1.Required texts: L.N. Oaklander - Existentialist Philosophy Nietzsche - The Genealogy of Morals, Hackett S. Weil - Two Moral Essays Krell - Heidegger: Basic Writings Doestoevsky - The Grand Inquisitor, Hackett Barrett â Irrational Man, Anchor Books 2.Assignments: Two reflection papers (5-6 pages); final paper (7-8 pages). 3. Consultation: All students are strongly encouraged to meet with the instructor outside of class hours during the semester to discuss their participation in the course. 4. Attendance and Participation: Class attendance is presumed. The benefit of the course to the individual student will depend largely on intelligent classroom participation. This in turn presumes careful reading of the assigned texts before they are discussed in class.

title: Epistemology Proseminar
description: This is a graduate level introduction to epistemology. The topics we will cover include the analysis of knowledge (âThe Gettier Problemâ), empiricism vs. rationalism, the nature of perception and the justification of perceptual knowledge, the problem of induction, and foundationalist vs. coherentist theories of justification. The final exam serves as the Departmentâs comprehensive exam in epistemology. Students will make at least one oral presentation to the seminar, and one substantial argumentative paper is due at the end of the semester. Students are required to hand in the penultimate draft for comments and criticism at least three weeks in advance.

title: Euthanasia in Nazi Germany
description: Goal This course will explore how approximately 300,000 people with disabilities were murdered in a program that deceptively claimed to provide its victims with a âgood death.â Overview The content of this course will be divided into four major sections: (1) the plight of German institutionalized patients after World War I and initial discussions of âeuthanasiaâ (1918-1933); (2) Hitlerâs views about people with disabilities; the Nazi eugenic sterilization program; and cost-saving measures introduced at the expense of institutionalized people (1933-1939); (3) the Nazi âeuthanasiaâ programs for âAryanâ children and adults, as well as for other targeted groups (1939-1945); and (4) post-war trials of participants in Nazi âeuthanasiaâ (1945-1950). General Requirements Weekly readings; periodic journals; a field trip; a biographical presentation based on the life of a perpetrator, opponent, or victim of âeuthanasiaâ; and a final term paper. There is no final examination. Textbooks Michael Burleigh, Death and Deliverance: âEuthanasiaâ in Germany 1900-1945 (Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 1994). ISBN 0-521-47769-7. Henry Friedlander, The Origins of Nazi Genocide: From Euthanasia to the Final Solution (Chapel Hill: University of North Carolina Press, 1995). ISBN 0-8078-4675-9. Hugh G. Gallagher, By Trust Betrayed: Patients, Physicians, and the License to Kill in the Third Reich (revised edition: Arlington, VA: Vandamere Press, 1995). ISBN 0-918339-36-7 J. Noakes and G. Pridham, eds., Nazism 1919-1945, Volume 3: Foreign Policy, War and Racial Extermination: A Documentary Reader (revised edition; Exeter: University of Exeter Press, 2001). ISBN 0-85989-602-1.

title: Deontic Pluralism
description: TBA

title: Ethics of Death & Dying
description: The ethical problems of the care of the terminally ill have been among the most controversial issues in medical ethics over the past decade: the definition of death, intentional killing or mercy, forgoing life-support, living wills, surrogate decision-making, ethics committees, neonatal death, and demands for futile care. This course will explore these issues combining lecture, discussion, and film. The course will not emphasize the sociological and psychological aspects of death and dying, concentrating instead on philosophical, ethical, and public policy issues. There will be a mid-term during the first hour of one class and a final exam. The final exam will be a take-home final distributed the last day of class and due on the scheduled examination date.

title: Mental Illness & Philosophy
description: In the first part of the course, we will address the question of biological or psychological causation of mental illness. We will do that for schizophrenia, hyperactivity, and depression. This will raise the problem of the body/mind distinction and also the question of the distinction between the organic body and the lived body. In the second part, we will study more in depth one of the most profound forms of mental illness: schizophrenia (psychosis). We will read the Memoirs of Judge Scheber. Then we will study Freudâs analysis which is a marvel of phenomenological analysis. We then will move to Lacanâs interpretation of Judge Schreber who stresses the conformity of Freudâs analysis with phenomenology. Lacan will then go further by stressing the crucial function of language and sexual difference in the creation of the human subject. We will see how Lacan, contrary to many other psychoanalytic schools, gives the father figure a crucial function in understanding how human subjectivity emerges. In a third part we will study several of the crucial concepts of Lacan which will help us to understand his theory of the human subject and how therefore talk can be understood as a crucial healing method for the mentally ill. We will study some of the following concepts: imaginary, symbolic and real; aggressivity; alienation; anxiety; Borromean knot; castration complex; discourse; drive; ego; enunciation; father; foreclosure; graph of desire; jouissance; lack; law; letter; metaphor and metonymy; mirror stage; paternal metaphor and name-of-the-father; phallus; psychosis; sexual difference and sexual relationship; sinthome; time; truth. In a fourth part we will study how there are ambiguities in Lacanâs early conceptualization of schizophrenia (psychosis). This will allow us to understand Lacanâs different conceptualization of psychosis later in his life. Here the concepts of Borromean knot and sinthome are crucial. Lacanâs later reinterpretation of psychosis relies a lot on Lacanâs interpretation of James Joyce. This course should allow the students to understand the psycho-social dimension of human beings and of mental illness. In the first part, a practicing psychoanalyst, interested in neuroscience, will give a presentation. Readings: References Evans, Dylan 1996 An Introductory Dictionary of Lacanian Psychoanalysis. London and New York: Routledge. Harari, Roberto 2002 How James Joyce made his Name: A Reading of the Final Lacan. Luke Thurston, trans. New York: Other Press.

title: Critical Theory
description: This is a graduate-level introduction to some of the key ideas of Critical Theory as it has been developed by the Frankfurt School and those who follow in its wake. Authors include Adorno, Habermas, Honneth, and Geuss. web page://web.me.com/titpaulSite About_Me.html

title: Moral Epistemology
description: This graduate seminar will explore a variety of issues in moral epistemology, including the role of intuition in moral argument; the role of discernment, judgment, and interpretation in moral knowledge; debates over the role and limits of moral principles; as well as debates over whether morality is best defended by assimilating it to science or by celebrating its difference to science.

title: Philosophy of Action
description: The aim of this course is to introduce students to the basic philosophical questions about agency. Some of these questions include: What is it for one to be an agent? Is being an agent merely a matter of being a locus of causal forces that move one to act? What is it to act intentionally? What is intention? Is intention a special kind of belief? What is it to act for a reason? Are the reasons for which one acts causes of one's action? What kind of knowledge does one have about one's own actions? What is it to act with autonomy? What is it for us to act together? Do we incur obligations to each other when we engage in joint activities? Some of the authors we will be reading include Davidson, Frankfurt, Velleman, Bratman, and Gilbert.

title: First Year Seminar
description: TBA

title: The Way Things Work
description: This is a conceptual science course, meaning that the emphasis will be on understanding, rather than calculating. Many natural phenomena and the technologies of our modern world can be understood through verbal descriptions, rather than calculations. Equations will be presented in the course, but they will be minimal and relatively straight forward. Given the qualitative, rather than quantitative, nature of this course, the focus will be on seeing and understanding the âscienceâ in everyday life. Most science courses start by presenting abstract theory and then treating applications of it. This course takes the opposite approach. We start with a well known phenomenon, e.g., the blue sky or the fact that microwave ovens make things hot, and then address the question: âWhy is this so?â or âHow does this happen?â A broad range of topics will be covered, including bicycles, roller coasters, clocks, rockets, ovens, refrigerators and air conditioners, copy machines, lasers, CD, DVD and Blu-ray players, nuclear weapons and reactors, cell phones, medical imaging, and trains that float. Classroom discussions will be augmented with a number of in-class demonstrations and video clips. This course serves as a core course for completion of the Universityâs mathematics/science curriculum requirement.

title: Hands on Physics
description: In this course, you will be the researcher uncovering the laws of physics. It will be your own efforts, your own work, and your own thinking, that will produce the results. That way, when you have finished an effort on a given topic, you will fully understand what is going on, and why, and you will be THE authority on that topic. Of course, the textbook will be available, and you will consult it, and your Professor will be available to answer questions and provide guidance, but they will play a supporting role rather than the usual primary role. The net result will be that although you may not cover as much ground as you would in a standard course, you will understand the material at a much deeper level than after a typical introductory course. Moreover, you will take away a much more useable idea of how to cover ground, that is, how to learn more physics, than you would take away from a standard course. Examples of observations that will guide you on your discovery of the laws of physics include the daily motion of the Sun and moon, and the fact that we can make a bulb light up with a specific arrangement of the bulb, a battery, and a wire. From such observations, we will construct scientific models that allow us to answer questions such as âWhy do we believe that the Earth revolves around the Sun?â and to make predictions about the behavior of increasingly complex electric circuits. By the end of the course, we will gain an appreciation for what it means to âknowâ something scientifically. No background in physics and very little background in mathematics is assumed. This course will count toward the college mathematics and science requirement.

title: The Science of Sound and Music
description: This course includes an introduction to the scientific principles behind musical instruments, musical pitch, scales, tone color, room and concert hall acoustics, sound reproduction, and hearing. The course will include hands-on activities in addition to lectures and demonstrations, and will also consider relationships between concepts of form and beauty in music and in the mathematical sciences. This course, in conjunction with a core math/science course, may be used to complete the College math/science requirement.

title: Modern Astronomy
description: The aim of this course is to present a primarily descriptive introduction to modern astronomy. No previous background in physics or astronomy is presumed and no mathematics beyond simple algebra and a very little trigonometry is required. Topics covered will be chosen from the appearance of the sky, instruments used by amateur and professional astronomers, formation and structure of our solar system, properties of stars, nature of galaxies, basics of cosmology, the onging search for extrasolar planets, and the possibility of life elsewhere in the universe. Tutorial activities will be incorporated into class periods. Local resources, such as the Georgetown Observatory, the Naval Observatory, and the Smithsonian Institution, will be utilized. Students will carry out a modest hands-on observing project. This course serves as a core course for completion of the math/science requirement in the College.

title: Principles of Physics
description: These two courses constitute a year-long comprehensive introduction to physics, particularly suited to the needs and interests of pre-medical students. Topics covered are Newton's laws, linear and planar motion, work, energy, momentum, gravitation, periodic motion and waves, fluid mechanics, sound, thermodynamics, electric fields, electric potential, dielectrics, magnetic fields, induction, DC circuits, electromagnetic waves and light, interference and diffraction of light, geometric optics, and atomic physics. Familiarity with elementary calculus is assumed. Three lecture hours and two laboratory hours.

title: Principles of Physics
description: These two courses constitute a year-long comprehensive introduction to physics, particularly suited to the needs and interests of pre-medical students. Topics covered are Newton's laws, linear and planar motion, work, energy, momentum, gravitation, periodic motion and waves, fluid mechanics, sound, thermodynamics, electric fields, electric potential, dielectrics, magnetic fields, induction, DC circuits, electromagnetic waves and light, interference and diffraction of light, geometric optics, and atomic physics. Familiarity with elementary calculus is assumed. Three lecture hours and two laboratory hours.

title: Mechanics
description: This is the first semester of a calculus-based introduction to physics, particularly suited to the needs of majors in physics and other sciences. The primary topics covered are: Newtonian mechanics of particles and rigid bodies, with particular attention to planetary orbits and oscillations; introduction to the fundamental classical forces and fields (gravitational and electromagnetic); frames of reference, and elementary relativity. Facility with calculus is assumed. Three lecture hours, one tutorial hour, and two laboratory hours.

title: Vibrations, Fluids, and Waves
description: This is the second semester of a calculus-based introduction to physics, particularly suited to the needs of majors in physics and other sciences. Topics to be covered include: thermodynamics; fluid mechanics; linear and nonlinear oscillations; use of complex numbers for describing oscillations; systems of coupled oscillators; normal modes of vibration; Doppler effects; interference and diffraction; evidence for the wave nature of light. Prerequisite: PHYS-105 or permission of the instructor. Three lecture hours, one tutorial hour, and two laboratory hours.

title: Mathematical & Computational Methods
description: The goals for this course are to help the student learn enough about the mathematical and computational methods used in physics to serve as a foundation for advanced study in physics (and other fields), develop problem-solving skills, and apply those problem-solving skills to simple but realistic situations. It is intended that the depth of coverage of each topic be sufficient to allow the use of the basics of that topic in subsequent study of physics and the acquisition of a more thorough and deeper understanding of that topic should need and/or desire so dictate. In other words, rather than providing exhaustive coverage, this course is intended to get the student up and running. Topics covered will be chosen from ordinary and partial differential equations, complex variables, linear algebra, vector algebra and calculus, partial differentiation, multiple integrals, Fourier series, integral transforms, calculus of variations, and probability. Mathematica will be taught and used as an integral part of the course.

title: Shaping National Science Policy
description: This class introduces students to science policy while emphasizing how to bring about change. Students will work on one project throughout the semester. They will break into teams, identify a politically hot science-issue, develop a lobbying strategy, and take their issue to Capitol Hill. The class is three credits and will meet once a week. The course is intended for science majors, but is open to non-science majors who have a working knowledge of government or marketing. Cross-listed as BIOL-362.

title: Science & Society: Grand Challenges
description: This seminar introduces students to some of the most significant and complex science and public interest challenges of our time including: managing a global pandemic, reducing carbon emissions, meeting growing global energy demand, and containing weapons of mass destruction. Guest speakers - including scientists, venture capitalists, congressional staff, and journalists - will examine the science, politics and public perceptions surrounding these challenges. Throughout the semester, students will break into groups and develop a report on one "Grand Challenge" that covers: the science, pending legislation, any Administration initiatives, relevant activity in the private sector, and the various policy options and barriers for solving the problem. The class, which meets once a week, is open to sophomores through seniors and is three credits. Biology students may count one credit towards a degree. Physics students count the class as a restricted elective towards a degree. This course does not satisfy the General Education Requirements for science.

title: SCI PEDAGOGY:PRIN & PRAC
description: This course is designed to give a theoretical and practical introduction to teaching Biology.It is limited to Biology majors who have already been approved for BIOL-331 and BIOL-332 Senior Thesis: Teaching Biology and is to be taken in the spring of Junior year. All three credits from this course will count toward the Biology major. Students must be concurrently enrolled in BIOL-300 Tutorial: Thesis Research for one credit. Permission of the instructor is required.

title: Contemporary Physics Seminar
description: This course is a 2-credit seminar (counts as a half-course College general elective) and is only offered Pass/Fail. It meets once a week for 2 hours. The course will consist of readings at the Scientific American-level on a wide variety of physics topics ranging over such areas as string theory, dark matter, superfluids, chaos, econophysics, etc. The goals are to develop a "conversational" (and accurate) knowledge of the physics behind each topic and to see a broader spectrum of physics topics than are covered in the rest of the courses in the department. Students will be expected to read the material before class and to participate in discussions. A small group of students will be chosen to lead the discussion for each week.

title: Relativity and Quantum Physics
description: Relativity and quantum mechanics are the cornerstones of modern physics and deal with some of the most bizarre and paradoxical behavior seen in the physical world. In this course, we will show how to systematically build a theory based on the fact that the speed of light is a constant in any inertial reference frame, which leads to time dilation, length contraction, and a number of surprising paradoxes. We will then note how Einstein's principle of equivalence leads to a reformulation of our understanding of gravity, namely general relativity. Next we will introduce the quantum-mechanical world and see how wave-particle duality leads to a probabilistic interpretation of physical reality. We will employ the two-slit experiment to understand the bizarre predictions of quantum mechanics. We will use Schroedinger's equation to model simple quantum systems, culminating in a description of the hydrogen atom. The emphasis of the course will be on conceptual ideas and theory building, with support through quantitative problem solving. Weekly tutorials and laboratories supplement the lectures. Prerequisite: PHYS-042 or -108, or permission of the instructor. Three lecture hours, one tutorial hour, and two laboratory hours.

title: Electricity and Magnetism
description: In the introductory part of this course, vector functions and electrostatics are covered, followed by reacquaintance of the students with the concepts of surface integrals (divergence), line integrals (curl), and gradients. The manifestation of the magnetic field is treated from a relativistic point of view, including transformations of electric fields, magnetic fields, and forces into inertial frames of reference. This background leads to the development of Maxwell's equations and the ideas of electromagnetic wave theory. Three lecture hours, one tutorial hour, and two laboratory hours per week. Prerequisites: MATH-137 and either PHYS-105 or PHYS-041, or permission of the instructor.

title: Methods of Experimental Physics
description: This is a laboratory-intensive introduction to modern measurement techniques, particularly electronic circuits, digital electronics, optics, and opto-electronics. The emphasis is on the art of experimental design, the evaluation of sources of noise and uncertainties, and scientific writing skills. Prerequisite: PHYS-214 or permission of the instructor. One lecture hour and four laboratory hours.

title: The Structure of Matter
description: The subject of this course is applications of quantum mechanics to the material world, specifically the structure of atoms and molecules, statistical physics and quantum statistics, the electronic properties of solids, and nuclear and elementary particle physics. Prerequisite: PHYS-211 or permission of the instructor.

title: Classical Mechanics
description: The focus of this course is on the review of Newtonian mechanics, development of the Lagrange formulation of classical mechanics, and applications to one- and two-dimensional motion, central forces, collisions, and oscillations. Accelerated coordinate systems, geometrical phases, and rigid-body rotations will also be covered. Throughout the course, there will be emphasis on developing problem-solving techniques and applications to the real world.

title: Electrodynamics and Optics
description: This course will concentrate on further development of topics in electrodynamics begun in Physics 214, and applications to reflection and refraction, interference, geometrical optics, dispersion, coherence, Fraunhofer and Fresnel diffraction, lasers, and holography.

title: Solid State Technology and Microelectronics
description: The topics dealt with in this course are: semiconductor energy bands and carrier concentrations; carrier transport; p-n junctions; unipolar devices; photonic devices; crystal growth, oxidation, thin film deposition, diffusion, ion implantation, lithography, and etching. Two lecture hours and two laboratory hours.

title: Quantum Mechanics
description: This course is an intermediate level treatment of quantum mechanics, covering the origins of quantum mechanics, wave mechanics of a free particle, particles in potentials, axiomatic formulation of quantum mechanics, time evolution of quantum systems, particles in three dimensions, angular momentum, central potentials, spin, and perturbation theory. Prerequisites: PHYS-150 and -211, or permission of the instructor.

title: Thermal and Statistical Physics
description: The first half of this course deals with classical thermodynamics; the second half deals with its historical derivative, statistical mechanics. Topics to be covered include exact and inexact differentials, the Zeroth, First, Second, and Third Laws of Thermodynamics; energy transport via work, heat, and mass flow; closed and open systems; various power and refrigeration cycles; the Carnot cycle and entropy; properties of pure substances; phase transformations; equations of state; reversible and irreversible processes; exergy and entropy generation minimization; thermodynamic potential functions; Lagrange multipliers; Legendre transformations; the kinetic theory of gases; classical and quantum statistical thermodynamics; Bose-Einstein, Maxwell-Boltzmann, and Fermi-Dirac statistics; black body radiation; heat capacities of gases and solids; Einstein and Debye temperatures.

title: Solid State Physics/Nano Science
description: This course is an introduction to fundamental phenomena in solid state and condensed matter physics. Topics will include crystal lattices, reciprocal lattices, phonons, thermal properties of solids, electronic transport, energy bands, semiconductors, and superconductors. There will be a focus on materials that are nanometer size in one or more dimensions and the exploration of properties that are quantum confined in two, one, or zero dimensions.

title: Special Topics
description: This course may be offered depending on student interest and faculty availability. It will cover advanced topics that are not part of the regular curriculum, chosen from areas such as nuclear physics, elementary particle physics, nonlinear dynamics, general relativity, and astrophysics.

title: Independent Research
description: The focus of this course is independent research carried out under the close supervision of a member of the faculty. A substantial written final report and a brief oral report on the term's work will be required. A list of advisers and possible research projects, together with departmental guidelines for work in this course, is available on the Physics Department website. This course may be taken more than once, with approval of the faculty mentor and the department.

title: Sensors and Sensing
description: The basics of sensors and instrumentation will be covered, including sensor properties, precision and accuracy, and control and interfacing . The physical principles of electrical, mechanical, optical, magnetic, and chemical sensing will be reviewed. Applications to the measurement of position, velocity, acceleration, force, strain, pressure, flow, electrical and thermal transport, electromagnetic radiation, and temperature will be discussed.

title: Advanced Characterization Methods
description: Techniques of characterization of materials will be demonstrated and analyzed. Tools for structural characterization, including X-ray, SEM, and TEM, surface analysis, including Auger, LEED, SIMS, and scanning probe microscopies, and chemical analysis, such as mass spectroscopy and optical spectroscopy, will be described.

title: Computational Techniques
description: Techniques for solving practical problems with numerical computation, symbolic computation, and classical mathematical methods will be presented. Topics covered will include Kramers-Kronig analysis, root finding, numerical quadrature, Fast-Fourier transforms, approximation of functions, integration by residues, steepest descents, stationary phase, and matrix operations.

title: Numerical Simulation Techniques
description: Topics will include classical Monte Carlo methods, multidimensional quadrature, Metropolis algorithms, and molecular dynamics. Visualization techniques and parallel computing will also be explored.

title: Quantum Mechanics
description: Beginning with the Dirac formalism, the basics of eigenvalue problems and variational principles will be developed, all in one-dimension. The semiclassical formalism of coherent states and the WKB approximation will also be discussed. These methods will be applied to the simplest lattice model for describing solid state materials properties, the Kronig-Penney model.

title: Hydrogenic Atoms and Defects in Semiconductors
description: The extension of quantum mechanics to three dimensions will be covered. The ideas of spin and angular momentum will be introduced along with non-degenerate perturbation theory. Applications to atomic systems and semiconductor dopants will be covered.

title: Statistical Mechanics
description: Basic ideas of statistical mechanics as applied to solid state systems will be considered, including the connection between thermodynamics and statistical mechanics, ensembles of harmonic oscillators, ideal Fermi and Bose gases, the one dimensional Ising model, Landau's theory of phase transitions, and magnetism.

title: Electromagnetic Radiation
description: Various aspects of the propagation of electromagnetic waves will be discussed, including transmission lines and waveguides, antennas, scattering, skin effects and London penetration.

title: Semiconductors & Insulators
description: Why do crystals form? Why are some crystal structures favored over others? What are semiconductors? Can we build a laser out of semiconductor materials? In this module, we will cover three main topics: crystal bonding and cohesion of solids, optical properties of solids, and semiconductor physics. We will build on material you encountered in PHYS-510 on the statics and dynamics of crystal lattices, along with material you will be learning concurrently in PHYS-515 on electronic states in solids. We will cover most of Capters 19, 20, 27, 28, and 29 in the text. We will also draw from material in some of the supplementary texts.

title: Digital Processing and Control
description: A combination of lecture and laboratory work will focus on analog-to-digital and digital-to-analog conversion, combinational and sequential logic networks, counters and shift registers, and interfacing to computer hardware.

title: Metals
description: The physics of metals will be covered including Drude transport, Fermi liquid theory, the Thomas-Fermi method, Hartree-Fock theory, and density functional theory. The effects of disorder and localization will be discussed, as well as the Cooper problem and how it relates to superconductivity.

title: Electronic Correlations
description: This module will cover advanced topics including degenerate perturbation theory, time-dependent phenomena, and second quantization (which will be applied to the Hubbard model).

title: Materials Physics
description: Mechanical properties of materials including elasticity, dislocations, and fracture along with contemporary developments in materials such as biomaterials, complex fluids and polymers, emulsions, and membranes.

title: Physics Laboratory Rotation
description: Students engage in three five-week-long lab rotations in groups of three to five students. Two rotations are in experimental labs and one in a theoretical lab (or vice versa for theory students). Students interested in finance will be able to take one laboratory rotation in the finance group of the Business school. The students work together to solve sophisticated problems on a deadline. The groups are required to present both oral and written reports on their work. Joint M.S.-M.B.A. students take only two rotations.
